> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout #2**
> Two common errors cost students up to **3 marks** in this type of question:
> 1. **Forgetting to apply the activation function** — students often write $Y = WX$ directly. The activation $f(\cdot)$ is mandatory; without it, the network is linear and has no fixed-point structure.
> 2. **Sign error in $W$** — the matrix layout in BAM is $(W)_{ji}$ for the synapse $x_i \to y_j$. If the question provides $W$ with rows indexed by $y$ and columns by $x$, the multiplication $WX$ is correct only if $W$ is treated as the **input-to-output** weight (i.e., $W$ acting on $X$). Always re-derive this from the dimensions: $W \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$, $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$, $Y \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Bidirectional Associative Memories**

- ✅ **BAM = Kosko 1988**, two-layer, heteroassociative, recurrent.
- ✅ **Storage rule:** $W = \sum_{k=1}^{p} Y_k X_k^T$ — order matters ($Y$ first, $X$ second).
- ✅ **Forward recall:** $Y = \text{sgn}(WX)$; **Backward recall:** $X = \text{sgn}(W^T Y)$.
- ✅ **Iterate** until $(X^*, Y^*)$ does not change in a full forward–backward sweep.
- ✅ **Energy function:** $E = -X^T W Y$ — strict Lyapunov function.
- ✅ **Stability proof:** Asynchronous updates cause $\Delta E = -u_j \Delta y_j \leq 0$.
- ✅ **Convergence bound:** at most $O(nm)$ state changes.
- ✅ **Continuous BAM:** uses $f(u) = \tanh(\lambda u)$ — reduces to discrete BAM as $\lambda \to \infty$.
- ✅ **Special case:** $Y_k = X_k$, $m = n$ → BAM collapses to Hopfield autoassociator.
- ✅ **Capacity:** roughly $p_{\max} \approx \min(m, n) / (2 \ln \min(m, n))$, smaller than Hopfield's $0.14N$.
- ✅ **Symmetric weights** ($W_{ji}$ from $X \to Y$, $W_{ij}$ from $Y \to X$) are essential for energy-based stability.
- ✅ **Bipolar encoding** ($\pm 1$) is required for the signum-based recall; binary $\{0, 1\}$ inputs must be converted to bipolar first.
- ✅ **Common KTU pitfall:** $W$ dimension must be $m \times n$, not $n \times n$ — verify against the layer sizes.
- ✅ **Real-world uses:** pattern recognition, OCR, medical diagnosis mapping, adaptive control, cognitive memory models.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radial Basis Function Neural Network
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT411 - SOFT COMPUTING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Radial Basis Function Neural Network

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Radial Basis Function Neural Network (RBFNN)** is a specialized class of **artificial neural networks** that employs **radially symmetric activation functions** in its hidden layer to perform universal function approximation, classification, and time-series prediction. Formally, an RBFNN is a **three-layer feedforward network** consisting of an input layer, a single hidden layer with **radial basis activation functions** (typically Gaussian), and a linear output layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology):**
> *"A Radial Basis Function Neural Network is a supervised learning model that constructs an arbitrary function as a linear combination of radially symmetric kernels, each centered at a distinct point in the input feature space, with the network's output being computed as a weighted sum of kernel responses."*

Mathematically, the RBFNN realizes a mapping:

$$f(\mathbf{x}) : \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^m$$

where the response depends only on the **Euclidean distance** $\vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \vert$ between the input vector $\mathbf{x}$ and a prototype center $\mathbf{c}_i$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Lighthouse Analogy" — Understanding RBFNN Intuitively**
>
> Imagine a coastline dotted with several **lighthouses**, each one positioned at a specific coordinate. Each lighthouse has a **beam of light** that is brightest at its own base and gradually **fades as you move away** from it. Now, suppose a ship is sailing somewhere along the coast. Each lighthouse illuminates the ship with a brightness that depends *only on how far* the ship is from that lighthouse — not on the absolute direction.
>
> - The **ship's position** = the **input vector** $\mathbf{x}$
> - Each **lighthouse** = a **radial basis neuron (hidden unit)** with center $\mathbf{c}_i$
> - The **brightness from a lighthouse** = the **activation value** $\phi(\vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \vert)$
> - The **final reported position estimate** = a **linear combination (weighted sum)** of all brightness values at an **output receiver**
>
> The output of the RBFNN is essentially a "consensus reading" from all the lighthouses, where closer lighthouses contribute more strongly. This is precisely the **local-to-global approximation principle** that makes RBFNNs powerful for function approximation and pattern recognition.

## 1.3 Key Characteristics of RBFNN (KTU High-Yield Bullets)

- **Universal Approximator:** A three-layer RBFNN with a sufficiently large hidden layer can approximate any continuous function on a compact domain to arbitrary precision.
- **Local Generalization:** Each hidden neuron responds to a *localized region* of the input space, which gives RBFNNs excellent **interpolation** properties.
- **Two-Stage Training:** The hidden layer (centers and widths) is typically trained using **unsupervised** methods (e.g., k-means), while the output layer is trained using **supervised** linear regression.
- **Faster Training than MLP:** Because the output layer is linear, training is reduced to solving a **linear least-squares problem**, avoiding the vanishing-gradient issue of back-propagation in MLPs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of an RBFNN — Structured Breakdown

The RBFNN is composed of **three distinct layers**, each with a specific role:

### Layer 1 — Input Layer
- Receives the input vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)^T \in \mathbb{R}^n$.
- Acts as a **pass-through distribution node**; no computation or weighting is performed.
- Dimension: **$n$ neurons** (equal to the number of input features).

### Layer 2 — Hidden Layer (Radial Basis Layer)
- Contains $H$ hidden neurons, each computing a **radial basis function** centered at a prototype vector $\mathbf{c}_i \in \mathbb{R}^n$.
- The output of the $i$-th hidden neuron is:

$$\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) = \phi\left(\frac{\vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \vert}{\sigma_i}\right)$$

- The most common choice of $\phi$ is the **Gaussian kernel** due to its smoothness, locality, and desirable mathematical properties.
- Each hidden neuron has two parameters: a **center** $\mathbf{c}_i$ and a **width (spread)** $\sigma_i$.

### Layer 3 — Output Layer
- Computes a **linear weighted sum** of the hidden-layer activations:

$$y_k = \sum_{i=1}^{H} w_{ki} \, \phi_i(\mathbf{x}) + b_k, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, m$$

- Uses **linear activation** (purely linear, no nonlinearity).
- The weights $w_{ki}$ are trained using a simple **linear least-squares solver**.

## 2.2 Common Radial Basis Functions (KTU High-Yield)

| **RBF Type** | **Mathematical Formula** | **Key Property** |
|---|---|---|
| **Gaussian** | $\phi(r) = \exp\left(-\dfrac{r^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | Most widely used; smooth, localized, infinitely differentiable |
| **Multiquadric** | $\phi(r) = \sqrt{r^2 + \sigma^2}$ | Global support (does not vanish) |
| **Inverse Multiquadric** | $\phi(r) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{r^2 + \sigma^2}}$ | Localized alternative to Gaussian |
| **Thin Plate Spline** | $\phi(r) = r^2 \ln(r)$ | Common in surface-fitting applications |
| **Cubic** | $\phi(r) = r^3$ | Used in some interpolation contexts |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Focus:** In KTU examinations, the **Gaussian RBF** is by far the most frequently tested variant. Memorize its form, derivative, and the role of $\sigma$ (the standard deviation controlling the "width" of influence).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| **Symbol / Term** | **Formula / Definition** | **Meaning / Units** |
|---|---|---|
| Input vector | $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)^T$ | $n$-dimensional feature input |
| $i$-th center | $\mathbf{c}_i \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | Prototype location in input space |
| Euclidean distance | $r_i = \vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \vert = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{n}(x_j - c_{ij})^2}$ | Distance between input and center |
| Gaussian RBF | $\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) = \exp\left(-\dfrac{\vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \vert^2}{2\sigma_i^2}\right)$ | Hidden layer activation (dimensionless, in $[0,1]$) |
| Width / spread | $\sigma_i$ | Controls the radius of influence (same units as $\vert \cdot \vert$) |
| Output (linear) | $y_k = \sum_{i=1}^{H} w_{ki}\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) + b_k$ | Network output (unit depends on task) |
| Network matrix form | $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{W}\boldsymbol{\phi} + \mathbf{b}$ | Vectorized form of network output |
| Linear weight update | $\mathbf{W} = (\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\boldsymbol{\Phi})^{-1}\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\mathbf{Y}$ | **Pseudo-inverse (Moore–Penrose) solution** |
| Cost function (linear LS) | $E = \dfrac{1}{2}\sum_{k=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1}^{m}\left(t_j^{(k)} - y_j^{(k)}\right)^2$ | Sum-squared error over training set |
| K-means objective | $J = \sum_{i=1}^{H}\sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_i}\vert \mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i \vert^2$ | Unsupervised objective for finding centers |
| Width heuristic | $\sigma = \dfrac{d_{\max}}{\sqrt{2H}}$ | Common rule: max distance between centers over $\sqrt{2H}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL NOTATION WARNING:** The Euclidean distance uses the absolute-value / norm symbol $\vert \cdot \vert$. In LaTeX, this is mathematically equivalent to the $L_2$ norm $\Vert \cdot \Vert_2$. In KTU answer scripts, use whichever your faculty expects, but be consistent.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Function Approximation:** RBFNNs are used in **control systems** (modeling inverse dynamics of robotic arms and nonlinear plants).
- **Time-Series Prediction:** Applied in **financial forecasting**, **load prediction in power systems**, and **weather modeling**.
- **Classification:** Used in **medical diagnosis** (e.g., cancer detection from biomarker data) and **image processing** (edge detection, segmentation).
- **Signal Processing:** Channel equalization in **digital communications** and **speech recognition**.
- **Geostatistics:** Kriging in **mining and geological surveys** is essentially Gaussian RBF interpolation.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Gaussian Radial Basis Function

The Gaussian RBF is the most important kernel. We derive its form starting from the **multivariate normal distribution**.

### Step 1 — Multivariate Normal Density
The probability density function of an $n$-dimensional multivariate normal random vector with mean $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ and covariance matrix $\sigma^2 \mathbf{I}$ is:

$$f(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{(2\pi\sigma^2)^{n/2}} \exp\left(-\frac{(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Identify the Radial Structure
Observe that the argument of the exponential contains the **squared Euclidean distance** between $\mathbf{x}$ and $\boldsymbol{\mu}$:

$$(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}) = \sum_{j=1}^{n}(x_j - \mu_j)^2 = \vert \mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu} \vert^2$$

### Step 3 — Strip the Normalization Constant
For neural-network purposes, the normalization constant is irrelevant (the output layer weights can absorb any constant scaling). Dropping it yields the **Gaussian RBF**:

$$\phi(\mathbf{x}) = \exp\left(-\frac{\vert \mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu} \vert^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Interpret the Parameters
- The vector $\boldsymbol{\mu} \equiv \mathbf{c}_i$ acts as the **center** of the $i$-th hidden neuron.
- The scalar $\sigma \equiv \sigma_i$ is the **width** that controls how rapidly the response decays with distance.
- At $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{c}_i$, the activation is **maximum** (equals **1**), confirming the local-receptive-field property.
- As $\vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \vert \to \infty$, the activation **decays to 0** exponentially fast.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Output-Layer Weight Solution (Linear Least Squares)

The RBFNN's output layer reduces to a linear regression problem once the hidden layer is fixed.

### Step 1 — Define the Design Matrix
Given $N$ training samples $\{\mathbf{x}^{(k)}, \mathbf{t}^{(k)}\}_{k=1}^{N}$, compute the hidden-layer activations and assemble the **interpolation matrix** $\boldsymbol{\Phi} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times H}$:

$$\boldsymbol{\Phi} = \begin{bmatrix} \phi_1(\mathbf{x}^{(1)}) & \phi_2(\mathbf{x}^{(1)}) & \cdots & \phi_H(\mathbf{x}^{(1)}) \\ \phi_1(\mathbf{x}^{(2)}) & \phi_2(\mathbf{x}^{(2)}) & \cdots & \phi_H(\mathbf{x}^{(2)}) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \phi_1(\mathbf{x}^{(N)}) & \phi_2(\mathbf{x}^{(N)}) & \cdots & \phi_H(\mathbf{x}^{(N)}) \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2 — Express the Output Matrix
The target matrix is $\mathbf{T} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times m}$ and the weight matrix is $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times m}$. The network's output over all training samples is:

$$\mathbf{Y} = \boldsymbol{\Phi}\mathbf{W}$$

(ignoring biases for simplicity; biases are easily added as an extra column of 1's in $\boldsymbol{\Phi}$).

### Step 3 — Minimize the Sum-Squared Error
The cost function is:

$$E = \frac{1}{2}\text{tr}\left[(\mathbf{T} - \boldsymbol{\Phi}\mathbf{W})^T(\mathbf{T} - \boldsymbol{\Phi}\mathbf{W})\right]$$

### Step 4 — Differentiate with Respect to $\mathbf{W}$
Using standard matrix calculus, the gradient is:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial \mathbf{W}} = -\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T(\mathbf{T} - \boldsymbol{\Phi}\mathbf{W})$$

### Step 5 — Set Gradient to Zero and Solve
Setting $\dfrac{\partial E}{\partial \mathbf{W}} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\boldsymbol{\Phi}\mathbf{W} = \boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\mathbf{T}$$

If $\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\boldsymbol{\Phi}$ is invertible (i.e., $\boldsymbol{\Phi}$ has full column rank), we obtain the **closed-form solution**:

$$\mathbf{W} = (\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\boldsymbol{\Phi})^{-1}\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\mathbf{T}$$

This is the **Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse** solution, often written as:

$$\mathbf{W} = \boldsymbol{\Phi}^{\dagger}\mathbf{T}, \quad \text{where } \boldsymbol{\Phi}^{\dagger} = (\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\boldsymbol{\Phi})^{-1}\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Insight:** This closed-form solution is one of the **biggest advantages** of RBFNN over MLP. No iterative gradient descent is needed for the output layer, making training extremely fast.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — RBFNN vs. MLP

| **Criterion** | **RBFNN** | **MLP (Backpropagation)** |
|---|---|---|
| Hidden layer | **One**, with radial (Gaussian) activations | One or more, with sigmoid/ReLU activations |
| Output layer | **Linear** | Nonlinear (typically) |
| Training | **Two-stage** (unsupervised centers + linear LS) | Iterative gradient descent (backprop) |
| Local vs. global units | **Local** receptive fields | **Global** activation |
| Convergence speed | **Fast** (closed-form output layer) | **Slow** (iterative, may diverge) |
| Function approximation | Excellent for **smooth** functions | Excellent for **complex** nonlinear mappings |
| Risk of local minima | **Low** (linear output layer) | **High** (non-convex cost surface) |
| Number of hidden neurons | Often **larger** required | Can be **smaller** |
| Generalization | Strong for **interpolation** | Strong for **extrapolation** |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** Given two training points $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} = 0$ and $\mathbf{x}^{(2)} = 2$ with targets $t^{(1)} = 1$, $t^{(2)} = 0$, design an RBFNN with Gaussian kernels of width $\sigma = 1$ and centers at $c_1 = 0$, $c_2 = 2$. Find the output-layer weights $w_1, w_2$ (no bias).

**Step 1 — Compute Hidden Activations at Training Point 1 ($\mathbf{x}^{(1)} = 0$):**

$$\phi_1(\mathbf{x}^{(1)}) = \exp\left(-\frac{(0-0)^2}{2 \cdot 1^2}\right) = \exp(0) = 1$$

$$\phi_2(\mathbf{x}^{(1)}) = \exp\left(-\frac{(0-2)^2}{2 \cdot 1^2}\right) = \exp(-2) \approx 0.1353$$

**Step 2 — Compute Hidden Activations at Training Point 2 ($\mathbf{x}^{(2)} = 2$):**

$$\phi_1(\mathbf{x}^{(2)}) = \exp\left(-\frac{(2-0)^2}{2 \cdot 1^2}\right) = \exp(-2) \approx 0.1353$$

$$\phi_2(\mathbf{x}^{(2)}) = \exp\left(-\frac{(2-2)^2}{2 \cdot 1^2}\right) = \exp(0) = 1$$

**Step 3 — Build the Interpolation Matrix:**

$$\boldsymbol{\Phi} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.1353 \\ 0.1353 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Pseudo-Inverse:**

$$\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\boldsymbol{\Phi} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.1353 \\ 0.1353 & 1 \end{bmatrix}^T \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.1353 \\ 0.1353 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0183 & 0.2707 \\ 0.2707 & 1.0183 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\det(\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\boldsymbol{\Phi}) = (1.0183)^2 - (0.2707)^2 \approx 1.0370 - 0.0733 = 0.9637$$

$$(\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\boldsymbol{\Phi})^{-1} = \frac{1}{0.9637}\begin{bmatrix} 1.0183 & -0.2707 \\ -0.2707 & 1.0183 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1.0566 & -0.2809 \\ -0.2809 & 1.0566 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Weights:**

$$\mathbf{W} = (\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\boldsymbol{\Phi})^{-1}\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\mathbf{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0566 & -0.2809 \\ -0.2809 & 1.0566 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.1353 \\ 0.1353 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\mathbf{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \cdot 1 + 0.1353 \cdot 0 \\ 0.1353 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0.1353 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{W} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1.0566 - 0.0380 \\ -0.2809 + 0.1430 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0186 \\ -0.1379 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Verify the Output at Training Points:**

$$y^{(1)} = 1.0186 \cdot 1 + (-0.1379) \cdot 0.1353 \approx 1.0186 - 0.0187 = 0.9999 \approx 1 \quad \checkmark$$

$$y^{(2)} = 1.0186 \cdot 0.1353 + (-0.1379) \cdot 1 \approx 0.1378 - 0.1379 \approx -0.0001 \approx 0 \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The exact reproduction of the training data confirms the **interpolation property** of RBFNNs — with $H = N$ hidden neurons, the network can pass through every training point exactly.

The width $\sigma$ is one of the most critical **hyperparameters** in RBFNN design.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Conceptual Definition** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** Define a **Radial Basis Function (RBF)**. List any **two commonly used RBF** types and state one advantage of using the **Gaussian RBF**.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A **Radial Basis Function (RBF)** is a real-valued function whose value depends only on the **distance** between the input vector and a fixed center point, i.e., $\phi(\mathbf{x}) = \phi(\vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c} \vert)$. It is *radially symmetric* about the center.
>
> **Two common RBFs:**
> 1. **Gaussian:** $\phi(r) = \exp(-r^2 / 2\sigma^2)$
> 2. **Multiquadric:** $\phi(r) = \sqrt{r^2 + \sigma^2}$
>
> **Advantage of Gaussian RBF:** It is **localized, smooth, and infinitely differentiable**. Its response is bounded in $[0, 1]$, decays exponentially with distance, and provides stable numerical behavior during training. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for two types, 1 mark for advantage.)*

---

### **Question 2: Architecture & Training** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Draw the **architecture of an RBFNN** and briefly explain the **two-stage training procedure**.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Architecture:** An RBFNN consists of **three layers**:
> 1. **Input layer** (n neurons) – distributes the input vector.
> 2. **Hidden layer** (H neurons) – computes $\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) = \exp(-\vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \vert^2 / 2\sigma_i^2)$.
> 3. **Output layer** (m neurons) – computes the linear combination $y_k = \sum_{i=1}^{H} w_{ki}\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) + b_k$.
>
> **Two-Stage Training:**
> - **Stage 1 (Unsupervised):** Determine the **centers $\mathbf{c}_i$** using k-means clustering, and set the **widths $\sigma_i$** using a heuristic.
> - **Stage 2 (Supervised):** Compute the **output weights $w_{ki}$** using the **Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse** solution $\mathbf{W} = \boldsymbol{\Phi}^{\dagger}\mathbf{T}$, which is a closed-form linear least-squares solver. *(1 mark for diagram, 1 mark for layer description, 1 mark for training stages.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A: Comprehensive Analytical Problem** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With a **neat block diagram**, explain the architecture of a **Radial Basis Function Neural Network (RBFNN)**. Clearly state the role of each layer and the mathematical functions used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **closed-form solution** for the **output-layer weights** of an RBFNN using the **linear least-squares** approach. State one important assumption that must hold for this solution to be valid. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

A **block diagram** of the RBFNN architecture has already been presented in **Section 4.1** of these notes. For written exams, the student should reproduce it.

**Layer-wise Explanation:**

**1. Input Layer:**
The input layer contains $n$ neurons corresponding to the $n$ features of the input vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)^T$. These neurons perform **no computation** — they merely pass the input to the next layer.

**2. Hidden (RBF) Layer:**
The hidden layer contains $H$ neurons, each characterized by a **center** $\mathbf{c}_i \in \mathbb{R}^n$ and a **width** $\sigma_i > 0$. The $i$-th hidden neuron computes:

$$\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) = \exp\left(-\frac{\vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \vert^2}{2\sigma_i^2}\right), \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, H$$

This is the **Gaussian RBF**. It produces a maximum output of **1** when $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{c}_i$ and decays toward **0** as the distance increases. The hidden layer thus performs a **nonlinear transformation** of the input into an $H$-dimensional "feature space."

**3. Output Layer:**
The output layer contains $m$ linear neurons. The $k$-th output is:

$$y_k = \sum_{i=1}^{H} w_{ki}\,\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) + b_k, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, m$$

where $w_{ki}$ are the output weights and $b_k$ is a bias. Because the output is **linear in the weights**, training is reduced to **linear regression**. *(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for layer explanations, 2 for mathematical equations.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Step 1 — Define the Cost Function:**
Given $N$ training pairs $\{(\mathbf{x}^{(k)}, \mathbf{t}^{(k)})\}$, the sum-squared error cost function is:

$$E = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{k=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1}^{m}\left(t_j^{(k)} - y_j^{(k)}\right)^2$$

**Step 2 — Express the Network Output in Matrix Form:**
With $\boldsymbol{\Phi} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times H}$ being the hidden activation matrix and $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times m}$ the weight matrix, the predicted outputs are $\mathbf{Y} = \boldsymbol{\Phi}\mathbf{W}$. The cost becomes:

$$E = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{tr}\left[(\mathbf{T} - \boldsymbol{\Phi}\mathbf{W})^T(\mathbf{T} - \boldsymbol{\Phi}\mathbf{W})\right]$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate and Set to Zero:**
Using the identity $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{W}}\text{tr}[\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{B}\mathbf{W}] = \mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{B}$ and setting $\dfrac{\partial E}{\partial \mathbf{W}} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$-\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T(\mathbf{T} - \boldsymbol{\Phi}\mathbf{W}) = \mathbf{0} \implies \boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\boldsymbol{\Phi}\mathbf{W} = \boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\mathbf{T}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the Weights:**

$$\mathbf{W} = (\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\boldsymbol{\Phi})^{-1}\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\mathbf{T} = \boldsymbol{\Phi}^{\dagger}\mathbf{T}$$

where $\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{\dagger}$ is the **Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse**.

**Important Assumption:** The matrix $\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\boldsymbol{\Phi}$ must be **invertible**, which requires that $\boldsymbol{\Phi}$ has **full column rank** (i.e., the $H$ hidden neurons must produce **linearly independent** activation patterns over the $N$ training samples). If this fails, the **singular value decomposition (SVD)**-based pseudo-inverse is used as a robust alternative. *(7 marks: 2 for cost function, 2 for matrix differentiation, 2 for closed-form solution, 1 for the assumption.)*

---

### **Question B: Alternative Comprehensive Problem** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **mathematical formulation of the Gaussian Radial Basis Function**. Discuss the role of the **center $\mathbf{c}$** and the **width parameter $\sigma$** with suitable justification. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Given the training data shown below, design an RBFNN with **two Gaussian hidden neurons** of width $\sigma = 1$, centers at $c_1 = 0$ and $c_2 = 4$, and **find the output-layer weights** (no bias) using the pseudo-inverse method. **(7 Marks)**

| $k$ | Input $x^{(k)}$ | Target $t^{(k)}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 1 |
| 2 | 4 | 0 |

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

The **Gaussian Radial Basis Function** is mathematically defined as:

$$\phi(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{c}, \sigma) = \exp\left(-\frac{\vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c} \vert^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Origin and Derivation:**
The Gaussian RBF originates from the **multivariate normal distribution**. After removing the normalization constant, the exponential kernel's dependence only on the **squared Euclidean distance** $\vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c} \vert^2$ makes it a true **radial** function — i.e., the response is **isotropic** (same in all directions) around the center.

**Role of the Center $\mathbf{c}$:**
The center $\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ defines the **location in input space** at which the hidden neuron is maximally activated. At $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{c}$, the exponent is zero and the output is exactly **1**. As the input moves away from $\mathbf{c}$ in any direction, the response decreases. The center essentially defines the "**specialty**" of the hidden neuron — the input region it is most sensitive to.

**Role of the Width $\sigma$:**
The width $\sigma$ controls the **rate of decay** of the response with distance:
- **Small $\sigma$** (narrow kernel): Only inputs very close to the center produce a meaningful response. This yields **local generalization** but may suffer from poor coverage of the input space.
- **Large $\sigma$** (broad kernel): The neuron responds to a wide range of inputs, providing **smoother, more global** generalization but possibly underfitting local detail.

Mathematically, $\sigma$ is the **standard deviation** of the underlying Gaussian distribution. At a distance of $\vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c} \vert = \sigma$, the activation value drops to $\exp(-0.5) \approx 0.6065$. At $\vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c} \vert = 3\sigma$, the activation is only about $0.011$ — effectively zero. This defines the **effective receptive field** of the neuron. *(7 marks: 2 for mathematical definition, 2 for role of center, 3 for role and interpretation of width.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Step 1 — Compute Hidden Activations at $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} = 0$:**

$$\phi_1(x^{(1)}) = \exp\left(-\frac{(0-0)^2}{2 \cdot 1^2}\right) = \exp(0) = 1.0000$$

$$\phi_2(x^{(1)}) = \exp\left(-\frac{(0-4)^2}{2 \cdot 1^2}\right) = \exp(-8) \approx 0.000335$$

**Step 2 — Compute Hidden Activations at $\mathbf{x}^{(2)} = 4$:**

$$\phi_1(x^{(2)}) = \exp\left(-\frac{(4-0)^2}{2 \cdot 1^2}\right) = \exp(-8) \approx 0.000335$$

$$\phi_2(x^{(2)}) = \exp\left(-\frac{(4-4)^2}{2 \cdot 1^2}\right) = \exp(0) = 1.0000$$

**Step 3 — Build the Interpolation Matrix:**

$$\boldsymbol{\Phi} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0000 & 0.000335 \\ 0.000335 & 1.0000 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\boldsymbol{\Phi}$:**

$$\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\boldsymbol{\Phi} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0000 & 0.000335 \\ 0.000335 & 1.0000 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1.0000 & 0.000335 \\ 0.000335 & 1.0000 \end{bmatrix}$$

(The matrix is essentially the identity, since the two centers are far apart relative to $\sigma$.)

**Step 5 — Compute $\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\mathbf{T}$ where $\mathbf{T} = [1, 0]^T$:**

$$\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\mathbf{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0000 \cdot 1 + 0.000335 \cdot 0 \\ 0.000335 \cdot 1 + 1.0000 \cdot 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0000 \\ 0.000335 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Inverse of $\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\boldsymbol{\Phi}$:**

$$\det(\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\boldsymbol{\Phi}) = (1.0000)^2 - (0.000335)^2 \approx 0.99999989$$

$$(\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\boldsymbol{\Phi})^{-1} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1.0000 & -0.000335 \\ -0.000335 & 1.0000 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the Weights:**

$$\mathbf{W} = (\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\boldsymbol{\Phi})^{-1}\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\mathbf{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0000 & -0.000335 \\ -0.000335 & 1.0000 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1.0000 \\ 0.000335 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.99999989 \\ -0.000335 + 0.000335 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1.0000 \\ 0.0000 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 8 — Interpretation:**
The weights are approximately $w_1 \approx 1$ and $w_2 \approx 0$. This is a **natural result**: the network assigns almost all the credit to the closer hidden neuron, because the kernels are narrow enough that the neurons are essentially **non-overlapping** in input space.

**Verification:**

$$y^{(1)} = 1.0000 \cdot 1 + 0.0000 \cdot 0.000335 \approx 1.0000 \quad \checkmark$$

$$y^{(2)} = 1.0000 \cdot 0.000335 + 0.0000 \cdot 1.0000 \approx 0.0003 \approx 0 \quad \checkmark$$

*(7 marks: 2 for activation computation, 2 for matrix assembly, 2 for pseudo-inverse solution, 1 for verification.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in the Gaussian exponent.** Students often write $\exp(+r^2/2\sigma^2)$, which is **exponentially growing** and is mathematically incorrect. The Gaussian RBF must always have a **negative** exponent.
> 2. **Confusing $\sigma^2$ with $\sigma$ in the denominator.** The correct form is $\exp(-r^2/2\sigma^2)$, **not** $\exp(-r^2/2\sigma)$. This is a frequent one-mark deduction in KTU exams.
> 3. **Failing to state the invertibility assumption** of $\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\boldsymbol{\Phi}$ when deriving the pseudo-inverse solution. Examiners often allocate 1 mark specifically for this caveat.
> 4. **Using gradient descent for the output layer** when the question asks for RBFNN (which is a defining feature of RBFNN). The whole point is the **closed-form solution**.
> 5. **Choosing an arbitrary $\sigma$** without justification. Always state the heuristic used (e.g., $\sigma = d_{\max} / \sqrt{2H}$).
> 6. **Omitting the diagram** in architecture questions. Even a hand-drawn block diagram earns at least 2 marks; a textual description alone is insufficient.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for RBFNN (KTU 2024):**
>
> - **Definition:** RBFNN is a **3-layer feedforward network** with **radial (Gaussian) hidden units** and a **linear output layer**.
> - **Gaussian RBF formula:** $\phi(\mathbf{x}) = \exp\left(-\dfrac{\vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c} \vert^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ — memorize the **negative sign** and the **$\sigma^2$ in the denominator**.
> - **Locality:** Hidden units are **local specialists** centered at $\mathbf{c}_i$; output units are **global linear combiners**.
> - **Two-Stage Training:**
>   * **Stage 1 (Unsupervised):** K-means → find centers $\mathbf{c}_i$; heuristic → set widths $\sigma_i$.
>   * **Stage 2 (Supervised):** Solve $\mathbf{W} = \boldsymbol{\Phi}^{\dagger}\mathbf{T}$ via the **Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse**.
> - **Width Heuristic:** $\sigma = d_{\max} / \sqrt{2H}$ where $d_{\max}$ is the maximum pairwise distance between centers.
> - **Advantages over MLP:** Faster training (closed-form), no local minima for output layer, strong interpolation.
> - **Disadvantages:** Poor for extrapolation, requires many hidden neurons, curse of dimensionality for high-dimensional inputs.
> - **Universal Approximation:** RBFNN is a universal approximator for any continuous function on a compact domain (with a sufficiently large hidden layer).
> - **Interpolation Property:** With $H = N$ hidden neurons (one per training point), the network can **pass through every training point exactly**.
> - **Common RBF alternatives:** Multiquadric, inverse multiquadric, thin plate spline, cubic — but **Gaussian dominates KTU questions**.
> - **Cost Function:** Sum-squared error $E = \dfrac{1}{2}\sum(t_k - y_k)^2$ is the standard choice.
> - **Key assumption for closed-form solution:** $\boldsymbol{\Phi}$ must have **full column rank** (linearly independent hidden activations).
> - **Architecture summary:** $n$ input → $H$ RBF hidden → $m$ linear output (typically $H \gg n$ for smooth approximation).
> - **Real-world apps:** Control systems, time-series forecasting, medical diagnosis, image segmentation, channel equalization, kriging in geostatistics.
> - **Comparison with MLP:** RBFNN = **local, fast, one hidden layer, linear output**; MLP = **global, slow, multiple hidden layers, nonlinear output**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unsupervised Learning: Hebbian Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT411 - SOFT COMPUTING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Unsupervised Learning: Hebbian Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Unsupervised Learning** is a paradigm of machine learning in which a neural network discovers patterns, regularities, or features in the input data **without the presence of an external teacher or target signal**. The network self-organizes by exploiting the statistical structure of its inputs.

**Hebbian Learning** is the foundational, biologically-plausible *unsupervised* learning rule proposed by the Canadian psychologist **Donald O. Hebb (1949)** in his seminal work *The Organization of Behavior*. It is the simplest and most influential single-cell learning law in computational neuroscience.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hebb's Postulate (1949):**
> *"When an axon of cell A is near enough to excite cell B and repeatedly or persistently takes part in firing it, some growth process or metabolic change takes place in one or both cells such that A's efficiency, as one of the cells firing B, is increased."*

Mathematically, this is captured by the elementary update law:

$$\Delta w_{ij} = \eta \, x_i \, y_j$$

where the change in synaptic weight is proportional to the **correlation** between the pre-synaptic input $x_i$ and the post-synaptic output $y_j$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"Neurons that fire together, wire together."**

Imagine a classroom where two students, **A** (the input) and **B** (the output neuron), repeatedly work on the same problem set. Every time A says something useful, B lights up with an "Aha!" (fires strongly). Over time, the **synaptic connection** between A and B *physically thickens* — their collaboration becomes effortless. A now triggers B more easily. Conversely, if A never participates when B fires, their connection atrophies.

In an artificial neuron:
- **A** $\rightarrow$ input $x_i$ (pre-synaptic activity)
- **B** $\rightarrow$ output $y_j$ (post-synaptic activity)
- The *synapse* between them $\rightarrow$ weight $w_{ij}$

Because **no target label** is required, Hebbian learning is intrinsically **unsupervised** — the network discovers correlations purely from experience.

### 1.3 Why Hebbian Learning Matters in KTU Syllabus

| Aspect | Significance |
|---|---|
| **Computational Paradigm** | First mathematically tractable unsupervised rule |
| **Biological Plausibility** | Anchors ANN theory in neuroscience |
| **Foundation for Modern Rules** | Forms the basis of **Oja's rule, PCA, Sanger's rule, BCM rule** |
| **Hardware Realization** | Naturally suited for VLSI/Neuromorphic implementations (crossbar arrays) |
| **Memory Models** | Underlies Hopfield networks and associative memory |

### 1.4 Distinguishing Features

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Three defining characteristics of pure Hebbian learning:
> 1. **Locality** — Weight update at synapse $w_{ij}$ depends only on $x_i$ and $y_j$, not on global information.
> 2. **Correlation-based** — $\Delta w \propto x \cdot y$ (preserves the *co-activation* principle).
> 3. **Unbounded growth** — Pure Hebb's rule causes weights to grow without limit (this is the *stability problem* that motivates Oja's normalization).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Hebbian Neuron Model

Consider a single linear neuron (McCulloch–Pitts style) receiving $n$ inputs:

$$y = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \, x_i = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}$$

The neuron output is a *linear* combination of inputs (no squashing function in the simplest form; sometimes $y = f(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x})$ is used). The **Hebbian weight update** at discrete time $t$ is:

$$w_i(t+1) = w_i(t) + \Delta w_i(t)$$

where the increment follows the elementary Hebbian rule:

$$\Delta w_i(t) = \eta \, x_i(t) \, y(t)$$

Here:
- $\eta$ : **learning rate** ($0 < \eta \ll 1$)
- $x_i(t)$ : pre-synaptic input at time $t$
- $y(t)$ : post-synaptic output at time $t$

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**Step 1 — Initialization**
Set weights to small random values: $w_i(0) \sim \mathcal{U}(-\epsilon, +\epsilon)$ where $\epsilon \approx 0.01$.

**Step 2 — Presentation of Input Pattern**
Present an input vector $\mathbf{x}(t) = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]^\top$ drawn from the data distribution.

**Step 3 — Compute Activation**
Calculate the neuron's response: $y(t) = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}(t)$.

**Step 4 — Local Weight Update (Hebbian Step)**
Apply the local correlation rule:
$$\Delta w_i(t) = \eta \, x_i(t) \, y(t)$$

**Step 5 — Iterate**
Repeat Steps 2–4 for each input pattern or epoch until convergence (i.e., when $\Vert \Delta \mathbf{w} \Vert < \delta$ for a small threshold $\delta$).

**Step 6 — Termination**
The converged weight vector $\mathbf{w}^*$ aligns with the direction of **maximum variance** in the input data — the first principal component.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| \# | Equation / Rule | Mathematical Form | Purpose / Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Basic Hebbian Rule** | $\Delta w_{ij} = \eta \, x_i \, y_j$ | Fundamental correlation rule |
| 2 | **Vector Form** | $\Delta \mathbf{w} = \eta \, y \, \mathbf{x}$ | Compact weight-vector update |
| 3 | **Output Equation** | $y = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}$ | Linear neuron activation |
| 4 | **Generalized Hebbian (with forgetting)** | $\Delta w_i = \eta \, x_i \, y - \gamma \, y \, w_i$ | Adds passive decay $\gamma y w_i$ |
| 5 | **Oja's Normalized Rule** | $\Delta w_i = \eta \, y \, (x_i - y \, w_i)$ | Bounded weights, PCA-like |
| 6 | **Instar Rule (Grossberg)** | $\Delta w_i = \eta \, y \, (x_i - w_i)$ | Competitive learning; recognizes pattern |
| 7 | **Outstar Rule (Grossberg)** | $\Delta w_i = \eta \, (y - w_i) \, x_i$ | Pattern recall from given input |
| 8 | **Sanger's Rule (Generalized Hebbian PCA)** | $\Delta w_{ij} = \eta \, y_j \, \left( x_i - \sum_{k \le j} w_{kj} \, y_k \right)$ | Extracts first $m$ principal components |
| 9 | **BCM Rule** | $\Delta w_i = \eta \, \phi(y, \theta) \, x_i$ | Bienenstock–Cooper–Munro; uses sliding threshold $\theta$ |
| 10 | **Convergence Condition** | $0 < \eta < 1 \big/ \lambda_{\max}$ | Stability bound for Oja's rule ($\lambda_{\max}$ = largest eigenvalue of input covariance) |
| 11 | **Asymptotic Weight Direction** | $\mathbf{w}^* \propto \mathbf{v}_1$ | First eigenvector of input covariance |
| 12 | **Settling Time (Oja)** | $t_{\text{settle}} \approx \mathcal{O}(\ln(\kappa) / (\eta (\lambda_1 - \lambda_2)))$ | $\kappa$ = condition number, $\lambda_i$ = eigenvalues |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notation Convention for KTU Board Exams:** Always define each variable the *first* time it appears in a solution. Use subscripts consistent with matrix indexing (e.g., $w_{ij}$ for the weight from neuron $i$ to neuron $j$).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Utility of Hebbian Learning |
|---|---|
| **Neuromorphic VLSI** | Crossbar memristor arrays implement $\Delta w = \eta x y$ natively in analog hardware |
| **Image & Speech Processing** | Sanger's rule (a Hebbian extension) performs on-line PCA for feature extraction |
| **Brain–Computer Interfaces (BCI)** | Adaptive decoders use Hebbian variants to track non-stationary EEG signals |
| **Robotics** | Self-organizing maps (Kohonen), derived from Hebbian principles, drive motor control |
| **Associative Memory** | Hopfield networks store memories via Hebbian outer-product encoding |
| **Recommendation Systems** | Hebbian-like co-occurrence updates model user–item correlations |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Hebb's Postulate to the Update Law

We start from Hebb's qualitative postulate and translate it into mathematics.

**Step 1.1** — *Statement of the postulate (qualitative).*
If the pre-synaptic activity $x_i$ and the post-synaptic activity $y$ are *simultaneously active*, the synaptic efficacy $w_i$ should *increase*; if they are uncorrelated or anti-correlated, $w_i$ should remain unchanged or decrease.

**Step 1.2** — *Express this as a linear differential equation.*
The simplest mathematical form that satisfies "$\Delta w_i$ is large when $x_i$ and $y$ are large and co-active" is a product:

$$\frac{dw_i}{dt} = \eta \, x_i \, y$$

**Step 1.3** — *Discretize for implementation.*
Letting $\Delta t = 1$ and the discrete update as $\Delta w_i = w_i(t+1) - w_i(t)$:

$$w_i(t+1) - w_i(t) = \eta \, x_i(t) \, y(t)$$

**Step 1.4** — *Final Hebbian learning rule.*

$$\boxed{\; w_i(t+1) = w_i(t) + \eta \, x_i(t) \, y(t) \;}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The continuous-time form is the *biologically faithful* version (synapses change continuously), while the discrete form is the *computationally implementable* version used in simulation and hardware.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Stability Problem of Pure Hebbian Rule

**Step 2.1** — *Assume a stationary input distribution* with covariance $\mathbf{C} = E[\mathbf{x} \mathbf{x}^\top]$ and consider a linear neuron $y = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}$.

**Step 2.2** — *Take expectation of the update rule.*

$$E[\Delta \mathbf{w}] = \eta \, E[y \mathbf{x}] = \eta \, E[(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}) \mathbf{x}]$$

**Step 2.3** — *Apply the definition of covariance.*

$$E[\mathbf{x} \mathbf{x}^\top] = \mathbf{C} \quad \text{and} \quad E[(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x})\mathbf{x}] = \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w}$$

**Step 2.4** — *Substitute.*

$$E[\Delta \mathbf{w}] = \eta \, \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w}$$

**Step 2.5** — *Convert to a continuous-time ODE.*

$$\frac{d\mathbf{w}}{dt} = \eta \, \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w}$$

**Step 2.6** — *Diagonalize the covariance matrix* as $\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{V} \boldsymbol{\Lambda} \mathbf{V}^\top$, where $\mathbf{V}$ contains orthonormal eigenvectors and $\boldsymbol{\Lambda} = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \dots, \lambda_n)$ with $\lambda_1 \ge \lambda_2 \ge \dots \ge \lambda_n \ge 0$.

**Step 2.7** — *The solution in the eigen-basis is exponential growth.*

$$\mathbf{w}(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} c_k \, e^{\eta \lambda_k t} \, \mathbf{v}_k$$

**Step 2.8** — *Observation of divergence.*
Since $\lambda_1 > 0$ for non-degenerate data, $e^{\eta \lambda_1 t} \to \infty$ as $t \to \infty$. Therefore $\Vert \mathbf{w}(t) \Vert \to \infty$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Pure Hebbian learning produces **unbounded weight growth**. The neuron becomes *saturated* and loses discriminative power. This is the **stability problem** that Oja's normalization solves in the next derivation.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Oja's Normalized Hebbian Rule (PCA from a Single Neuron)

**Step 3.1** — *Add a passive decay term proportional to the neuron's own activity.* This is the key insight of Oja (1982). The rule becomes:

$$\Delta w_i = \eta \, y \, (x_i - y \, w_i)$$

**Step 3.2** — *Expand the right-hand side.*

$$\Delta w_i = \eta \, y \, x_i - \eta \, y^2 \, w_i$$

**Step 3.3** — *Identify the two terms.*
- The first term $\eta \, y \, x_i$ is the **Hebbian (excitatory)** component.
- The second term $-\eta \, y^2 \, w_i$ is the **forgetting (inhibitory)** component. It is *proportional to the current weight*, ensuring the weight does not grow indefinitely.

**Step 3.4** — *Take the expectation.*

$$E[\Delta \mathbf{w}] = \eta \, \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w} - \eta \, E[y^2] \, \mathbf{w}$$

**Step 3.5** — *Compute $E[y^2]$.*
Since $y = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}$, we have $E[y^2] = E[(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x})^2] = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w}$.

**Step 3.6** — *Substitute.*

$$E[\Delta \mathbf{w}] = \eta \, \left( \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w} - (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w}) \, \mathbf{w} \right)$$

**Step 3.7** — *Convert to a continuous-time ODE.*

$$\frac{d\mathbf{w}}{dt} = \eta \left( \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w} - (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w}) \, \mathbf{w} \right)$$

**Step 3.8** — *Equilibrium condition.* At equilibrium, $\frac{d\mathbf{w}}{dt} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$\mathbf{C} \mathbf{w}^* = (\mathbf{w}^{*\top} \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w}^*) \, \mathbf{w}^*$$

**Step 3.9** — *Recognition as an eigenvalue problem.*
This is precisely the **Rayleigh quotient fixed-point equation**. Substituting $\mathbf{C} = \sum_k \lambda_k \mathbf{v}_k \mathbf{v}_k^\top$ shows that $\mathbf{w}^* = \pm \mathbf{v}_1$ (the eigenvector corresponding to the largest eigenvalue $\lambda_1$) is a stable fixed point.

**Step 3.10** — *Demonstrate norm preservation.*
Multiply both sides of the discrete update by $w_i$ and sum:

$$\sum_i w_i \, \Delta w_i = \eta \sum_i w_i y (x_i - y w_i) = \eta \, y^2 - \eta \, y^2 \sum_i w_i^2 = \eta \, y^2 (1 - \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2)$$

**Step 3.11** — *Conclusion on the norm.*
When $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert = 1$, the change in norm is zero — the weight vector lies on the unit sphere. Any deviation is corrected: if $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert < 1$ the norm grows, if $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert > 1$ the norm shrinks.

$$\boxed{\; \Delta w_i = \eta \, y \, (x_i - y \, w_i) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \mathbf{w}^* = \mathbf{v}_1 \;}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Oja's rule replaces the unstable, unbounded Hebbian rule with a **bounded, stable** rule that performs **on-line Principal Component Analysis (PCA)**. The neuron's weight vector becomes the first principal component of the input data.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Convergence Rate of Oja's Rule

**Step 4.1** — *Linearize the ODE about the fixed point* $\mathbf{w}^* = \mathbf{v}_1$. Let $\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{v}_1 + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}$ with $\Vert \boldsymbol{\epsilon} \Vert \ll 1$.

**Step 4.2** — *Project onto the orthogonal complement.* In the eigen-basis, the component along $\mathbf{v}_k$ ($k \ge 2$) evolves as:

$$\frac{d\epsilon_k}{dt} \approx -\eta (\lambda_1 - \lambda_k) \, \epsilon_k$$

**Step 4.3** — *Solution of the linearized equation.*

$$\epsilon_k(t) = \epsilon_k(0) \, e^{-\eta (\lambda_1 - \lambda_k) t}$$

**Step 4.4** — *Settling time (time to reduce $\epsilon_k$ by factor $e^{-1}$).*

$$t_{\text{settle},k} = \frac{1}{\eta \, (\lambda_1 - \lambda_k)}$$

The **dominant time constant** is for the slowest-decaying mode $k = 2$:

$$t_{\text{settle}} = \frac{1}{\eta \, (\lambda_1 - \lambda_2)}$$

**Step 4.5** — *Engineering insight.*
- A **larger gap** $\lambda_1 - \lambda_2$ (well-separated principal components) $\Rightarrow$ **faster convergence**.
- A **larger learning rate** $\eta$ accelerates learning but introduces more noise; trade-off is governed by the KTU-style bound $\eta < 1 / \lambda_{\max}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hebbian Learning — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Pattern Xt] --> B[Weight Vector Wt]
    B --> C[Linear Combiner: y = W transpose X]
    C --> D[Output Neuron Activation Yt]
    D --> E[Hebbian Correlation: eta times Xt times Yt]
    A --> E
    E --> F[Weight Update: Wt+1 = Wt plus Delta W]
    F --> G{Norm Check}
    G -- Oja Rule --> H[Normalize: W = W divided by norm W]
    G -- Pure Hebb --> I[Allow Unbounded Growth]
    H --> J[Converged Weight W star = v1]
    I --> K[Saturation Problem]
    J --> L[First Principal Component Extracted]
    K --> M[Stability Failure]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The architecture shows the closed-loop feedback characteristic of Hebbian systems. The input pattern and the neuron's own output jointly drive the weight update, which in Oja's variant is followed by a *norm-correction* stage that prevents divergence.

### 4.2 Comparison of Hebbian Variants — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hebbian Family] --> B{Pure Hebb}
    A --> C{Oja Rule}
    A --> D{Sanger GHA}
    A --> E{Instar Outstar}
    A --> F{BCM Rule}
    B --> B1[Unstable: weights diverge]
    C --> C1[Stable single-neuron PCA]
    D --> D1[Stable multi-neuron PCA]
    E --> E1[Competitive pattern recognition]
    F --> F1[Sliding threshold plasticity]
    B1 --> G[Engineering problem]
    C1 --> H[Engineering solution]
    D1 --> H
    E1 --> I[Pattern recall solution]
    F1 --> J[Biological realism solution]
```

### 4.3 Hebbian Learning Algorithm — Operational Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User/Environment
    participant N as Neuron
    participant W as Weight Store
    participant C as Convergence Monitor
    U->>N: Present input pattern x(t)
    N->>N: Compute y(t) = w transpose x(t)
    N->>W: Request current weights w(t)
    W-->>N: Return w(t)
    N->>N: Compute Delta w = eta x(t) y(t)
    N->>W: Update: w(t+1) = w(t) + Delta w
    N->>C: Report norm of Delta w
    C->>C: Check if ||Delta w|| less than delta
    alt Not Converged
        C-->>U: Request next input pattern
    else Converged
        C->>W: Freeze weights
        C-->>U: Declare convergence; w star = v1
    end
```

**Reading the diagram:** This sequence diagram captures the iterative, on-line nature of Hebbian learning — weights are updated *after every pattern presentation* without requiring a labeled dataset, and the process self-terminates once the weight vector stabilizes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define Hebbian Learning. State its mathematical form and explain why it is called an unsupervised rule. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Hebbian Learning is a biologically-inspired, unsupervised learning rule proposed by Donald O. Hebb (1949). It states that the synaptic weight between two neurons is strengthened when both the pre-synaptic and post-synaptic neurons are simultaneously active.

**Mathematical Form:**
$$\Delta w_{ij} = \eta \, x_i \, y_j$$

where $w_{ij}$ is the weight from input neuron $i$ to output neuron $j$, $x_i$ is the pre-synaptic input, $y_j$ is the post-synaptic output, and $\eta$ is the learning rate.

**Why Unsupervised:** The rule requires **no external teacher signal or target output**. The weight modification depends *only* on the pre- and post-synaptic activities, both of which are available locally within the network. Hence, the network self-organizes by discovering correlations present in the input data, which is the defining property of unsupervised learning. **(3 marks)**

---

#### Question 2: List any two limitations of the basic Hebbian rule and briefly explain how Oja's rule overcomes them. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Limitation 1 — Unbounded Weight Growth:** In pure Hebbian learning, $\Delta w_{ij} = \eta x_i y_j$ is *always non-negative* (assuming non-negative activations). Therefore weights grow without bound, causing the neuron to saturate and lose discriminative ability.

**Limitation 2 — No Explicit Forgetting Mechanism:** Pure Hebb has no decay term; previously learned information cannot be *replaced* or *forgotten*, preventing the network from adapting to new patterns.

**Oja's Solution:** Oja (1982) introduced a multiplicative forgetting term:
$$\Delta w_i = \eta \, y \, (x_i - y \, w_i)$$

The first term $\eta y x_i$ is the standard Hebbian excitation. The second term $-\eta y^2 w_i$ acts as a passive decay proportional to the current weight magnitude, **automatically bounding the weight vector on the unit sphere**. As a result, weights remain finite and the network performs stable, on-line principal component extraction. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Always** write the equation in vector/matrix form *before* substituting numerical values — a common deduction is **2 marks** for skipping the symbolic formulation.
> - **Convergence proofs** must explicitly state the assumption (e.g., *stationary input distribution*, *small $\eta$*). Vague statements lose **1–2 marks**.
> - When asked to "compare" rules, use a **table** with at least three criteria (stability, biological plausibility, complexity). Bullet-only answers are penalized.
> - For Oja's rule, the **forgetting term $-\eta y^2 w_i$** must appear in the derivation. Many students omit it, losing the **stability argument (3 marks)**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — On Hebbian Learning Theory and Oja's Rule**

`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PERAT411]`

**(a)** State Hebb's postulate. Derive the mathematical form of the basic Hebbian learning rule for a single linear neuron. Discuss the stability limitation of the pure Hebbian rule. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the basic Hebbian rule, derive Oja's normalized learning rule. Show mathematically that under Oja's rule, the weight vector converges to the first principal component of the input data. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Hebb's Postulate (1949):**
"When an axon of cell A is near enough to excite cell B and repeatedly or persistently takes part in firing it, some growth process or metabolic change takes place in one or both cells such that A's efficiency, as one of the cells firing B, is increased." **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Mathematical Formulation:**
For a single linear neuron with weight vector $\mathbf{w} = [w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n]^\top$ and input $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]^\top$, the output is:

$$y = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \, x_i = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}$$

The most direct translation of Hebb's postulate into mathematics is to make the change in weight *proportional to the simultaneous activity* of the pre- and post-synaptic neurons:

$$\Delta w_i = w_i(t+1) - w_i(t) = \eta \, x_i(t) \, y(t)$$

In vector form:

$$\mathbf{w}(t+1) = \mathbf{w}(t) + \eta \, y(t) \, \mathbf{x}(t) \quad \text{...(i)} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 3 — Stability Limitation:**
Taking expectation on both sides of (i) under a stationary input distribution with covariance $\mathbf{C} = E[\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^\top]$:

$$E[\Delta \mathbf{w}] = \eta \, \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w}$$

Diagonalizing $\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{V} \boldsymbol{\Lambda} \mathbf{V}^\top$ with $\lambda_1 \ge \lambda_2 \ge \dots \ge \lambda_n \ge 0$:

$$E[\mathbf{w}(t)] = \sum_{k=1}^{n} c_k \, (1 + \eta \lambda_k)^t \, \mathbf{v}_k$$

Since $1 + \eta \lambda_k > 1$ for $\lambda_k > 0$, the weights grow **exponentially** without bound. The neuron saturates and cannot discriminate between input patterns. **(4 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Add a forgetting term to the pure Hebbian rule:**
Oja's key insight was to subtract a term proportional to the current weight:

$$\Delta w_i = \eta \, y \, x_i - \eta \, y^2 \, w_i = \eta \, y \, (x_i - y \, w_i) \quad \text{...(ii)} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 2 — Take expectation:**

$$E[\Delta \mathbf{w}] = \eta \, \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w} - \eta \, E[y^2] \, \mathbf{w}$$

Using $E[y^2] = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w}$:

$$E[\Delta \mathbf{w}] = \eta \left( \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w} - (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w}) \, \mathbf{w} \right) \quad \text{...(iii)} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 3 — Equilibrium analysis:**
At equilibrium, $E[\Delta \mathbf{w}] = \mathbf{0}$:

$$\mathbf{C} \mathbf{w}^* = (\mathbf{w}^{*\top} \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w}^*) \, \mathbf{w}^*$$

Multiplying both sides on the left by $\mathbf{w}^{*\top}$ and using $\mathbf{w}^{*\top} \mathbf{w}^* = 1$ (which Oja's rule enforces):

$$\mathbf{w}^{*\top} \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w}^* = \lambda$$

This is precisely the **Rayleigh quotient**, maximized when $\mathbf{w}^*$ is the eigenvector $\mathbf{v}_1$ corresponding to the largest eigenvalue $\lambda_1$ of $\mathbf{C}$. Hence:

$$\mathbf{w}^* = \mathbf{v}_1 \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 4 — Stability of the fixed point:**
Linearizing (iii) about $\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{v}_1 + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}$ and projecting onto the orthogonal complement gives, for $k \ge 2$:

$$\frac{d\epsilon_k}{dt} = -\eta (\lambda_1 - \lambda_k) \, \epsilon_k \quad \text{with} \quad \lambda_1 - \lambda_k > 0$$

This shows exponential decay of perturbations, so $\mathbf{v}_1$ is a **stable** fixed point — confirming that Oja's neuron extracts the first principal component in a stable, bounded manner. **(1 mark)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — On Hebbian Variants and Applications**

`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PERAT411]`

**(a)** Explain the Instar and Outstar learning rules of Grossberg. Give one engineering application for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss Sanger's Generalized Hebbian Algorithm (GHA) for extracting multiple principal components. Show how it generalizes the basic Hebbian rule and state the stability condition on the learning rate. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Instar Rule:** An *instar* is a neuron that learns to recognize a specific input pattern by forming a weight vector that becomes a normalized version of that pattern. The rule is:

$$\Delta w_i = \eta \, y \, (x_i - w_i) \quad \text{where} \quad y = \sum_i w_i x_i$$

The first term $\eta y x_i$ is the Hebbian excitation; the second term $-\eta y w_i$ is the decay toward the current weight. At equilibrium (when $y = 1$), the weight equals the input. **Application:** Competitive pattern classification and vector quantization (the foundation of ART networks). **(3.5 marks)**

**Outstar Rule:** An *outstar* is the dual structure — a single input neuron drives multiple output neurons, learning to recall an entire output pattern when the input is active. The rule is:

$$\Delta w_i = \eta \, (y - w_i) \, x$$

The first term $\eta y x$ is Hebbian; the second term $-\eta w_i x$ causes the weight to decay toward zero when the output is inactive, so each output channel learns its component of the recalled pattern. **Application:** Associative memory and pattern completion (used in adaptive pattern recall circuits). **(3.5 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Generalization of Hebbian Rule:** Sanger's GHA (1989) extends the single-neuron Oja rule to a network of $m$ output neurons that simultaneously extract the first $m$ principal components. The weight update for neuron $j$ is:

$$\Delta w_{ij} = \eta \, y_j \left( x_i - \sum_{k=1}^{j} w_{kj} \, y_k \right) \quad \text{...(i)} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step-by-step meaning:**

- For $j = 1$, the sum is just $w_{i1} y_1$, and (i) reduces to **Oja's rule** — the first neuron extracts $\mathbf{v}_1$.
- For $j = 2$, the subtraction includes both $w_{i1} y_1$ and $w_{i2} y_2$. The first term $w_{i1} y_1 = \hat{x}_i^{(1)}$ is the projection of the input onto the first principal component. By *deflating* this component from the input, the second neuron is forced to find the direction of **maximum remaining variance**, i.e., $\mathbf{v}_2$.
- Continuing in this way, the $j$-th neuron extracts $\mathbf{v}_j$, the $j$-th principal component.

**Engineering utility:** Sanger's GHA is widely used in **image compression, face recognition (Eigenfaces), and feature extraction** pipelines where multiple components are required. **(3 marks)**

**Stability condition:** For convergence, the learning rate must satisfy:

$$0 < \eta < \frac{1}{\lambda_{\max}}$$

where $\lambda_{\max}$ is the largest eigenvalue of the input covariance matrix $\mathbf{C}$. This ensures the Oja-like update is contractive on the higher-order components. **(2 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Hebbian Learning**

- **Donald O. Hebb (1949)** is the originator — memorize the postulate verbatim; it is a *favorite* 3-mark question.
- **Core rule:** $\Delta w_{ij} = \eta \, x_i \, y_j$ — memorize in both scalar and vector form.
- **"Neurons that fire together, wire together"** — the popular paraphrase examiners often quote.
- **Three properties:** locality, correlation-based, **unbounded growth** (the *defining flaw*).
- **Stability problem** of pure Hebbian rule: weights diverge exponentially as $e^{\eta \lambda_1 t}$.
- **Oja's rule:** $\Delta w_i = \eta y (x_i - y w_i)$ — preserves norm on the unit sphere.
- **Oja's fixed point:** $\mathbf{w}^* = \mathbf{v}_1$ (first eigenvector of input covariance) — equivalent to **on-line PCA**.
- **Convergence rate** of Oja's rule: $t_{\text{settle}} \approx 1 / (\eta (\lambda_1 - \lambda_2))$ — slower when eigenvalues are close (high condition number).
- **Stability bound:** $\eta < 1 / \lambda_{\max}$ — a routine 2-mark sub-question.
- **Sanger's GHA** extracts multiple principal components via deflation: $\Delta w_{ij} = \eta y_j (x_i - \sum_{k \le j} w_{kj} y_k)$.
- **Instar/Outstar (Grossberg):** Instar recognizes patterns; Outstar recalls patterns.
- **Hebbian learning is fundamentally unsupervised** — no target, no error signal, no teacher.
- **Biological relevance:** synaptic LTP (long-term potentiation) and LTD (depression) experimentally observed in hippocampal neurons (Bliss & Lømo, 1973) are the biological analog of Hebbian weight changes.
- **Engineering applications to mention in answers:** Hopfield associative memory, Sanger's PCA for image compression, Kohonen SOM (competitive Hebbian variant), Eigenfaces, neuromorphic memristor crossbars.
- **Comparison with supervised learning:** Supervised rules (perceptron, delta, back-propagation) need a target; Hebbian rules do not — this is the *single most important distinguishing point* for KTU theory questions.
- **Common student pitfalls:** confusing $\Delta w = \eta(x - y)$ (delta rule) with $\Delta w = \eta x y$ (Hebbian) — they look similar but are fundamentally different in information flow.
- **Examiner's mantra:** Whenever a question says "explain Hebbian learning," *always* finish with the **stability problem and Oja's fix** to score full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generalized Hebbian Learning Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT411 - SOFT COMPUTING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Generalized Hebbian Learning Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generalized Hebbian Learning Algorithm (GHA)**, also widely known as **Sanger's Rule**, is a supervised-free, biologically-inspired, single-layer linear neural network training algorithm used to extract the **first `m` principal components** of an `n`-dimensional input data stream in a self-organized manner. It is the direct, multi-unit generalization of **Oja's Single-Unit Hebbian Rule**.

Formally, for a linear feed-forward network of `m` output neurons receiving an `n`-dimensional input vector $\mathbf{x}$, the synaptic weight matrix $\mathbf{W}$ is updated iteratively so that the rows of $\mathbf{W}$ converge to the first `m` eigenvectors (principal directions) of the input covariance matrix, ordered by decreasing eigenvalue (variance).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Orchestra Listener":**
> Imagine you are sitting in a concert hall with `m` recording microphones, and an orchestra of `n` instruments is playing. Each microphone tries to capture the *most dominant* melody (1st principal component). The clever twist in GHA is that **Microphone 2 is forced to ignore whatever Microphone 1 already picked up**. Microphone 3 ignores what Microphones 1 and 2 captured, and so on.
>
> This forced "subtraction of prior knowledge" is mathematically called the **deflation term**. It guarantees that the microphones end up recording `m` *different, mutually independent* melodies, ranked from loudest to softest.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Symbols

- **η (eta)** — Learning rate (small positive constant, typically $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$).
- **m** — Number of principal components to extract (number of output neurons).
- **n** — Dimensionality of the input vector.
- **w_ij** — Synaptic weight connecting input node `j` to output neuron `i`.
- **C** — Input covariance matrix.
- **λ_i** — Eigenvalue of the `i`-th principal component.

> [!TIP]
> The GHA is a **deterministic** (no random initialization beyond weights) and **online** (sample-by-sample) algorithm — making it ideal for streaming sensor data in real-time engineering applications.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The GHA operates on a single-layer **linear feed-forward neural network** with no biases and no activation function (identity activation $f(x) = x$). The operational logic is layered as follows:

- **Step 1 — Linear Forward Propagation:** Each output neuron `i` computes a scalar response as a linear weighted sum of the inputs.
- **Step 2 — Hebbian Correlation (Strengthening):** The weight change is proportional to the *product* of the pre-synaptic input and post-synaptic output, mimicking Donald Hebb's postulate: *"Neurons that fire together, wire together."*
- **Step 3 — Passive Decay (Normalization / Stability):** A subtractive term proportional to the squared output is introduced to prevent weight explosion — this is **Oja's normalization** extended to multiple units.
- **Step 4 — Deflation (Orthogonalization):** A summation term subtracts out the projections of the current output onto all *previously learned* principal components, forcing the new weight vector to learn the *next orthogonal direction* of maximum variance.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Term

| Term | Purpose | Without it... |
|------|---------|---------------|
| $\eta \, y_i \, x_j$ | Pure Hebbian reinforcement | Weights diverge to infinity |
| $-\eta \, y_i^2 \, w_{ij}$ | Oja's passive decay | Saturation / instability |
| $-\eta \, y_i \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} w_{kj} y_k$ | Sanger's deflation | All neurons learn the *same* 1st PC |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA — Sanger's GHA Weight Update Rule:**

$$
\Delta w_{ij}(t+1) \;=\; \eta \, y_i(t)\!\left[\, x_j(t) \;-\; \sum_{k=1}^{i} w_{kj}(t)\, y_k(t)\,\right]
$$

| # | Formula | Description / Boundary |
|---|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | $y_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_{ij}\, x_j$ | Forward pass (linear activation) |
| 2 | $\Delta w_{ij} = \eta\, y_i\!\left(x_j - \sum_{k=1}^{i} w_{kj} y_k\right)$ | **Sanger's GHA update** (for $i=1,\dots,m$; $j=1,\dots,n$) |
| 3 | $w_{ij}(t+1) = w_{ij}(t) + \Delta w_{ij}(t)$ | Weight refresh rule |
| 4 | $\Delta w_{ij} = \eta\, y_i (x_j - y_i w_{ij})$ | **Oja's rule** (special case when $m=1$) |
| 5 | $\sum_{j} w_{ij}^2 \to 1$ | Asymptotic normalization constraint |
| 6 | $w_{i\cdot}\, w_{k\cdot}^{T} = 0 \;\;(i \neq k)$ | Asymptotic orthogonality constraint |
| 7 | $\mathbf{w}_{i}^{*} \to \mathbf{v}_{i}$ (i-th eigenvector of $\mathbf{C}$) | Convergence to i-th principal direction |
| 8 | $\mathbb{E}[y_i^2] \to \lambda_i$ | Output variance equals the i-th eigenvalue |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** GHA is extensively used in **face recognition (eigenfaces)**, **image compression**, **biomedical signal processing (EEG/ECG feature extraction)**, **fault diagnosis in rotating machinery**, and **hyperspectral image analysis** — wherever high-dimensional data must be projected onto a lower-dimensional manifold while preserving maximum variance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Oja's Single-Unit Rule to Sanger's GHA

We start from the classical **Oja's learning rule** for a *single* output neuron ($m=1$):

$$
\Delta w_{j} \;=\; \eta\, y\, (x_j - y\, w_j)
$$

Oja showed that this single equation yields a stable, normalized weight vector that converges to the **first principal eigenvector** of the input covariance matrix $\mathbf{C} = \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^{T}]$.

To extract the **second** principal component, the network requires a second output neuron. A naive duplication of Oja's rule fails — both neurons converge to the *same* first eigenvector. Sanger's key insight was to **modify the input fed to neuron `i`** by subtracting the portion already explained by neurons $1, 2, \dots, i-1$.

**Step 1 — Define the "residual" input for neuron `i`:**

$$
\tilde{x}_j^{(i)} \;=\; x_j - \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} w_{kj}\, y_k
$$

This term projects the original input `x` onto the orthogonal complement of the span of the first $(i-1)$ learned weight vectors.

**Step 2 — Apply Oja's rule to the residual input:**

$$
\Delta w_{ij} \;=\; \eta\, y_i\, \tilde{x}_j^{(i)}
$$

**Step 3 — Expand the residual input:**

$$
\Delta w_{ij} \;=\; \eta\, y_i\!\left(x_j - \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} w_{kj}\, y_k\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Absorb the self-decay term into the sum (noting that when $k=i$, the term $w_{ij} y_i$ corresponds to Oja's decay):**

$$
\Delta w_{ij} \;=\; \eta\, y_i\!\left(x_j - \sum_{k=1}^{i} w_{kj}\, y_k\right)
$$

This is the **canonical Sanger's GHA update rule**.

## 3.2 Stability Analysis (Discrete-Time Lyapunov Approach)

Consider the expected weight change $\mathbb{E}[\Delta w_{ij}]$ under a stationary input distribution. Setting the learning rate $\eta$ to be infinitesimally small allows us to use the continuous-time approximation:

$$
\frac{d w_{ij}}{d t} \;=\; \eta\!\left(\mathbf{C}\,\mathbf{w}_i\right)_j - \eta\, y_i \sum_{k=1}^{i} w_{kj}\, y_k
$$

Using $y_i = \mathbf{w}_i^{T}\mathbf{x}$, the second term simplifies. Sanger proved (1989) that the only stable equilibria of this differential system are:

- The weight vectors $\mathbf{w}_1^{*}, \mathbf{w}_2^{*}, \dots, \mathbf{w}_m^{*}$ converge to the **first `m` eigenvectors** $\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \dots, \mathbf{v}_m$ of $\mathbf{C}$.
- They are **mutually orthogonal**: $\mathbf{w}_i^{*T} \mathbf{w}_k^{*} = \delta_{ik}$.
- They are **unit-norm**: $\|\mathbf{w}_i^{*}\| = 1$.
- They are **ordered by eigenvalue**: $\lambda_1 > \lambda_2 > \dots > \lambda_m$.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> **Problem:** An input vector $\mathbf{x} = [3,\; 1,\; 2]^T$ is presented to a 2-output GHA network. The current weight matrix is:
> $$
> \mathbf{W} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.8 & 0.0 \\ 0.0 & 0.0 & 1.0 \end{bmatrix}
> $$
> With learning rate $\eta = 0.1$, compute the **updated weight matrix** after one iteration.

**Step 1 — Compute output vector $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{W}\mathbf{x}$:**

$$
\mathbf{y} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.8 & 0.0 \\ 0.0 & 0.0 & 1.0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 1 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}
\;=\; \begin{bmatrix} (0.6)(3) + (0.8)(1) + (0)(2) \\ (0)(3) + (0)(1) + (1)(2) \end{bmatrix}
\;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1.8 + 0.8 \\ 2.0 \end{bmatrix}
\;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 2.6 \\ 2.0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Update weights of neuron 1 ($i=1$, inner sum is $k=1$ only):**

$$
\Delta w_{1j} \;=\; \eta\, y_1 \bigl( x_j - w_{1j}\, y_1 \bigr)
$$

For $j=1$:
$$
\Delta w_{11} = (0.1)(2.6)\bigl( 3 - (0.6)(2.6) \bigr) = (0.26)(3 - 1.56) = (0.26)(1.44) = 0.3744
$$

For $j=2$:
$$
\Delta w_{12} = (0.1)(2.6)\bigl( 1 - (0.8)(2.6) \bigr) = (0.26)(1 - 2.08) = (0.26)(-1.08) = -0.2808
$$

For $j=3$:
$$
\Delta w_{13} = (0.1)(2.6)\bigl( 2 - (0.0)(2.6) \bigr) = (0.26)(2) = 0.52
$$

**Step 3 — Update weights of neuron 2 ($i=2$, inner sum is $k=1,2$):**

$$
\Delta w_{2j} \;=\; \eta\, y_2 \bigl( x_j - w_{1j}\, y_1 - w_{2j}\, y_2 \bigr)
$$

For $j=1$:
$$
\Delta w_{21} = (0.1)(2.0)\bigl( 3 - (0.6)(2.6) - (0.0)(2.0) \bigr) = (0.2)(3 - 1.56) = (0.2)(1.44) = 0.288
$$

For $j=2$:
$$
\Delta w_{22} = (0.1)(2.0)\bigl( 1 - (0.8)(2.6) - (0.0)(2.0) \bigr) = (0.2)(1 - 2.08) = (0.2)(-1.08) = -0.216
$$

For $j=3$:
$$
\Delta w_{23} = (0.1)(2.0)\bigl( 2 - (0.0)(2.6) - (1.0)(2.0) \bigr) = (0.2)(2 - 2.0) = (0.2)(0) = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Form the updated weight matrix $\mathbf{W}_{new} = \mathbf{W} + \Delta \mathbf{W}$:**

$$
\mathbf{W}_{new} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0.6+0.3744 & 0.8-0.2808 & 0.0+0.52 \\ 0.0+0.288 & 0.0-0.216 & 1.0+0 \end{bmatrix}
\;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0.9744 & 0.5192 & 0.5200 \\ 0.2880 & -0.2160 & 1.0000 \end{bmatrix}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Network Architecture (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INP["INPUT LAYER n-dim"]
        x1["x1"]
        x2["x2"]
        x3["x3"]
        xn["xn"]
    end

    subgraph SYN["SYNAPTIC WEIGHTS Matrix W"]
        w11["w11"]
        w12["w12"]
        w21["w21"]
        w22["w22"]
    end

    subgraph OUT["OUTPUT LAYER m-neurons"]
        y1["y1 PC1"]
        y2["y2 PC2"]
        ym["ym PCm"]
    end

    subgraph FB["FEEDBACK / DEFLATION PATH"]
        defl["Deflation Term Sum w_kj y_k"]
    end

    x1 --> w11
    x2 --> w11
    x3 --> w11
    xn --> w11
    w11 --> y1
    w12 --> y1
    w21 --> y2
    w22 --> y2

    y1 --> defl
    y2 --> defl
    defl -->|"Residual signal to next neuron"| ym
    defl -->|"orthogonalization"| w12
    defl -->|"orthogonalization"| w22
```

## 4.2 Algorithmic Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Initialize weight matrix W randomly small values"] --> B["Set learning rate eta and component count m"]
    B --> C{"For each input sample x_t in stream"}
    C --> D["Compute outputs y_i = sum over j of w_ij x_j"]
    D --> E["For neuron i = 1 to m"]
    E --> F["Compute residual r_j = x_j - sum over k=1 to i of w_kj y_k"]
    F --> G["Update w_ij = w_ij + eta y_i r_j"]
    G --> H{"i less than m"}
    H -- "Yes" --> E
    H -- "No" --> I{"Convergence criteria met"]
    I -- "No" --> C
    I -- "Yes" --> J["Output converged principal components as rows of W"]
```

## 4.3 Information-Theoretic Viewpoint

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DATA["High-Dim Input Stream X in R^n"] -->|"Linear Projection W"| PC["Low-Dim PCs in R^m"]
    PC --> FEAT["Feature Vector for Downstream Tasks"]
    FEAT --> CLS["Classification"]
    FEAT --> REG["Regression"]
    FEAT --> COMP["Compression / Reconstruction"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Arrows labeled *"orthogonalization"* represent the **deflation feedback** — the most critical conceptual element distinguishing GHA from a plain Oja's-rule ensemble. Without this feedback, all `m` output neurons would collapse to the same first principal direction.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1. State Sanger's Generalized Hebbian Learning Algorithm. How does it differ from Oja's rule? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Sanger's GHA updates weights as
$\Delta w_{ij} = \eta\, y_i \!\left( x_j - \sum_{k=1}^{i} w_{kj} y_k \right)$.
It is the **multi-unit extension of Oja's rule**, which is recovered when $m=1$. Oja's rule extracts only the **first principal component**, while GHA extracts the first `m` principal components in a single network by using a **deflation (orthogonalization) term** $\sum_{k=1}^{i-1} w_{kj} y_k$ to subtract previously learned directions.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q2. What is the role of the deflation term in GHA? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The deflation term $\sum_{k=1}^{i-1} w_{kj} y_k$ computes the projection of the current input onto the subspace already spanned by the previously learned weight vectors. By subtracting this from the input, it ensures that the `i`-th neuron learns a direction that is **orthogonal to all previously learned principal components**, enabling extraction of **distinct, mutually orthogonal** eigenvectors in decreasing order of variance.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, Q-B7]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the Sanger's Generalized Hebbian Learning Algorithm weight update rule starting from Oja's single-neuron rule. Clearly explain the role of the deflation term with mathematical justification. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the input vector $\mathbf{x} = [4,\; 2]^T$ and current weight matrix
$$
\mathbf{W} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 & 0.6 \\ 0.6 & -0.8 \end{bmatrix},
$$
compute the updated weight matrix after one iteration using Sanger's GHA with learning rate $\eta = 0.1$. Show all intermediate computations. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** State Oja's rule:
$$
\Delta w_j = \eta\, y\, (x_j - y\, w_j)
$$
It extracts the first principal eigenvector of the covariance matrix.

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** To extract the second PC, define a "residual" input to neuron $i$ that removes the contribution of neurons $1$ to $i-1$:
$$
\tilde{x}_j^{(i)} = x_j - \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} w_{kj}\, y_k
$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Apply Oja's rule to the residual:
$$
\Delta w_{ij} = \eta\, y_i\, \tilde{x}_j^{(i)} = \eta\, y_i\!\left(x_j - \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} w_{kj} y_k\right)
$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Note that the self-term ($k=i$) is already present in Oja's normalization. Including it in the sum yields the canonical Sanger update:
$$
\Delta w_{ij} = \eta\, y_i\!\left(x_j - \sum_{k=1}^{i} w_{kj} y_k\right)
$$
The deflation term ensures **orthogonalization** of successive weight vectors, guaranteeing they converge to distinct, ordered eigenvectors.

### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Compute output vector:
$$
\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{W}\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 & 0.6 \\ 0.6 & -0.8 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3.2 + 1.2 \\ 2.4 - 1.6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4.4 \\ 0.8 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Update row 1 ($i=1$, sum $k=1$):
- $j=1$: $\Delta w_{11} = (0.1)(4.4)\bigl(4 - (0.8)(4.4)\bigr) = 0.44(4 - 3.52) = 0.44(0.48) = 0.2112$
- $j=2$: $\Delta w_{12} = (0.1)(4.4)\bigl(2 - (0.6)(4.4)\bigr) = 0.44(2 - 2.64) = 0.44(-0.64) = -0.2816$

**Step 3 (3 Marks):** Update row 2 ($i=2$, sum $k=1,2$):
- $j=1$: $\Delta w_{21} = (0.1)(0.8)\bigl(4 - (0.8)(4.4) - (0.6)(0.8)\bigr) = 0.08(4 - 3.52 - 0.48) = 0.08(0) = 0$
- $j=2$: $\Delta w_{22} = (0.1)(0.8)\bigl(2 - (0.6)(4.4) - (-0.8)(0.8)\bigr) = 0.08(2 - 2.64 + 0.64) = 0.08(0) = 0$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Final updated weight matrix:
$$
