\mathbf{W}_{new} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 + 0.2112 & 0.6 - 0.2816 \\ 0.6 + 0 & -0.8 + 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0112 & 0.3184 \\ 0.6000 & -0.8000 \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the biological motivation behind Hebbian learning. Discuss its limitations and explain how Oja's rule and Sanger's GHA overcome them. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 2-D input stream with covariance matrix
$$
\mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}.
$$
Compute the eigenvalues and eigenvectors analytically. Show that the GHA network's weight vectors will asymptotically converge to these eigenvectors. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

**Biological motivation (2 Marks):** Donald Hebb (1949) postulated: *"When an axon of cell A is near enough to excite cell B, and repeatedly or persistently takes part in firing it, some growth process or metabolic change takes place in one or both cells…"* This is the synaptic plasticity principle — simultaneous activity strengthens connections.

**Limitations of pure Hebbian rule (2 Marks):** The unconstrained Hebbian update $\Delta w = \eta\, y\, x$ causes **unbounded weight growth** (synaptic saturation), and all output neurons converge to the **same dominant direction** in a multi-unit setup, preventing extraction of multiple features.

**Oja's remedy (1.5 Marks):** Adds a passive decay term $-\eta\, y^2 w$, stabilizing the weight norm to unity and extracting the first principal component.

**GHA remedy (1.5 Marks):** Adds the deflation term $-\eta\, y_i \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} w_{kj} y_k$ so that the `i`-th neuron learns from the **residual input** not explained by previous neurons, enabling extraction of multiple ordered principal components.

### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Characteristic equation $\det(\mathbf{C} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0$:
$$
(5 - \lambda)(3 - \lambda) - 4 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \lambda^2 - 8\lambda + 11 = 0
$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Eigenvalues:
$$
\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{8 \pm \sqrt{64 - 44}}{2} = \frac{8 \pm \sqrt{20}}{2} = 4 \pm \sqrt{5} \approx 4 \pm 2.236
$$
So $\lambda_1 \approx 6.236$ and $\lambda_2 \approx 1.764$.

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Eigenvector for $\lambda_1$ via $(\mathbf{C} - \lambda_1 \mathbf{I})\mathbf{v}_1 = 0$:
$$
\begin{bmatrix} 5 - 6.236 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 - 6.236 \end{bmatrix}\mathbf{v}_1 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; -1.236\, v_{11} + 2\, v_{12} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; v_{12} = 0.618\, v_{11}
$$
Normalize: $\mathbf{v}_1 \approx [0.851,\; 0.526]^T$.

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** By Sanger's stability theorem, the weight vectors of the GHA network converge to these eigenvectors, i.e., $\mathbf{w}_1^* \to \mathbf{v}_1$ and $\mathbf{w}_2^* \to \mathbf{v}_2$, with $\mathbb{E}[y_1^2] \to \lambda_1$ and $\mathbb{E}[y_2^2] \to \lambda_2$, demonstrating maximum-variance projection.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Sanger's GHA Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the upper limit of the sum** — Many students write the sum from $k=1$ to $i-1$ (which is the pure deflation) but forget to include the $k=i$ self-decay term in Oja's normalization. The *correct* Sanger formula has the sum running to $i$, not $i-1$.
> 2. **Confusing the sign of the deflation term** — It must be **subtracted** inside the parentheses; the entire term is then **multiplied** by $\eta\, y_i$ (positive).
> 3. **Skipping the Oja derivation** — A "Derive" question expects you to *start* from Oja's rule, not state Sanger's formula directly. Marks are awarded for the *transition* steps.
> 4. **Not showing the residual input** $\tilde{x}_j^{(i)}$ explicitly — the residual construction is the conceptual heart of Sanger's contribution.
> 5. **Numerical errors in forward pass** — Forgetting to multiply weights with corresponding inputs and add correctly in the matrix-vector product. Use a fresh line for each dot product.
> 6. **Not stating convergence properties** in 14-mark answers — Always close with the convergence claims (orthogonality, unit norm, ordered by eigenvalue) to fetch the last 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Sanger's Generalized Hebbian Learning Algorithm:**

- **Full name:** Generalized Hebbian Learning Algorithm (GHA) ≡ **Sanger's Rule** (1989).
- **Network type:** Single-layer, **linear**, feed-forward; no biases; identity activation.
- **Core update rule:**
  $\Delta w_{ij} = \eta\, y_i \!\left( x_j - \sum_{k=1}^{i} w_{kj} y_k \right)$.
- **Three terms inside the parentheses:**
  (i) input $x_j$,
  (ii) self-decay $-w_{ij} y_i$ (Oja's normalization),
  (iii) deflation $-\sum_{k=1}^{i-1} w_{kj} y_k$ (orthogonalization).
- **Special case:** $i=1$ reduces exactly to **Oja's rule**.
- **What it extracts:** First `m` **eigenvectors** of the input covariance matrix, ordered by decreasing eigenvalue.
- **Convergence guarantees:** Weight vectors become **orthonormal** and **maximize variance** (PCA solution).
- **Output variance:** $\mathbb{E}[y_i^2] \to \lambda_i$ (i-th eigenvalue).
- **Algorithm class:** Unsupervised, online, biologically motivated (Hebbian), deterministic given weights.
- **Engineering applications:** Face recognition (eigenfaces), image compression, biomedical feature extraction, fault diagnosis, data visualization, preprocessing for classifiers.
- **Comparison anchor:** Oja = 1 PC; GHA = `m` PCs; APEX / PCA via eigendecomposition = same result but **batch** (not online).
- **Examiner's mantra:** Always show the *forward pass* $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{W}\mathbf{x}$ *before* the update step in numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Competitive Learning
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT411 - SOFT COMPUTING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Competitive Learning

# Competitive Learning in Neural Networks

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Competitive Learning** is a form of **unsupervised learning** in artificial neural networks in which a set of output neurons (also called *units* or *clusters*) compete among themselves to become **active** (i.e., fire) in response to a given input pattern. Only **one** (or a small localized group of) output neuron(s) is selected as the winner, and only the weights of the winning neuron(s) are updated.

Mathematically, given an input vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ and a weight matrix $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ (where $m$ is the number of output neurons), the **winning neuron** $j^*$ is identified by:

$$j^* = \arg\min_{j \in \{1, 2, \ldots, m\}} \, d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{w}_j)$$

where $d(\cdot, \cdot)$ is a **dissimilarity metric** (commonly the Euclidean distance) and $\mathbf{w}_j$ is the weight vector of the $j$-th output neuron.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Competitive learning is a foundational block for advanced architectures such as **Kohonen's Self-Organizing Maps (SOM)**, **Learning Vector Quantization (LVQ)**, and **Adaptive Resonance Theory (ART)**. The *Winner-Takes-All (WTA)* and *Winner-Takes-Most (WTM)* strategies are mandatory topics for ESE and module-internal assessments.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **classroom quiz** where the teacher asks a question, and every student mentally computes how confident they are about the answer. The student with the **highest confidence** (closest match) is asked to answer, and the teacher *only* gives feedback to that one student. The other students simply listen and refine their own understanding silently for future questions.

In the neural network:
- The **students** are the output neurons.
- The **question** is the input pattern $\mathbf{x}$.
- The **confidence score** is the similarity measure (e.g., inverse Euclidean distance).
- The **student selected to answer** is the **winning neuron** $j^*$.
- The **teacher's feedback** is the weight-update rule, which adjusts *only* the winner's weights.

This "competition" forces the neurons to **specialize** in different regions of the input space, effectively performing **unsupervised clustering** and **feature extraction** without any labeled target data.

> [!TIP]
> Think of competitive learning as a **"king of the hill"** rule — only the neuron that is currently closest to the input gets to climb higher (i.e., update its weights to become even closer next time). All other neurons are left unchanged.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Learning rate** $\eta$: typically initialized in the range $\mathbf{0.1 \leq \eta_0 \leq 1.0}$ and decays over time.
- **Number of output neurons** $m$: a hyperparameter that determines the granularity of clustering.
- **Neighborhood radius** $\sigma(t)$: a time-varying parameter used in WTM (especially in SOM) to define the spatial extent of cooperation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Steps of Competitive Learning

The competitive learning algorithm operates in a **four-phase** cycle for every input presentation:

1. **Initialization Phase**
   - Initialize all weight vectors $\mathbf{w}_j(0)$ randomly (or using a small random subset of input samples).
   - Set the learning rate $\eta(0)$ and define the total number of training epochs $T$.

2. **Similarity / Competition Phase**
   - Present an input vector $\mathbf{x}(t) \in \mathbb{R}^n$.
   - Compute the activation (or equivalently, the distance) of every output neuron $j$:
     $$a_j = -d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{w}_j) = -\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_j \Vert$$
   - The neuron with the **largest activation** (i.e., the **smallest distance**) is declared the winner:
     $$j^* = \arg\max_j \, a_j$$

3. **Update Phase (Winner-Takes-All, WTA)**
   - Update **only** the weight vector of the winning neuron $j^*$:
     $$\mathbf{w}_{j^*}(t+1) = \mathbf{w}_{j^*}(t) + \eta(t)\left[\mathbf{x}(t) - \mathbf{w}_{j^*}(t)\right]$$
   - All non-winning neurons remain unchanged:
     $$\mathbf{w}_j(t+1) = \mathbf{w}_j(t), \quad \forall j \neq j^*$$

4. **Decay / Annealing Phase**
   - Reduce the learning rate:
     $$\eta(t+1) = \eta_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau_\eta} \quad \text{or} \quad \eta(t+1) = \eta_0 \left(1 - \frac{t}{T}\right)$$
   - This ensures **convergence** and prevents oscillation in later epochs.

### 2.2 Why and How Each Step Matters

- **Why initialization?** Poor initialization leads to **dead neurons** (neurons that never win) and under-utilization of network capacity.
- **Why competition?** It enforces a *division of labor* — each output neuron becomes a *prototype* or *codebook vector* for a particular region of the input space.
- **Why decay?** Without decay, weights can oscillate and never settle. A time-decreasing $\eta(t)$ mirrors the **simulated annealing** philosophy borrowed from optimization theory.
- **How WTA differs from WTM?** In **WTA**, only the winning neuron updates. In **WTM (Winner-Takes-Most)**, neurons within a *neighborhood* of the winner also update, with magnitude governed by a **neighborhood function** $h_{j,j^*}(t)$. This is the central idea of **Kohonen's SOM**.

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind the WTA Weight Update

The Kohonen update rule:
$$\mathbf{w}_{j^*}(t+1) = \mathbf{w}_{j^*}(t) + \eta(t)\left[\mathbf{x}(t) - \mathbf{w}_{j^*}(t)\right]$$
can be algebraically rearranged as:
$$\mathbf{w}_{j^*}(t+1) = (1 - \eta(t))\,\mathbf{w}_{j^*}(t) + \eta(t)\,\mathbf{x}(t)$$
This is a **convex combination** — the new weight lies on the straight line segment connecting the old weight and the input pattern, with mixing ratio $\eta(t)$. As $\eta(t) \to 0$, weights **stabilize**, achieving a **local representation** of the input distribution.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of Competitive Learning |
|---|---|
| **Image Compression** | Vector Quantization using SOM codebooks |
| **Speech Recognition** | Phoneme clustering using LVQ |
| **Anomaly Detection** | ART networks detect novel patterns |
| **Robotics** | Topological maps via SOM for navigation |
| **Bioinformatics** | Gene expression clustering |
| **Data Mining** | Customer segmentation, market basket analysis |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the high-yield formula reference for all KTU examinations on this topic.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Euclidean Distance | $d_j = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - w_{ij})^2}$ | Measures dissimilarity; unitless |
| 2 | Cosine Similarity | $\cos\theta_j = \dfrac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{w}_j}{\Vert \mathbf{x}\Vert \,\Vert \mathbf{w}_j\Vert}$ | Measures directional similarity |
| 3 | Winner Selection (Min-Distance) | $j^* = \arg\min_j d_j$ | Index of winning neuron |
| 4 | Winner Selection (Max-Similarity) | $j^* = \arg\max_j \cos\theta_j$ | Equivalent when norms are equal |
| 5 | Kohonen WTA Update | $\Delta \mathbf{w}_{j^*} = \eta(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_{j^*})$ | Only the winner updates |
| 6 | Kohonen WTM Update | $\Delta \mathbf{w}_j = \eta \, h_{j,j^*}(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_j)$ | Neighborhood also updates |
| 7 | Gaussian Neighborhood Function | $h_{j,j^*} = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Vert \mathbf{r}_j - \mathbf{r}_{j^*}\Vert^2}{2\sigma^2(t)}\right)$ | $r$ = neuron coordinates on map grid |
| 8 | Learning Rate Decay (Exponential) | $\eta(t) = \eta_0 \, e^{-t/\tau_\eta}$ | $\tau_\eta$ = time constant (epochs) |
| 9 | Neighborhood Radius Decay | $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \, e^{-t/\tau_\sigma}$ | $\tau_\sigma$ = time constant (epochs) |
| 10 | Quantization Error | $E_q = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N}\Vert \mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{w}_{j^*(k)}\Vert^2$ | Performance metric |

> **Note on notation:** In the table above, all occurrences of absolute value or norm use the LaTeX `\Vert` symbol to avoid breaking the markdown table structure.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Kohonen WTA Weight Update Rule

We begin from the **objective function** that competitive learning implicitly minimizes — the average distortion error between each input $\mathbf{x}_k$ and the weight vector of its winning neuron:

$$E = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{k=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1}^{m} \mathbf{1}_{\{j = j^*(k)\}} \, \Vert \mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{w}_j \Vert^2$$

**Step 1:** Take the partial derivative of $E$ with respect to a single winning weight $\mathbf{w}_{j^*}$ (assuming $j^*$ is the winner for input $\mathbf{x}_k$):

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial \mathbf{w}_{j^*}} = -\left(\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{w}_{j^*}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Apply **gradient descent** in discrete time with step size $\eta(t)$:

$$\mathbf{w}_{j^*}(t+1) = \mathbf{w}_{j^*}(t) - \eta(t)\,\frac{\partial E}{\partial \mathbf{w}_{j^*}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the gradient:

$$\mathbf{w}_{j^*}(t+1) = \mathbf{w}_{j^*}(t) + \eta(t)\left(\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{w}_{j^*}(t)\right)$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to verify the convex combination property:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{w}_{j^*}(t+1) &= \mathbf{w}_{j^*}(t) + \eta(t)\,\mathbf{x}_k - \eta(t)\,\mathbf{w}_{j^*}(t) \\
&= \mathbf{w}_{j^*}(t)\,(1 - \eta(t)) + \eta(t)\,\mathbf{x}_k
\end{aligned}$$

This is a **convex combination** because $0 < \eta(t) < 1$, ensuring that the new weight vector always lies between the old weight and the input — this is precisely the **Kohonen WTA update rule**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A competitive layer has **3 output neurons** with the following weight vectors:
> $\mathbf{w}_1 = (0.6, 0.2)$, $\mathbf{w}_2 = (0.3, 0.9)$, $\mathbf{w}_3 = (0.8, 0.7)$.
> An input $\mathbf{x} = (0.5, 0.4)$ is presented. Identify the winning neuron and update its weight with $\eta = 0.2$.

**Step 1 — Compute Euclidean distance to each neuron:**

$$d_1 = \sqrt{(0.5 - 0.6)^2 + (0.4 - 0.2)^2} = \sqrt{0.01 + 0.04} = \sqrt{0.05} \approx 0.2236$$

$$d_2 = \sqrt{(0.5 - 0.3)^2 + (0.4 - 0.9)^2} = \sqrt{0.04 + 0.25} = \sqrt{0.29} \approx 0.5385$$

$$d_3 = \sqrt{(0.5 - 0.8)^2 + (0.4 - 0.7)^2} = \sqrt{0.09 + 0.09} = \sqrt{0.18} \approx 0.4243$$

**Step 2 — Identify the winning neuron (minimum distance):**

$$j^* = \arg\min\{0.2236,\, 0.5385,\, 0.4243\} = 1$$

> **Neuron 1 wins the competition.**

**Step 3 — Apply the Kohonen WTA update rule to neuron 1 only:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{w}_1^{\text{new}} &= \mathbf{w}_1^{\text{old}} + \eta(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_1^{\text{old}}) \\
&= (0.6, 0.2) + 0.2\,[(0.5, 0.4) - (0.6, 0.2)] \\
&= (0.6, 0.2) + 0.2\,(-0.1, 0.2) \\
&= (0.6, 0.2) + (-0.02, 0.04) \\
&= (0.58, 0.24)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Confirm neurons 2 and 3 remain unchanged:**

$$\mathbf{w}_2^{\text{new}} = (0.3, 0.9), \qquad \mathbf{w}_3^{\text{new}} = (0.8, 0.7)$$

**Step 5 — Verify the convex combination property:**

For each component of $\mathbf{w}_1$:
- New $w_{1,1} = 0.8 \times 0.6 + 0.2 \times 0.5 = 0.48 + 0.10 = 0.58$ ✓
- New $w_{1,2} = 0.8 \times 0.2 + 0.2 \times 0.4 = 0.16 + 0.08 = 0.24$ ✓

The result confirms that the new weight is a **weighted average** of the old weight and the input pattern.

---

### 3.3 Multi-Input Iterative Update Example (Convergence Insight)

Suppose the same network is trained over **2 epochs** with the input sequence $\mathbf{x}_1 = (0.5, 0.4)$, $\mathbf{x}_2 = (0.9, 0.1)$, and $\eta = 0.5$ (fixed for illustration).

| Epoch | Input | Winner | Old $\mathbf{w}_{j^*}$ | New $\mathbf{w}_{j^*}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $(0.5, 0.4)$ | Neuron 1 | $(0.6, 0.2)$ | $(0.55, 0.30)$ |
| 1 | $(0.9, 0.1)$ | Neuron 2 | $(0.3, 0.9)$ | $(0.60, 0.50)$ |
| 2 | $(0.5, 0.4)$ | Neuron 1 | $(0.55, 0.30)$ | $(0.525, 0.350)$ |
| 2 | $(0.9, 0.1)$ | Neuron 2 | $(0.60, 0.50)$ | $(0.750, 0.300)$ |

Notice that **Neuron 3 never won** in this short run — this is a classic case of the **"dead neuron" / "under-utilization" problem**, which is mitigated in practice by:
- **Conscience mechanism** (DeSieno, 1988) — adds a bias term to penalize frequent winners.
- **Initialization with random samples** instead of random weights.
- **Soft competition** / WTM with broad neighborhood radius.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Maxnet Architecture (Competition Sub-Network)

The **Maxnet** is a recurrent network introduced by Lippmann (1987) that is used to identify the *maximum* among a set of activations. The competitive layer often uses a Maxnet-like mechanism to perform the WTA selection.

```mermaid
graph TD
    inputA["INPUT ACTIVATIONS"] --> nodeA1["UNIT 1"]
    inputA --> nodeA2["UNIT 2"]
    inputA --> nodeA3["UNIT 3"]
    inputA --> nodeA4["UNIT M"]

    nodeA1 -- "POSITIVE SELF FEEDBACK +EPSILON" --> nodeA1
    nodeA2 -- "POSITIVE SELF FEEDBACK +EPSILON" --> nodeA2
    nodeA3 -- "POSITIVE SELF FEEDBACK +EPSILON" --> nodeA3
    nodeA4 -- "POSITIVE SELF FEEDBACK +EPSILON" --> nodeA4

    nodeA1 -- "INHIBITORY WEIGHT -MU" --> nodeA2
    nodeA1 -- "INHIBITORY WEIGHT -MU" --> nodeA3
    nodeA1 -- "INHIBITORY WEIGHT -MU" --> nodeA4

    nodeA2 -- "INHIBITORY WEIGHT -MU" --> nodeA1
    nodeA2 -- "INHIBITORY WEIGHT -MU" --> nodeA3
    nodeA2 -- "INHIBITORY WEIGHT -MU" --> nodeA4

    nodeA3 -- "INHIBITORY WEIGHT -MU" --> nodeA1
    nodeA3 -- "INHIBITORY WEIGHT -MU" --> nodeA2
    nodeA3 -- "INHIBITORY WEIGHT -MU" --> nodeA4

    nodeA4 -- "INHIBITORY WEIGHT -MU" --> nodeA1
    nodeA4 -- "INHIBITORY WEIGHT -MU" --> nodeA2
    nodeA4 -- "INHIBITORY WEIGHT -MU" --> nodeA3

    nodeA1 --> outputA1["OUTPUT: 1 FOR WINNER, 0 FOR OTHERS"]
    nodeA2 --> outputA2["OUTPUT: 1 FOR WINNER, 0 FOR OTHERS"]
    nodeA3 --> outputA3["OUTPUT: 1 FOR WINNER, 0 FOR OTHERS"]
    nodeA4 --> outputA4["OUTPUT: 1 FOR WINNER, 0 FOR OTHERS"]
```

**Key parameters of Maxnet:**
- **Self-feedback weight** $= +\epsilon$ (small positive value, $0 < \epsilon < 1/m$).
- **Lateral inhibition weight** $= -\mu$ (negative value, $\mu > 0$, chosen such that $0 < \mu < 1/m$).
- After several iterations, **only the maximum** activation survives; all others decay to zero.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of a Complete Competitive Learning Network

```mermaid
graph TD
    inp["INPUT PATTERN X VECTOR N DIMENSIONS"] --> distCalc["DISTANCE COMPUTATION MODULE: EUCLIDEAN OR COSINE"]
    weightBank["WEIGHT MATRIX W: M NEURONS, EACH N DIMENSIONS"] --> distCalc
    distCalc --> compete["COMPETITION LAYER: MAXNET OR HARD WTA"]
    compete --> winnerSel["WINNING NEURON SELECTION J STAR"]
    winnerSel --> updateRule["KOHONEN UPDATE RULE: W J STAR PLUS ETA TIMES X MINUS W J STAR"]
    updateRule --> weightBank
    decay["LEARNING RATE DECAY: ETA T PLUS 1 EQUALS ETA ZERO EXP T BY TAU"] --> updateRule
    neighDecay["NEIGHBORHOOD DECAY: SIGMA T DECREASES OVER TIME"] --> updateRule
```

---

### 4.3 Kohonen SOM Topological Grid (Block Functional View)

```mermaid
graph TD
    inX["INPUT SPACE: HIGH DIMENSIONAL DATA"] --> mapL["SOM LAYER: 2D GRID OF M NEURONS, FULLY CONNECTED"]
    mapL --> wts["WEIGHT VECTORS W J: EACH POINTS INTO INPUT SPACE"]
    wts --> bmu["BEST MATCHING UNIT BMU: NEURON CLOSEST TO INPUT"]
    bmu --> updateZone["UPDATE ZONE: BMU AND ITS NEIGHBORS"]
    updateZone --> topoMap["OUTPUT: TOPOLOGY PRESERVING 2D MAP OF INPUT SPACE"]
```

**Why the grid structure?** The 2D arrangement of neurons embeds a *topology* — neighboring neurons on the grid have similar weight vectors. This is the **topographic map** property of SOM, a direct consequence of the neighborhood function $h_{j,j^*}$.

---

### 4.4 Comparison: WTA vs. WTM Update Logic

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph WTA["WINNER TAKES ALL: ONLY BMU UPDATES"]
        wtaA["INPUT X"] --> wtaB["FIND BMU"]
        wtaB --> wtaC["UPDATE ONLY BMU WEIGHT"]
        wtaC --> wtaD["ALL OTHER NEURONS: NO CHANGE"]
    end

    subgraph WTM["WINNER TAKES MOST: BMU PLUS NEIGHBORHOOD UPDATES"]
        wtmA["INPUT X"] --> wtmB["FIND BMU"]
        wtmB --> wtmC["DEFINE NEIGHBORHOOD N J STAR"]
        wtmC --> wtmD["UPDATE WEIGHTS USING H J J STAR FUNCTION"]
        wtmD --> wtmE["FAR NEURONS: NEGLIGIBLE UPDATE"]
    end
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define competitive learning. What is meant by Winner-Takes-All (WTA) strategy?*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> Competitive learning is an **unsupervised learning paradigm** in which output neurons of a neural network **compete** with one another to become active in response to a given input. The neuron whose weight vector is *closest* to the input (i.e., has the **smallest Euclidean distance** or **largest similarity**) is declared the winner.
>
> In the **Winner-Takes-All (WTA)** strategy, **only the winning neuron** is permitted to update its weights, while all other neurons remain unchanged. The Kohonen update rule is:
>
> $$\mathbf{w}_{j^*}(t+1) = \mathbf{w}_{j^*}(t) + \eta(t)\left[\mathbf{x}(t) - \mathbf{w}_{j^*}(t)\right]$$
>
> The WTA rule produces **hard clustering** and is used in classical competitive learning and Learning Vector Quantization (LVQ). **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Winner-Takes-All (WTA) and Winner-Takes-Most (WTM) learning rules.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Winner-Takes-All (WTA) | Winner-Takes-Most (WTM) |
|---|---|---|
| Neurons updated | Only the winner (BMU) | Winner **plus** topological neighbors |
| Update magnitude | Equal $\eta$ for winner | Decays with $h_{j,j^*}(t)$ |
| Topology preservation | No (only discrete clustering) | **Yes** (SOM-like) |
| Common use | LVQ, classical clustering | Kohonen SOM |
| Equation | $\Delta \mathbf{w}_{j^*} = \eta(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_{j^*})$ | $\Delta \mathbf{w}_j = \eta \, h_{j,j^*}(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_j)$ |

WTA is a *special case* of WTM when the neighborhood function $h_{j,j^*}$ is a **Kronecker delta** (1 for the winner, 0 elsewhere). **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]*

**Q.A.** *(a)* Explain the architecture and learning algorithm of a **Maxnet** used in competitive learning networks. How does the choice of weights $\epsilon$ and $\mu$ affect convergence? **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A competitive layer has **4 output neurons** with the following weight vectors:
$\mathbf{w}_1 = (0.2, 0.8)$, $\mathbf{w}_2 = (0.6, 0.4)$, $\mathbf{w}_3 = (0.9, 0.1)$, $\mathbf{w}_4 = (0.1, 0.5)$.

An input $\mathbf{x} = (0.7, 0.3)$ is presented. With $\eta = 0.3$, identify the winning neuron and perform **one iteration of the Kohonen WTA update**. Verify the convex combination property. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Q.A.(a) — Maxnet Architecture**

**Architecture of Maxnet (Lippmann, 1987):**

The Maxnet is a **recurrent, fully interconnected** network of $m$ neurons, where each neuron is connected to itself with a **positive weight** $+\epsilon$ and to every other neuron with a **negative (inhibitory) weight** $-\mu$.

**Mathematical update at iteration $t$:**

$$y_j(t+1) = f\!\left[y_j(t) + \epsilon\, y_j(t) - \mu \sum_{k \neq j} y_k(t)\right]$$

where $f(\cdot)$ is a non-linear activation, often a **half-wave rectifier**:

$$f(z) = \begin{cases} z & \text{if } z \geq 0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step-by-step operating procedure:**

1. Initialize $y_j(0) = a_j$ (the input activations / distances or similarities from the previous layer).
2. For each iteration, apply the update rule above.
3. The neuron with the **largest initial activation** is reinforced by its self-feedback, while the lateral inhibition progressively **suppresses** all other neurons.
4. After $T$ iterations (typically $T \leq m$), only the **maximum** neuron remains active; all others are zero.

**Convergence constraints on $\epsilon$ and $\mu$:**

- $\epsilon > 0$ (self-excitation), typically $\epsilon < 1/m$.
- $\mu > 1/m$ is **NOT** allowed because it would cause all neurons to die out.
- $0 < \mu < 1/m$ ensures that the maximum value grows relative to others.
- A common safe choice: $\epsilon = 0.25/m$ and $\mu = 0.6/m$ (heuristic).

> **Why does this converge?** The dynamics form a **winner-take-all dynamical system** with the maximum activation as a stable attractor — equivalent to the *Hoppfield* energy minimization in the special case of a single positive fixed point. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Q.A.(b) — Numerical WTA Update**

**Step 1 — Compute distances from input $\mathbf{x} = (0.7, 0.3)$ to each weight vector:**

$$d_1 = \sqrt{(0.7 - 0.2)^2 + (0.3 - 0.8)^2} = \sqrt{0.25 + 0.25} = \sqrt{0.50} \approx 0.7071$$

$$d_2 = \sqrt{(0.7 - 0.6)^2 + (0.3 - 0.4)^2} = \sqrt{0.01 + 0.01} = \sqrt{0.02} \approx 0.1414$$

$$d_3 = \sqrt{(0.7 - 0.9)^2 + (0.3 - 0.1)^2} = \sqrt{0.04 + 0.04} = \sqrt{0.08} \approx 0.2828$$

$$d_4 = \sqrt{(0.7 - 0.1)^2 + (0.3 - 0.5)^2} = \sqrt{0.36 + 0.04} = \sqrt{0.40} \approx 0.6325$$

**Step 2 — Winner identification:**

$$j^* = \arg\min\{0.7071, 0.1414, 0.2828, 0.6325\} = 2$$

> **Neuron 2 wins** because $d_2 = 0.1414$ is the minimum.

**Step 3 — Apply Kohonen WTA update to neuron 2 with $\eta = 0.3$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{w}_2^{\text{new}} &= \mathbf{w}_2 + \eta(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_2) \\
&= (0.6, 0.4) + 0.3\,[(0.7, 0.3) - (0.6, 0.4)] \\
&= (0.6, 0.4) + 0.3\,(0.1, -0.1) \\
&= (0.6, 0.4) + (0.03, -0.03) \\
&= (0.63, 0.37)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Confirm neurons 1, 3, 4 are unchanged:**

$$\mathbf{w}_1 = (0.2, 0.8), \quad \mathbf{w}_3 = (0.9, 0.1), \quad \mathbf{w}_4 = (0.1, 0.5)$$

**Step 5 — Verify the convex combination:**

For each component, the new weight must equal $(1-\eta)\cdot w_{j^*}^{\text{old}} + \eta \cdot x_i$:

- $w_{2,1}^{\text{new}} = 0.7 \times 0.6 + 0.3 \times 0.7 = 0.42 + 0.21 = 0.63$ ✓
- $w_{2,2}^{\text{new}} = 0.7 \times 0.4 + 0.3 \times 0.3 = 0.28 + 0.09 = 0.37$ ✓

> Since $0 < \eta < 1$, the new weight lies on the line segment joining the old weight and the input. **The convex combination property is verified.** **(7 marks)**

---

### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**Q.B.** *(a)* With neat diagrams, describe the **architecture of a Kohonen Self-Organizing Map (SOM)**. Explain how the **neighborhood function** $h_{j,j^*}(t)$ governs the Winner-Takes-Most (WTM) update. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Explain **Learning Vector Quantization (LVQ)** as an extension of competitive learning. How does it differ from SOM in terms of supervision and update rule? **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Q.B.(a) — SOM Architecture & Neighborhood Function**

**Architecture of Kohonen SOM:**

A Kohonen SOM consists of **two layers**:
1. **Input layer** — $n$ neurons (one per input dimension), fully connected to the next layer.
2. **Kohonen / Output layer** — a 1D, 2D, or 3D **grid** of $m$ neurons (typically arranged as a rectangular or hexagonal lattice).

Each output neuron $j$ has a weight vector $\mathbf{w}_j \in \mathbb{R}^n$ (a *codebook* or *prototype*). The grid structure embeds a **topology** that the algorithm preserves in the input space.

**Step-by-step SOM algorithm:**

1. Initialize $\mathbf{w}_j(0)$ for all $j$ (random or sample-based).
2. For each input $\mathbf{x}(t)$:
   - Find the **Best Matching Unit (BMU)** $j^* = \arg\min_j \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_j \Vert$.
   - Update the weights of $j^*$ **and** its neighbors using:
     $$\mathbf{w}_j(t+1) = \mathbf{w}_j(t) + \eta(t)\,h_{j,j^*}(t)\left[\mathbf{x}(t) - \mathbf{w}_j(t)\right]$$
3. Decay $\eta(t)$ and $\sigma(t)$ (neighborhood radius) over time.

**Neighborhood function** (most common — Gaussian):

$$h_{j,j^*}(t) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Vert \mathbf{r}_j - \mathbf{r}_{j^*}\Vert^2}{2\sigma^2(t)}\right)$$

where $\mathbf{r}_j$ is the **2D coordinate** of neuron $j$ on the grid.

**Why the neighborhood function governs WTM:**
- $h_{j,j^*} = 1$ when $j = j^*$ (the winner itself) → strongest update.
- $h_{j,j^*} \to 0$ as $\mathbf{r}_j$ moves far from $\mathbf{r}_{j^*}$ → negligible update.
- As $\sigma(t)$ decays, the neighborhood **shrinks**, transitioning smoothly from **WTM (early phase)** to **WTA (late phase)**.
- This produces a **topology-preserving mapping** — nearby grid points end up representing nearby input patterns. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Q.B.(b) — Learning Vector Quantization (LVQ)**

**Conceptual Overview:**

Learning Vector Quantization (LVQ) is a **supervised** extension of competitive learning, introduced by **Teuvo Kohonen (1986)**. It uses *labeled* training data to adjust the positions of the codebook (weight) vectors so that they approximate the **Bayes decision boundaries** of the classification problem.

**LVQ Algorithm (LVQ1):**

1. Initialize codebook vectors $\mathbf{w}_j$ for each class (random or via clustering).
2. Present an input $\mathbf{x}$ with class label $c(\mathbf{x})$.
3. Find the **winning neuron** $j^* = \arg\min_j \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_j \Vert$.
4. Apply the **class-sensitive update rule**:

   - **If** $j^*$ belongs to the *correct* class as $\mathbf{x}$: **move $\mathbf{w}_{j^*}$ toward $\mathbf{x}$**:
     $$\mathbf{w}_{j^*}(t+1) = \mathbf{w}_{j^*}(t) + \eta(t)\left[\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_{j^*}(t)\right]$$
   - **If** $j^*$ belongs to the *wrong* class: **move $\mathbf{w}_{j^*}$ away from $\mathbf{x}$**:
     $$\mathbf{w}_{j^*}(t+1) = \mathbf{w}_{j^*}(t) - \eta(t)\left[\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_{j^*}(t)\right]$$

**Key Differences: LVQ vs. SOM**

| Aspect | LVQ | SOM |
|---|---|---|
| **Learning type** | Supervised (uses class labels) | Unsupervised (no labels) |
| **Update rule** | Class-dependent sign | Always positive (toward input) |
| **Neighborhood** | None (WTA only) | Yes (WTM with $h_{j,j^*}$) |
| **Output topology** | No fixed topology | Topology-preserving 2D map |
| **Application** | Pattern classification | Clustering, visualization, dimensionality reduction |
| **Stability** | Sensitive to initialization | Generally stable |

> **Engineering takeaway:** LVQ is the *classification-oriented* cousin of SOM — it sacrifices topological preservation for sharper class boundaries, making it ideal for small-to-medium-sized pattern recognition problems. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes where KTU students lose easy marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the decay of $\eta(t)$** — In long answer questions on competitive learning, students often write a *fixed* $\eta$ in the update rule. The KTU model answer key explicitly awards marks for $\eta(t)$ being *time-varying*. Always mention exponential or linear decay.
>
> 2. **Confusing WTA with WTM** — Many students interchange the two in numerical problems. Remember: **WTA = only winner**, **WTM = winner + neighbors** (governed by $h_{j,j^*}$).
>
> 3. **Sign error in distance vs. activation** — A neuron with **minimum distance** has **maximum activation**. Writing "$j^* = \arg\max d_j$" instead of "$j^* = \arg\min d_j$" costs 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Skipping the convex combination verification** — A numerical problem on WTA update is *incomplete* without verifying the convex combination property $(1-\eta)\mathbf{w}_{j^*} + \eta \mathbf{x}$. Examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for this step.
>
> 5. **Not mentioning Maxnet constraints** — Writing "$\mu < 1/m$" without justification is a half-answer. State *why*: to ensure the maximum activation grows while others decay, preserving convergence.
>
> 6. **Drawing SOM as a biological diagram** — Examiners expect a **block architecture** (input layer + Kohonen layer), not a brain sketch. Marks are lost on vague diagrams.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Competitive Learning (Module 3):**

- ✅ Competitive learning is **unsupervised** — neurons compete; only the closest (BMU) wins.
- ✅ **WTA**: $\mathbf{w}_{j^*} \leftarrow \mathbf{w}_{j^*} + \eta(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_{j^*})$; only winner updates.
- ✅ **WTM**: same rule multiplied by $h_{j,j^*}(t) = \exp\!\left(-\Vert \mathbf{r}_j - \mathbf{r}_{j^*}\Vert^2 / 2\sigma^2(t)\right)$; neighbors also update.
- ✅ Winner identification: $j^* = \arg\min_j d_j = \arg\min_j \sqrt{\sum_i (x_i - w_{ij})^2}$.
- ✅ **Convex combination property**: new weight = $(1-\eta)\cdot\text{old} + \eta\cdot\text{input}$.
- ✅ **Maxnet**: recurrent WTA — $+\epsilon$ self-feedback, $-\mu$ lateral inhibition, $0 < \epsilon, \mu < 1/m$.
- ✅ **Learning rate decay**: $\eta(t) = \eta_0 e^{-t/\tau_\eta}$ ensures convergence.
- ✅ **SOM** = Kohonen network = unsupervised, topology-preserving 2D map.
- ✅ **LVQ** = supervised extension of competitive learning; uses class labels; WTA update with sign determined by label match.
- ✅ **Dead neuron problem** solved by: (a) conscience mechanism, (b) sample-based initialization, (c) soft competition (WTM).
- ✅ **Quantization error** $E_q = \frac{1}{N}\sum_k \Vert \mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{w}_{j^*(k)}\Vert^2$ measures clustering quality.
- ✅ Engineering applications: image compression, speech recognition, anomaly detection, customer segmentation, robotics mapping.

---

topics.title: Self-Organizing Computational Maps: Kohonen Network
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT411 - SOFT COMPUTING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Self-Organizing Computational Maps: Kohonen Network

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Self-Organizing Map (SOM)**, also known as a **Kohonen Self-Organizing Network** (named after Finnish academic **Teuvo Kohonen**, 1982), is a type of **unsupervised artificial neural network** that employs a **competitive learning paradigm** to produce a low-dimensional (typically two-dimensional), discretized representation of the input space of the training samples. This is called a **map** or **feature map**.

The network uses a process called **self-organization** to group similar input patterns together in the output layer without the provision of target outputs. The architecture is fundamentally based on the concept of **topographic mapping** found in the human cerebral cortex, where spatially adjacent neurons respond to similar sensory inputs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Precise):**
> A Kohonen Self-Organizing Feature Map (SOFM) is a competitive, unsupervised learning network that preserves the topological structure of the input data by projecting high-dimensional input vectors onto a lower-dimensional (usually 1-D or 2-D) lattice of neurons, where spatially close neurons in the lattice become tuned to similar input features.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

**Real-World Analogy: The "City Map of Sounds" Analogy**

Imagine a large, blank wall in a city, and you want to create a "map of music genres." You have 1000 sound clips — some are rock, some are jazz, some are classical, some are pop. You don't know which clip belongs to which genre (no labels).

You give the wall a 2D grid of pins (the output neurons). For each sound clip you play:
1. You find the pin that "vibrates the most" with the sound — this is the **Best Matching Unit (BMU)** — the most excited neuron.
2. That pin, and its **neighbors** on the wall, get "tuned" a little to remember that sound better.
3. You play the next sound. Again, the closest-vibrating pin lights up, and it learns too.

After thousands of sound clips, the wall organizes itself: rock clusters in one region, jazz in another, classical in yet another — and the regions are **topographically arranged** such that similar genres (e.g., blues and jazz) end up adjacent to each other. **The wall has "self-organized" without ever being told the genres.**

The Kohonen network does exactly this — but for **vectors of numbers** instead of sound clips, producing a topological map where similar inputs activate nearby neurons.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:**
> Unlike supervised networks (like backpropagation), SOM does NOT need target outputs. It discovers structure in data autonomously — making it a powerful **clustering and dimensionality reduction** tool.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Notation | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|---|
| Input dimension | $n$ | 2, 3, ..., 100+ |
| Output grid dimension | $m \times m$ or $m_1 \times m_2$ | $5 \times 5$ to $20 \times 20$ |
| Number of input samples | $N$ | Problem dependent |
| Initial learning rate | $\eta_0$ | **0.1** to **1.0** (commonly 0.5) |
| Final learning rate | $\eta_f$ | **0.01** (decays to small value) |
| Initial neighborhood radius | $\sigma_0$ | Equal to grid radius |
| Final neighborhood radius | $\sigma_f$ | **1.0** (often 1) |
| Total iterations | $\lambda$ | $500$ to $50,\!000$ |
| Distance metric | Euclidean | $\lVert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w} \rVert$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Network Architecture

The Kohonen network consists of two layers:

1. **Input Layer:** $n$ neurons (one per input feature). Acts as a passive distribution layer — does NOT process the input.
2. **Output/Competition Layer (Kohonen Layer):** A 1-D or 2-D array of $M$ output neurons arranged on a lattice. Each output neuron is fully connected to every input neuron.

```
        Input Layer              Output Layer (2-D Grid)
       (n neurons)              (e.g., 3x3 grid shown below)
                                    
        x1 ────────╲            O ─── O ─── O
        x2 ────────╲╲          /│\  /│\  /│\
        x3 ──────── ><────────O───O───O
        ..         ╱╱          \│/  \│/  \│/
        xn ────────╱            O ─── O ─── O
                                    
        w_ij = weight         Each circle is an output neuron
        from input i to       with its own weight vector
        output j
```

**Weight Vector Notation:** Each output neuron $j$ has a weight vector $\mathbf{w}_j = [w_{1j}, w_{2j}, \ldots, w_{nj}]^T$ of the same dimension as the input vector $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^T$.

## 2.2 The Three Core Operations

The Kohonen algorithm performs three operations at each iteration $t$:

### Operation 1: Competition (Finding the BMU)
For an input vector $\mathbf{x}(t)$, the **Best Matching Unit (BMU)**, denoted $i^*(\mathbf{x})$, is the output neuron whose weight vector is **closest to the input** in Euclidean distance:

$$
i^*(\mathbf{x}) = \arg\min_{j} \; \lVert \mathbf{x}(t) - \mathbf{w}_j(t) \rVert
$$

This neuron "wins" the competition and represents the input.

### Operation 2: Cooperation (Neighborhood Function)
The BMU's topological neighbors also get updated. The neighborhood is defined by a **Gaussian neighborhood function** centered on the BMU:

$$
h_{j,i^*}(t) = \eta(t) \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{d_{j,i^*}^2}{2\sigma^2(t)}\right)
$$

Where:
- $d_{j,i^*}$ = Euclidean distance between neuron $j$ and the BMU $i^*$ on the output grid
- $\sigma(t)$ = time-decaying neighborhood radius
- $\eta(t)$ = time-decaying learning rate

### Operation 3: Weight Update (Adaptation)
The weight vectors of the BMU and its neighbors are moved **closer to the input vector**:

$$
\mathbf{w}_j(t+1) = \mathbf{w}_j(t) + h_{j,i^*}(t)\bigl[\mathbf{x}(t) - \mathbf{w}_j(t)\bigr]
$$

Neurons far from the BMU on the grid are unaffected (their $h \approx 0$).

## 2.3 Decay Schedules (Monotonically Decreasing Functions)

The learning rate and neighborhood radius decay with time to ensure convergence and fine-tuning:

$$
\eta(t) = \eta_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{t}{\tau_\eta}\right) \quad \text{or} \quad \eta(t) = \eta_0 \left(1 - \frac{t}{T}\right)
$$

$$
\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{t}{\tau_\sigma}\right) \quad \text{or} \quad \sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \left(1 - \frac{t}{T}\right)
$$

Where $T$ is the total number of iterations and $\tau_\eta, \tau_\sigma$ are time constants.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $i^* = \arg\min_j \lVert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_j \rVert$ | Best Matching Unit (Winner Selection) | $i^*$: winning neuron index |
| 2 | $d_{j,i^*} = \lVert \mathbf{r}_j - \mathbf{r}_{i^*} \rVert$ | Grid-distance between neuron $j$ and BMU | $\mathbf{r}$: position on output lattice |
| 3 | $h_{j,i^*}(t) = \eta(t) \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{d_{j,i^*}^2}{2\sigma^2(t)}\right)$ | Gaussian Neighborhood Function | $h$: update strength; $\sigma$: effective radius |
| 4 | $\mathbf{w}_j(t+1) = \mathbf{w}_j(t) + h_{j,i^*}(t)\bigl[\mathbf{x}(t) - \mathbf{w}_j(t)\bigr]$ | Weight Update Rule | $\mathbf{w}$: weight vector |
| 5 | $\eta(t) = \eta_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{t}{\tau_\eta}\right)$ | Exponential learning-rate decay | $\eta_0$: initial rate; $\tau_\eta$: time constant |
| 6 | $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{t}{\tau_\sigma}\right)$ | Exponential neighborhood-radius decay | $\sigma_0$: initial radius |
| 7 | $\mathbf{w}_j(\text{init}) \sim U(0,1)$ or random | Weight initialization | $U$: uniform distribution |
| 8 | $\lVert \mathbf{x} \rVert = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2}$ | Euclidean norm of vector | $n$: input dimension |
| 9 | $\text{Quantization Error (QE)} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} \lVert \mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{w}_{i^*} \rVert$ | Map accuracy metric | $N$: number of input samples |
| 10 | $\text{Topographic Error} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} u(\mathbf{x}_k)$ | Topography preservation | $u=1$ if 1st and 2nd BMU are non-adjacent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL: Use `\lVert` and `\rVert` (or `\vert` / `\mid`) for norms. NEVER use the bare pipe `|` character — it breaks Markdown tables!**

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Clustering & Pattern Recognition:** Customer segmentation, gene expression analysis.
- **Dimensionality Reduction:** Pre-processing for high-dimensional sensor data (IoT, biomedical signals).
- **Anomaly Detection:** Identifying outliers in industrial process control.
- **Speech Recognition:** Phoneme mapping in acoustic models.
- **Robotics:** Adaptive control, navigation maps (self-organizing spatial representations).
- **Image Compression:** Vector quantization using the map's codebook.
- **Process Monitoring in KTU-relevant industries (Kerala context):** Coconut/cashew quality grading, marine fisheries clustering, power-load pattern discovery in Kerala State Electricity Board datasets.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Weight Update Rule

The Kohonen weight update can be derived by **expectation maximization** considerations of the input distribution.

**Starting Principle:** We wish to minimize the average distortion between the input vectors and their BMU codebook vectors.

$$
E = \int \lVert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_{i^*(\mathbf{x})} \rVert^2 \, p(\mathbf{x}) \, d\mathbf{x}
$$

**Gradient Descent Approach:** The stochastic gradient of $E$ with respect to the weight of the winning neuron $i^*$ (and its neighbors in the topographic sense) is:

$$
\frac{\partial E}{\partial \mathbf{w}_j} = -2 \, h_{j,i^*} \, \bigl[\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_j\bigr]
$$

Performing gradient descent with step size $\eta(t)$:

$$
\mathbf{w}_j(t+1) = \mathbf{w}_j(t) - \eta(t) \frac{\partial E}{\partial \mathbf{w}_j}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\mathbf{w}_j(t+1) = \mathbf{w}_j(t) + 2 \, \eta(t) \, h_{j,i^*} \, \bigl[\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_j\bigr]
$$

Absorbing the constant "2" into the definition of $\eta(t)$ yields the **canonical Kohonen update rule**:

$$
\boxed{\,\mathbf{w}_j(t+1) = \mathbf{w}_j(t) + \eta(t) \, h_{j,i^*} \, \bigl[\mathbf{x}(t) - \mathbf{w}_j(t)\bigr]\,}
$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Favorite)

### **Problem Statement**

A Kohonen network has **2 input neurons** and a **1-D output layer of 3 neurons** (line topology: A—B—C). Use the following:

- **Input pattern:** $\mathbf{x} = (1, 1)^T$
- **Initial weight matrix:**
$$
\mathbf{W} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & 0.8 & 0.5 \\ 0.6 & 0.4 & 0.9 \end{bmatrix}
$$
(Column $j$ is the weight vector $\mathbf{w}_j$ of neuron $j$.)
- **Initial learning rate:** $\eta(0) = 0.5$
- **Neighborhood function** at $t=0$: $h_{j,i^*}(0) = 0.5$ if $j$ is the BMU or its immediate neighbor; $h_{j,i^*}(0) = 0$ otherwise.

**Task:** Perform **one complete iteration** of the Kohonen algorithm and find the new weights.

### **Step 1: Compute Euclidean Distance from Input to Each Neuron**

For neuron A ($\mathbf{w}_A = (0.2, 0.6)^T$):

$$
d_A = \sqrt{(1-0.2)^2 + (1-0.6)^2} = \sqrt{0.64 + 0.16} = \sqrt{0.80} \approx 0.894
$$

For neuron B ($\mathbf{w}_B = (0.8, 0.4)^T$):

$$
d_B = \sqrt{(1-0.8)^2 + (1-0.4)^2} = \sqrt{0.04 + 0.36} = \sqrt{0.40} \approx 0.632
$$

For neuron C ($\mathbf{w}_C = (0.5, 0.9)^T$):

$$
d_C = \sqrt{(1-0.5)^2 + (1-0.9)^2} = \sqrt{0.25 + 0.01} = \sqrt{0.26} \approx 0.510
$$

### **Step 2: Identify the Best Matching Unit (BMU)**

The BMU is the neuron with the **minimum** distance:

$$
i^* = \arg\min\{d_A, d_B, d_C\} = \arg\min\{0.894,\; 0.632,\; 0.510\} = C
$$

$\therefore$ **Neuron C wins the competition.**

### **Step 3: Identify the Neighborhood**

In a 1-D line topology A—B—C, the immediate neighbors of C are: **B** (and **A** if 2-neighborhood radius, but here radius = 1).

So, the neurons to be updated are: **B** and **C** (BMU + immediate neighbor).

Neuron A is too far → $h_{A,C} = 0$.

### **Step 4: Compute the Update for BMU (Neuron C)**

Using the update rule:

$$
\mathbf{w}_C(1) = \mathbf{w}_C(0) + h_{C,C}(0) \cdot \bigl[\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_C(0)\bigr]
$$

$$
= \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.9 \end{bmatrix} + 0.5 \cdot \left(\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.9 \end{bmatrix}\right)
$$

$$
= \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.9 \end{bmatrix} + 0.5 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.9 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.25 \\ 0.05 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.75 \\ 0.95 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### **Step 5: Compute the Update for Neighbor (Neuron B)**

$$
\mathbf{w}_B(1) = \mathbf{w}_B(0) + h_{B,C}(0) \cdot \bigl[\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_B(0)\bigr]
$$

$$
= \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 \\ 0.4 \end{bmatrix} + 0.5 \cdot \left(\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 \\ 0.4 \end{bmatrix}\right)
$$

$$
= \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 \\ 0.4 \end{bmatrix} + 0.5 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 \\ 0.6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 \\ 0.4 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.10 \\ 0.30 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.90 \\ 0.70 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### **Step 6: Neuron A is NOT updated (Outside Neighborhood)**

$$
\mathbf{w}_A(1) = \mathbf{w}_A(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 \\ 0.6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### **Step 7: New Weight Matrix After One Iteration**

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{W}(1) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.20 & 0.90 & 0.75 \\ 0.60 & 0.70 & 0.95 \end{bmatrix}}
$$

### **Verification / Sanity Check**

Notice that $\mathbf{w}_C = (0.75, 0.95)^T$ moved **closer to** $\mathbf{x} = (1,1)^T$ (distance reduced from 0.510 to 0.250). ✓ The BMU has learned. Neighbor B also moved toward $\mathbf{x}$. Neuron A remained unchanged. ✓

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Kohonen SOM vs. Other ANN Paradigms

| Feature | Kohonen SOM | Backpropagation MLP | RBF Network |
|---|---|---|---|
| Learning type | Unsupervised | Supervised | Hybrid (unsup. centers + sup. weights) |
| Target output required? | **No** | Yes | Yes (for output layer only) |
| Network architecture | 2-D (or 1-D) lattice | Fully connected layered | Input → Hidden (Gaussian) → Output |
| Neuron update | Winner + neighbors | All neurons via gradient | All neurons (Gaussian weighted) |
| Activation function | None (winner-take-all) | Sigmoid / ReLU | Gaussian (hidden) + linear (output) |
| Learning rule | Competitive Hebbian | Error backpropagation | Hybrid |
| Primary use | Clustering, dimensionality reduction | Function approximation, classification | Function approximation, interpolation |
| Output | Discrete (winning neuron index) | Continuous vector | Continuous vector |
| Weight update neighborhood | Defined by $\sigma(t)$ on grid | None (global) | None (defined by Gaussian width $\beta$) |
| Convergence property | Topographic ordering of input | Local/global minima of MSE error | Local minima of MSE error |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse the Kohonen BMU determination with the RBF center selection. Remember: **Kohonen uses competitive winner selection (no Gaussian weighting during competition)**, while RBF uses **Gaussian activation values for ALL hidden neurons** simultaneously.
