# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a fuzzy relation. How does it differ from a crisp relation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **fuzzy relation** $R$ on two crisp sets $X$ and $Y$ is a fuzzy subset of the Cartesian product $X \times Y$. It is defined as a mapping:

$$R : X \times Y \rightarrow [0, 1]$$

where each ordered pair $(x, y)$ is assigned a membership grade $\mu_R(x, y) \in [0, 1]$ representing the *strength* of the relationship between $x$ and $y$.

**Difference from crisp relation:** A crisp relation maps $(x, y)$ to either **0 or 1** (binary), indicating presence or absence of the relationship in absolute terms. In contrast, a fuzzy relation maps to **any value in [0, 1]**, capturing the *degree* of relatedness. Thus, a fuzzy relation generalizes the crisp relation by replacing the Boolean truth values with a continuous truth scale. Every crisp relation is a special case of a fuzzy relation where all membership grades are restricted to $\{0, 1\}$. (3 marks)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`
**State and explain max-min composition of fuzzy relations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **max-min composition** is a method of combining two fuzzy relations $R(X \times Y)$ and $S(Y \times Z)$ to produce a new fuzzy relation $R \circ S$ on $X \times Z$. The membership function is defined as:

$$\mu_{R \circ S}(x, z) = \max_{y \in Y} \min[\mu_R(x, y),\ \mu_S(y, z)]$$

**Explanation:** For each pair $(x, z)$, we iterate over all possible intermediate elements $y \in Y$. We take the *minimum* of the memberships of $R(x, y)$ and $S(y, z)$ for each $y$ (this represents the weakest link in the chain), and then select the *maximum* of these minimums (this represents the strongest possible path). It is the most widely used composition technique in fuzzy inference systems, including the **Mamdani fuzzy model**. (3 marks)

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Define fuzzy equivalence relation and fuzzy partial order relation. List the properties required for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the fuzzy relation $R$ on $X = \{a, b, c\}$ as:

$$R = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.6 & 0.4 \\ 0.6 & 1 & 0.8 \\ 0.4 & 0.8 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Examine whether $R$ is a fuzzy equivalence relation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

##### Part (a) — Definitions and Properties

**Fuzzy Equivalence Relation:** A binary fuzzy relation $R$ on $X$ is a fuzzy equivalence relation if it satisfies three properties:
1. **Reflexivity:** $\mu_R(x, x) = 1$ for all $x \in X$.
2. **Symmetry:** $\mu_R(x, y) = \mu_R(y, x)$ for all $x, y \in X$.
3. **Max-Min Transitivity:** $\mu_R(x, z) \geq \max_y \min(\mu_R(x, y), \mu_R(y, z))$ for all $x, z \in X$.

(3.5 marks)

**Fuzzy Partial Order Relation:** A binary fuzzy relation $R$ on $X$ is a fuzzy partial order if:
1. **Reflexivity:** $\mu_R(x, x) = 1$ for all $x \in X$.
2. **Anti-symmetry:** If $\mu_R(x, y) > 0$ and $\mu_R(y, x) > 0$, then $x = y$.
3. **Max-Min Transitivity:** $\mu_R(x, z) \geq \max_y \min(\mu_R(x, y), \mu_R(y, z))$ for all $x, z \in X$.

(3.5 marks)

---

##### Part (b) — Verifying Fuzzy Equivalence for R

**Step 1: Check Reflexivity**

Diagonal entries: $\mu_R(a, a) = 1$, $\mu_R(b, b) = 1$, $\mu_R(c, c) = 1$. All equal to 1. **Reflexivity holds.** ✓ (1 mark)

**Step 2: Check Symmetry**

Comparing symmetric pairs:

$\mu_R(a, b) = 0.6 = \mu_R(b, a)$ ✓
$\mu_R(a, c) = 0.4 = \mu_R(c, a)$ ✓
$\mu_R(b, c) = 0.8 = \mu_R(c, b)$ ✓

**Symmetry holds.** ✓ (1.5 marks)

**Step 3: Check Max-Min Transitivity**

Test for $(x, z) = (a, b)$: We compute $\max_y \min(\mu_R(a, y), \mu_R(y, b))$ over $y \in \{a, b, c\}$.

- $y = a$: $\min(1, 0.6) = 0.6$
- $y = b$: $\min(0.6, 1) = 0.6$
- $y = c$: $\min(0.4, 0.8) = 0.4$

Maximum = $0.6$. We need $\mu_R(a, b) = 0.6 \geq 0.6$ ✓ (1 mark)

Test for $(x, z) = (a, c)$: Compute $\max_y \min(\mu_R(a, y), \mu_R(y, c))$.

- $y = a$: $\min(1, 0.4) = 0.4$
- $y = b$: $\min(0.6, 0.8) = 0.6$
- $y = c$: $\min(0.4, 1) = 0.4$

Maximum = $0.6$. We need $\mu_R(a, c) = 0.4 \geq 0.6$? **No, $0.4 < 0.6$** ✗ (1.5 marks)

**Conclusion:** $R$ fails max-min transitivity. Hence, $R$ is **NOT a fuzzy equivalence relation** (it is a fuzzy tolerance relation). (2 marks for the conclusion and naming)

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – June 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of fuzzy relation composition. Discuss max-min and max-product composition with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given $R$ and $S$ as:

$$R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & 0.5 \\ 0.7 & 0.3 \end{bmatrix},\quad S = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.4 \\ 0.9 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute $R \circ S$ using both max-min and max-product composition. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

##### Part (a) — Concept and Types of Composition

**Concept:** Fuzzy relation composition is the operation of combining two fuzzy relations $R(X \times Y)$ and $S(Y \times Z)$ to produce a derived relation on $X \times Z$. It generalizes the concept of relational composition from crisp set theory to the fuzzy domain. The composition captures the "strength" of indirect connection between $x$ and $z$ through any intermediate $y$. (2 marks)

**Max-Min Composition:**

$$\mu_{R \circ S}(x, z) = \max_{y \in Y} \min[\mu_R(x, y), \mu_S(y, z)]$$

*Example:* If $R$ represents "x is close to y" and $S$ represents "y is similar to z", then max-min composition gives the "x is close to similar to z" relation. The min operation finds the weakest link in the chain; the max selects the best path. It is associative, monotone, and the most commonly used form. (2.5 marks)

**Max-Product Composition:**

$$\mu_{R \diamond S}(x, z) = \max_{y \in Y} [\mu_R(x, y) \cdot \mu_S(y, z)]$$

*Example:* Used in weighted scoring systems. The product operation penalizes low membership values more strongly than min does. Useful when the strengths of relationships are proportional rather than independent. (2.5 marks)

---

##### Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**Step 1: Compute Max-Min Composition $R \circ S$**

Compute $\mu_{R \circ S}(1, 1)$: $\max_y \min(\mu_R(1, y), \mu_S(y, 1))$

- $y = 1$: $\min(0.2, 0.6) = 0.2$
- $y = 2$: $\min(0.5, 0.9) = 0.5$

Maximum = **0.5** (0.5 marks)

Compute $\mu_{R \circ S}(1, 2)$:

- $y = 1$: $\min(0.2, 0.4) = 0.2$
- $y = 2$: $\min(0.5, 0.1) = 0.1$

Maximum = **0.2** (0.5 marks)

Compute $\mu_{R \circ S}(2, 1)$:

- $y = 1$: $\min(0.7, 0.6) = 0.6$
- $y = 2$: $\min(0.3, 0.9) = 0.3$

Maximum = **0.6** (0.5 marks)

Compute $\mu_{R \circ S}(2, 2)$:

- $y = 1$: $\min(0.7, 0.4) = 0.4$
- $y = 2$: $\min(0.3, 0.1) = 0.1$

Maximum = **0.4** (0.5 marks)

Therefore:

$$R \circ S = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.2 \\ 0.6 & 0.4 \end{bmatrix}\quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

**Step 2: Compute Max-Product Composition $R \diamond S$**

Compute $\mu_{R \diamond S}(1, 1)$: $\max_y [\mu_R(1, y) \cdot \mu_S(y, 1)]$

- $y = 1$: $0.2 \times 0.6 = 0.12$
- $y = 2$: $0.5 \times 0.9 = 0.45$

Maximum = **0.45** (0.5 marks)

Compute $\mu_{R \diamond S}(1, 2)$:

- $y = 1$: $0.2 \times 0.4 = 0.08$
- $y = 2$: $0.5 \times 0.1 = 0.05$

Maximum = **0.08** (0.5 marks)

Compute $\mu_{R \diamond S}(2, 1)$:

- $y = 1$: $0.7 \times 0.6 = 0.42$
- $y = 2$: $0.3 \times 0.9 = 0.27$

Maximum = **0.42** (0.5 marks)

Compute $\mu_{R \diamond S}(2, 2)$:

- $y = 1$: $0.7 \times 0.4 = 0.28$
- $y = 2$: $0.3 \times 0.1 = 0.03$

Maximum = **0.28** (0.5 marks)

Therefore:

$$R \diamond S = \begin{bmatrix} 0.45 & 0.08 \\ 0.42 & 0.28 \end{bmatrix}\quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Transitivity Traps:** Students often forget that fuzzy transitivity uses the **max-min** operator, NOT crisp transitivity. A common mistake is checking only two intermediate values instead of iterating over *all* elements of the set. This leads to incomplete checks and partial marks. (Penalty: 3-4 marks lost)
> 2. **Composition Confusion:** Max-Min and Max-Product are frequently confused. Remember: **Min** takes the **smaller** of the two values; **Product** **multiplies** them. Always re-read the question carefully.
> 3. **Reflexivity Oversights:** Students sometimes claim reflexivity by just glancing at the matrix. **Always explicitly write down** all three diagonal entries and verify each equals 1.
> 4. **Symmetry Direction:** Symmetric means $\mu_R(x, y) = \mu_R(y, x)$ — i.e., the matrix must be symmetric about the main diagonal. A quick visual check works, but write it down for full marks.
> 5. **Not Showing All Steps:** KTU examiners reward *stepwise work*. Always show the computation of $\min$ (or product) for *every* intermediate $y$, then take the max. Skipping intermediate steps costs 2-3 marks per question.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Fuzzy Relation** is a fuzzy subset of $X \times Y$, mapping to $[0, 1]$.
- A **crisp relation** is a special case where values are restricted to $\{0, 1\}$.
- **Union** uses $\max$, **Intersection** uses $\min$, **Complement** uses $1 - \mu$.
- **Projection** onto $X$: $\mu_{\pi_X}(x) = \max_y \mu_R(x, y)$.
- **Cylindrical Extension** of a set $A$: $\mu_{\text{cyl}(A)}(x, y) = \mu_A(x)$.
- **Reflexivity:** all diagonal entries equal **1**.
- **Symmetry:** matrix is symmetric about the main diagonal.
- **Anti-symmetry:** $\mu_R(x, y) > 0$ and $\mu_R(y, x) > 0 \Rightarrow x = y$.
- **Max-Min Transitivity:** $\mu_R(x, z) \geq \max_y \min[\mu_R(x, y), \mu_R(y, z)]$ — the **KEEPER formula**.
- **Fuzzy Equivalence Relation** = Reflexive + Symmetric + Max-Min Transitive.
- **Fuzzy Tolerance Relation** = Reflexive + Symmetric (no transitivity needed).
- **Fuzzy Partial Order** = Reflexive + Anti-symmetric + Transitive.
- **Max-Min Composition:** $\max_y \min[\mu_R(x, y), \mu_S(y, z)]$ — used in Mamdani systems.
- **Max-Product Composition:** $\max_y [\mu_R(x, y) \cdot \mu_S(y, z)]$ — used in Sugeno and weighted scoring.
- **Composition associativity:** $(R \circ S) \circ T = R \circ (S \circ T)$ holds for max-min.
- **Composition monotonicity:** If $R_1 \subseteq R_2$, then $R_1 \circ S \subseteq R_2 \circ S$.
- **Real-world applications:** medical diagnosis, fuzzy controllers, IR systems, decision support, pattern recognition.
- **The membership values are always in $[0, 1]$** — exceeding this range is a common error in board exams.
- **Output dimension of $R \circ S$** is $(\text{rows of } R) \times (\text{columns of } S)$ — verify dimensions first before computing.
- **Intersection of two equivalence relations is an equivalence relation**; union is generally not.
- **Cylindrical extension is the inverse of projection** — a key duality result.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Min-Max Composition and Features of Membership Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT411 - SOFT COMPUTING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Min-Max Composition and Features of Membership Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Min-Max Composition — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Min-Max Composition (Sup-Min Composition)** is a binary operation used to combine two **fuzzy relations** into a single fuzzy relation. It is the workhorse operator behind Fuzzy Relational Inference, Mamdani-style controllers, and fuzzy database query processing.

Formally, let $R(x, y)$ be a fuzzy relation on the Cartesian product $X \times Y$ and $S(y, z)$ be a fuzzy relation on $Y \times Z$. The **min-max composition** of $R$ and $S$, denoted $R \circ S$, is a fuzzy relation on $X \times Z$ defined pointwise as:

$$
T(x, z) = (R \circ S)(x, z) = \max_{y \in Y} \, \min \, [R(x, y), \, S(y, z)]
$$

The inner **min** acts as a t-norm (logical AND), while the outer **max** acts as an s-norm (logical OR). The intermediate universe $Y$ is therefore called the *composition channel*.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Information Bridge"**
>
> Imagine $R$ as a *strength chart* of friendships between cities on Continent A and cities on Continent B, and $S$ as the *trade-route chart* between Continent B and Continent C. To estimate how strongly a city in A connects to a city in C, you would:
> 1. For every common city $y$ on Continent B, take the *weaker* of the two links (the **min** — a chain is only as strong as its weakest link).
> 2. Across all possible intermediate cities $y$, keep the *strongest* such value (the **max** — the best possible routing).
>
> This "weakest-link along the path, strongest-path overall" idea is exactly what min-max composition computes.

### 1.3 Membership Functions — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Membership Function** $\mu_A(x): X \rightarrow [0, 1]$ assigns to every element $x$ of the universe of discourse $X$ a *degree of belonging* to the fuzzy set $A$. The value $\mu_A(x) = 1$ denotes full membership, $\mu_A(x) = 0$ denotes no membership, and intermediate values represent graded belonging.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy for Membership Functions

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Twilight Scale"**
>
> Classical (Crisp) sets are like a strict door policy: you are either *inside* (1) or *outside* (0). A membership function is like a *gradient welcome mat* — at noon (sun directly overhead) you fully belong to the "Bright" set (membership 1); at midnight you are fully "Dark" (0); and during the *civil twilight* you might have membership 0.4 in "Bright" and 0.6 in "Dim", expressing the *gradual* transition that crisp mathematics cannot capture.

### 1.5 Standard Shapes of Membership Functions

| Shape | Typical Domain | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Triangular** | $x \in [a, c]$ | Fast, real-time control (low CPU cost) |
| **Trapezoidal** | $x \in [a, d]$ | "Acceptable range" specifications |
| **Gaussian** | $x \in (-\infty, \infty)$ | Sensor noise, smooth class boundaries |
| **Sigmoidal** | $x \in (-\infty, \infty)$ | Activation curves, decision boundaries |
| **Singleton (Crisp Spike)** | $x = x_0$ | Hybrid fuzzy-crisp rules |
| **Generalized Bell (G-Bell)** | $x \in (-\infty, \infty)$ | Adaptive neural-fuzzy systems (ANFIS) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Min-Max Composition — Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1 — Input Preparation:** Obtain two fuzzy relations $R$ of dimension $\vert X \vert \times \vert Y \vert$ and $S$ of dimension $\vert Y \vert \times \vert Z \vert$. The inner dimension $\vert Y \vert$ must match for composition to be defined.
- **Step 2 — Pairwise Min:** For a fixed pair $(x, z)$, scan the row $R(x, \cdot)$ against the column $S(\cdot, z)$. Compute $\min[R(x,y), S(y,z)]$ for every $y \in Y$. This represents the *joint plausibility* of the conjunction.
- **Step 3 — Aggregation by Max:** Aggregate the $\vert Y \vert$ min-values using the $\max$ operator. This corresponds to existential quantification over the bridging variable $y$.
- **Step 4 — Output Assembly:** The resulting $\max$ value is the membership degree $\mu_{R \circ S}(x, z)$ of the composed relation at $(x, z)$.
- **Step 5 — Matrix Generalisation:** Repeat Steps 2–4 for every $(x, z)$ pair to fill the $\vert X \vert \times \vert Z \vert$ output matrix $T$.

### 2.2 Algebraic and Engineering Properties

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the min–max choice dominate fuzzy systems?**
>
> 1. **Idempotence under the lattice $[0,1]$:** Min and max are the *greatest* t-norm and *least* s-norm respectively. They are the *pointwise* lattice operations of the underlying Boolean logic, which makes min-max composition the most natural generalisation of crisp relational composition.
> 2. **Computational Simplicity:** Both operators are $O(n)$ per element, making real-time fuzzy controllers feasible on microcontrollers.
> 3. **Interpretability:** Engineers can read off the strongest inference path from the intermediate universe, aiding in rule-base debugging.
> 4. **Associativity:** $(R \circ S) \circ T = R \circ (S \circ T)$ — essential for chaining multi-stage fuzzy rules.

### 2.3 Features of Membership Functions (Core, Support, Boundary, etc.)

A membership function $\mu_A(x)$ is dissected by the following structural features. These features are *guaranteed exam questions* in KTU 2024 scheme papers.

- **Core** ($C(A)$): The crisp set of all points in $X$ where $\mu_A(x) = 1$. Represents *full* belonging.
- **Support** ($S(A)$): The crisp set of all points where $\mu_A(x) > 0$. Defines the *extent* of the fuzzy set.
- **Boundary** ($B(A)$): The crisp set of points where $0 < \mu_A(x) < 1$. Represents *partial* belonging — the *fuzzy* zone.
- **Height** ($h(A)$): $\sup_{x \in X} \mu_A(x)$. The *peak* value of the set.
- **Normal Set:** A fuzzy set is **normal** if $h(A) = 1$. Most KTU textbook examples are normalised.
- **Subnormal Set:** A fuzzy set where $h(A) < 1$. Sometimes used to model *partial* prototypes.
- **Crossover Point:** An $x$ where $\mu_A(x) = 0.5$. The point of *maximum fuzziness* in a symmetric set.
- **Fuzziness ($F(A)$):** A scalar measure of the ambiguity. Klir's measure: $F(A) = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} T(\mu_A(x_i), 1 - \mu_A(x_i))$, with $T$ a t-norm.
- **Convexity:** A fuzzy set is **convex** if for all $x_1 \le x_2 \le x_3$, $\mu_A(x_2) \ge \min[\mu_A(x_1), \mu_A(x_3)]$. Convex fuzzy sets correspond to *nested interval families*.
- **Symmetry:** $\mu_A(c + d) = \mu_A(c - d)$ for some centre $c$. Useful in *type-2* and *symmetric membership* derivations.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NOTE TO STUDENT:** The table below is your one-stop reference for Module-3 numerical problems. Memorise the first four rows — they appear in nearly every KTU question paper.

| # | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Boundary / Constraint |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Min-Max Composition | $T(x,z) = \max_{y} \min [R(x,y), S(y,z)]$ | $\dim(Y)$ must match |
| 2 | Core | $C(A) = \{ x \mid \mu_A(x) = 1 \}$ | $C(A) \subseteq S(A)$ |
| 3 | Support | $S(A) = \{ x \mid \mu_A(x) > 0 \}$ | Always non-empty if $A \neq \emptyset$ |
| 4 | Boundary | $B(A) = \{ x \mid 0 < \mu_A(x) < 1 \}$ | $B(A) = S(A) \setminus C(A)$ |
| 5 | Height | $h(A) = \sup_{x \in X} \mu_A(x)$ | $0 \le h(A) \le 1$ |
| 6 | Normality | $h(A) = 1$ | Equivalent to "fully realised" set |
| 7 | Triangular M.F. | $\mu(x) = \max[0, \min[(x-a)/(b-a), (c-x)/(c-b)]]$ | $a \le b \le c$ |
| 8 | Trapezoidal M.F. | $\mu(x) = \max[0, \min[(x-a)/(b-a), 1, (d-x)/(d-c)]]$ | $a \le b \le c \le d$ |
| 9 | Gaussian M.F. | $\mu(x) = \exp[-0.5 ((x-c)/\sigma)^2]$ | $\sigma > 0$ |
| 10 | Sigmoidal M.F. | $\mu(x) = 1 / (1 + e^{-a(x-c)})$ | $a$ controls slope |
| 11 | Fuzziness (Klir) | $F(A) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i} \mu_A(x_i) [1 - \mu_A(x_i)]$ | $0 \le F(A) \le 0.25$ |
| 12 | Convexity | $\mu_A(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda)x_2) \ge \min[\mu_A(x_1), \mu_A(x_2)]$ | $\lambda \in [0,1]$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mamdani Fuzzy Controllers:** Min is used for fuzzification AND-ing of rule antecedents, while max aggregates the firing strengths of multiple rules in the *consequent* aggregation step.
- **Medical Decision Support:** Membership features (core, support, crossover) allow clinicians to map "mild / moderate / severe" zones of, e.g., blood pressure.
- **Industrial Quality Control:** Trapezoidal and Gaussian MFs are used to define "Acceptable / Marginal / Reject" tolerance bands for manufactured dimensions.
- **Image Processing:** Fuzzy relations on pixel neighbourhoods are composed with min-max to perform edge detection and noise suppression.
- **Database Querying:** SQL-like fuzzy queries are evaluated using min-max composition over fuzzy relations.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example of Min-Max Composition (Matrix Form)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Given two fuzzy relations $R$ on $X \times Y$ and $S$ on $Y \times Z$ (where $X = \{x_1, x_2\}$, $Y = \{y_1, y_2, y_3\}$, $Z = \{z_1, z_2\}$), compute the min-max composition $T = R \circ S$ and identify the core, support, and height of a derived fuzzy set.

**Step 1 — State the Input Relations**

The relation $R$ is a $2 \times 3$ matrix:

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix}
\mu_R(x_1, y_1) & \mu_R(x_1, y_2) & \mu_R(x_1, y_3) \\
\mu_R(x_2, y_1) & \mu_R(x_2, y_2) & \mu_R(x_2, y_3)
\end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix}
0.6 & 0.3 & 0.2 \\
0.1 & 0.7 & 0.5
\end{bmatrix}
$$

The relation $S$ is a $3 \times 2$ matrix:

$$
S = \begin{bmatrix}
\mu_S(y_1, z_1) & \mu_S(y_1, z_2) \\
\mu_S(y_2, z_1) & \mu_S(y_2, z_2) \\
\mu_S(y_3, z_1) & \mu_S(y_3, z_2)
\end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix}
0.9 & 0.5 \\
0.4 & 0.8 \\
0.6 & 0.3
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine Output Dimensions**

Since $R$ is $2 \times 3$ and $S$ is $3 \times 2$, the composition $T = R \circ S$ is a $2 \times 2$ matrix on $X \times Z$. We now compute each of the four cells.

**Step 3 — Compute $T(x_1, z_1)$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(x_1, z_1) &= \max_{y \in Y} \, \min \, [R(x_1, y), \, S(y, z_1)] \\
&= \max \, \big[ \min(0.6, 0.9), \; \min(0.3, 0.4), \; \min(0.2, 0.6) \big] \\
&= \max \, [0.6, \, 0.3, \, 0.2] \\
&= 0.6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $T(x_1, z_2)$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(x_1, z_2) &= \max_{y \in Y} \, \min \, [R(x_1, y), \, S(y, z_2)] \\
&= \max \, \big[ \min(0.6, 0.5), \; \min(0.3, 0.8), \; \min(0.2, 0.3) \big] \\
&= \max \, [0.5, \, 0.3, \, 0.2] \\
&= 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $T(x_2, z_1)$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(x_2, z_1) &= \max_{y \in Y} \, \min \, [R(x_2, y), \, S(y, z_1)] \\
&= \max \, \big[ \min(0.1, 0.9), \; \min(0.7, 0.4), \; \min(0.5, 0.6) \big] \\
&= \max \, [0.1, \, 0.4, \, 0.5] \\
&= 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute $T(x_2, z_2)$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(x_2, z_2) &= \max_{y \in Y} \, \min \, [R(x_2, y), \, S(y, z_2)] \\
&= \max \, \big[ \min(0.1, 0.5), \; \min(0.7, 0.8), \; \min(0.5, 0.3) \big] \\
&= \max \, [0.1, \, 0.7, \, 0.3] \\
&= 0.7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Assemble the Composed Relation**

$$
T = R \circ S = \begin{bmatrix}
0.6 & 0.5 \\
0.5 & 0.7
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 8 — Derive Features of a Fuzzy Set Induced by $T$**

Treating the *first row* of $T$ as a fuzzy set $A$ on $Z = \{z_1, z_2\}$: $A = \{ (z_1, 0.6), (z_2, 0.5) \}$.

- **Core** $C(A) = \{ z_1, z_2 \}$ (both reach 1? *No* — adjusted) — Corrected: since max value is $0.6 < 1$, $C(A) = \emptyset$ (set is *subnormal*).
- **Support** $S(A) = \{ z_1, z_2 \}$ (both values are strictly positive).
- **Boundary** $B(A) = \{ z_1, z_2 \}$ (both lie strictly in $(0, 1)$).
- **Height** $h(A) = \max(0.6, 0.5) = 0.6$.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Boundary Conditions for Membership Features

| Feature | Symbol | Definition | Example (Triangular M.F., $a=0, b=5, c=10$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Core | $C(A)$ | $\{ x \mid \mu_A(x) = 1 \}$ | $C(A) = \{5\}$ (single peak) |
| Support | $S(A)$ | $\{ x \mid \mu_A(x) > 0 \}$ | $S(A) = (0, 10)$ (open interval) |
| Boundary | $B(A)$ | $S(A) \setminus C(A)$ | $B(A) = (0, 5) \cup (5, 10)$ |
| Height | $h(A)$ | $\sup \mu_A(x)$ | $h(A) = 1$ at $x = 5$ |
| Normality | $h(A) = 1$ | Boolean predicate | TRUE (set is normal) |
| Crossover Point | $x_{0.5}$ | $\mu_A(x_{0.5}) = 0.5$ | $x_{0.5} = 2.5$ and $x_{0.5} = 7.5$ |
| Convex | $\mu_A(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda)x_2) \ge \min[\mu_A(x_1), \mu_A(x_2)]$ | Geometric predicate | TRUE (triangle is convex) |
| Fuzziness | $F(A)$ | $\frac{1}{n}\sum \mu_A(x_i)[1-\mu_A(x_i)]$ | $F(A) \approx 0.166$ (approx.) |

### 3.3 Proof Sketch — Associativity of Min-Max Composition

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem:** For fuzzy relations $R$ on $X \times Y$, $S$ on $Y \times Z$, and $T$ on $Z \times W$, we have $(R \circ S) \circ T = R \circ (S \circ T)$.

**Proof Outline:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\big[ (R \circ S) \circ T \big](x, w) &= \max_{z} \min \big[ (R \circ S)(x, z), \, T(z, w) \big] \\
&= \max_{z} \min \big[ \max_{y} \min[R(x,y), S(y,z)], \, T(z,w) \big] \\
&= \max_{z} \max_{y} \min \big[ R(x,y), \, S(y,z), \, T(z,w) \big] \quad \text{(distributivity of min over max)} \\
&= \max_{y} \max_{z} \min \big[ R(x,y), \, S(y,z), \, T(z,w) \big] \quad \text{(commutativity of max)} \\
&= \max_{y} \min \big[ R(x,y), \, \max_{z} \min [S(y,z), T(z,w)] \big] \\
&= \big[ R \circ (S \circ T) \big](x, w) \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Min-Max Composition

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below maps the data-flow of a min-max composition engine. Each block represents a mathematical operation; arrows represent the flow of fuzzy membership values.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuzzy Relation R on X x Y] --> B[Row Extractor: select row R of x]
    C[Fuzzy Relation S on Y x Z] --> D[Column Extractor: select column S of z]
    B --> E[Pairwise Min Operator]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Vector of min-values over Y]
    F --> G[Max Aggregator over Y]
    G --> H[Scalar Output T of x comma z]
    H --> I[Assemble into Composed Matrix R circle S]
    I --> J{Output Validation: dims match and values in 0 to 1}
    J -- Valid --> K[Return T]
    J -- Invalid --> L[Raise Dimension Mismatch Error]
```

### 4.2 M.F. Feature Decomposition Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> The following block diagram illustrates how a single membership function is decomposed into its core, support, and boundary regions — a recurring KTU exam sketch question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MF[Input Membership Function mu of A] --> C[Identify Core: mu equals 1]
    MF --> S[Identify Support: mu greater than 0]
    MF --> B[Derive Boundary: Support minus Core]
    C --> OUT1[Core C of A]
    S --> OUT2[Support S of A]
    B --> OUT3[Boundary B of A]
    MF --> H[Compute Height h of A]
    H --> OUT4[Height h of A]
    MF --> X[Find Crossover Point mu equals 0.5]
    X --> OUT5[Crossover x subscript 0.5]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Membership Function Shape Classification

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram classifies membership functions by shape — a frequently tested conceptual topic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Membership Function Classification] --> B[Piecewise Linear]
    A --> C[Smooth Continuous]
    A --> D[Singleton Discrete]
    B --> B1[Triangular]
    B --> B2[Trapezoidal]
    B --> B3[Linear Shoulder]
    C --> C1[Gaussian]
    C --> C2[Sigmoidal]

    D --> D1[Singleton Spike at x0]
```
