- Offspring 1: `100100` (segment 110 swapped with 101)
- Offspring 2: `011010` (the other direction)

**(iii) Uniform Crossover (2 marks):**
For each gene, a coin-flip (probability 0.5) decides which parent contributes the gene.

*Example:* Parents: `10110` and `01101`.
- Mask: `01101`
- Offspring: `0 0 1 1 0` = `00110` (first gene from P1, second from P1, etc.)

---

**Part (b) — Worked Numerical [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Decode (1 mark):**
Each 3-bit chromosome decodes to $x \in [0, 7]$.

**Step 2 — Evaluate fitness $f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 4x + 12$ (3 marks):**

| Individual | Chromosome | $x$ | $f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 4x + 12$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `101` | 5 | $125 - 150 + 20 + 12 = 7$ |
| 2 | `011` | 3 | $27 - 54 + 12 + 12 = -3$ |
| 3 | `110` | 6 | $216 - 216 + 24 + 12 = 36$ |
| 4 | `001` | 1 | $1 - 6 + 4 + 12 = 11$ |

**Step 3 — Transform to non-negative fitness (1 mark):**
Since one value is negative, apply $f'(x) = f(x) + C$ where $C = 3$ (to make the minimum zero):
- $f_1' = 10$, $f_2' = 0$, $f_3' = 39$, $f_4' = 14$. Total = 63.

**Step 4 — Selection Probability (2 marks):**

$$p_i = \frac{f_i'}{\sum f_j'}$$

- $p_1 = 10/63 \approx 0.1587$
- $p_2 = 0/63 = 0.0000$
- $p_3 = 39/63 \approx 0.6190$
- $p_4 = 14/63 \approx 0.2222$

**Inference (KTU Board Insight):** Individual 3 (`110`, $x=6$) is the strongest candidate with ~62% selection probability, while Individual 2 (`011`, $x=3$) is the weakest and is likely to be eliminated in the next generation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes (Read Carefully):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert the objective function to non-negative fitness.** If the raw fitness is negative, roulette wheel selection breaks down. Always add a constant $C$ or use a transformation like $f' = 1/(1 + g)$ for minimization. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
>
> 2. **Wrong decoding direction.** Binary-to-integer decoding uses LSB-on-right. Students often write MSB-on-right and lose 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Confusing expected count $E_i = N \cdot p_i$ with selection probability $p_i$.** These are different quantities; $E_i$ tells how many *copies* will enter the mating pool.
>
> 4. **Crossover position off-by-one error.** The crossover *point* lies *between* two bits, not on a bit. Specifying "cut at position 3" means the first 3 bits stay and the rest are swapped.
>
> 5. **Omitting $p_m$ value in mutation step.** Always state the mutation probability and the specific bit that flipped.
>
> 6. **Not comparing new generation fitness with old.** The whole point of a GA iteration is to show *improvement*. Conclude with $\bar{f}_{\text{new}} > \bar{f}_{\text{old}}$ to demonstrate progress.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Genetic Algorithms: Basic Concepts"**

- [x] **GA Definition:** A metaheuristic, population-based, search optimization algorithm inspired by natural selection and genetics.
- [x] **Inventors:** John Henry Holland (1975); popularized by David E. Goldberg (1989).
- [x] **Five Phases:** Initialize → Evaluate → Select → Crossover → Mutate → Replace.
- [x] **Encoding Types:** Binary, Real-valued, Integer/Permutation, Tree-based.
- [x] **Roulette Wheel Formula:** $p_i = f_i / \sum_j f_j$.
- [x] **Expected Count Formula:** $E_i = f_i / \bar{f}$.
- [x] **Tournament Selection:** Pick $k$ random, return the fittest.
- [x] **Single-point Crossover:** One cut, swap tails.
- [x] **Two-point Crossover:** Two cuts, swap middle.
- [x] **Uniform Crossover:** Each gene from either parent with $p = 0.5$.
- [x] **Bit-flip Mutation Rate:** $p_m \approx 1/\ell$ (Holland's recommendation).
- [x] **Crossover Rate Range:** $p_c \in [0.6, 0.9]$.
- [x] **Elitism:** Copy best $e$ individuals unchanged to the next generation.
- [x] **Schema Theorem (Holland):** Short, low-order, above-average schemata grow exponentially across generations.
- [x] **Termination Criteria:** Max generations, target fitness, or convergence.
- [x] **GA Advantages:** Population-based (parallel), gradient-free, finds global optima.
- [x] **GA Disadvantages:** No guarantee of optimality, computationally expensive, parameter tuning required.
- [x] **Applications:** VLSI design, job-shop scheduling, financial modeling, robotics, bioinformatics, antenna design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Board Advice:** For any 14-mark GA question, structure your answer as **(1) Diagram (3 marks) + (2) Operator Explanation (4 marks) + (3) Numerical / Worked Example (7 marks)**. This three-part structure matches the KTU mark distribution and is what senior examiners expect to see.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Encoding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT411 - SOFT COMPUTING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Encoding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Genetic Algorithms (GAs)** and evolutionary computing, **Encoding** (also called **Representation** or **Chromosome Encoding**) is the process of mapping the decision variables (phenotype space) of a candidate solution into a chromosome structure (genotype space) that can be processed by genetic operators such as selection, crossover, and mutation.

Formally, an encoding is a bijective (or near-bijective) mapping function:

$$
E : \mathcal{S}_p \rightarrow \mathcal{S}_g
$$

where $\mathcal{S}_p$ represents the **phenotype space** (the actual solution space) and $\mathcal{S}_g$ represents the **genotype space** (the encoded chromosome space). The inverse decoder is denoted $E^{-1}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Encoding is a foundational step that directly impacts the **convergence speed**, **search space coverage**, and **operator design** of any Genetic Algorithm. Incorrect choice of encoding leads to premature convergence or invalid offspring.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are a **travel agent** trying to find the best tour route across 10 cities. The *actual tour* (e.g., City 3 → City 7 → City 1 → ... ) is the **phenotype** — the real-world solution. However, computers cannot directly "see" tours. So, you **encode** each city as a number (1, 2, ..., 10) and represent the entire tour as a sequence of numbers like `[3, 7, 1, 5, 9, 2, 8, 4, 6, 10]`. This encoded list is the **chromosome** (genotype).

Just as DNA in biology encodes the blueprint of an organism, this numerical chromosome encodes the candidate solution. The GA then evolves these strings using genetic operations, and finally, you **decode** the best chromosome back to a meaningful tour.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Encoding is the *language* spoken by the GA. Choosing the right language makes the problem easier to solve; the wrong language can make even simple problems intractable.

## 1.3 Significance in Soft Computing

- It defines the **search space geometry**.
- It determines the **granularity** of the solution.
- It constrains which **crossover/mutation operators** are valid.
- It affects the **No Free Lunch** principle — there is no universal "best" encoding.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Encoding Schemes

Genetic Algorithms support multiple encoding schemes, each suited to specific problem classes.

### 2.1.1 Binary Encoding

Each chromosome is a fixed-length string of **bits** (0s and 1s).

- **Example:** A chromosome of length 8 for a variable with range $[-5, 5]$: `01101001`
- **Decoding formula:**

$$
x = L + \frac{(U - L)}{2^{n} - 1} \cdot d
$$

where $L$ is the lower bound, $U$ is the upper bound, $n$ is the bit length, and $d$ is the decimal value of the binary string.

- **Pros:** Simple, theoretically well-analyzed, easy to apply bit-flip mutation.
- **Cons:** Hamming cliff problem (e.g., 0111 → 1000 changes all bits for adjacent values), poor for continuous optimization.

### 2.1.2 Real-Valued (Floating-Point) Encoding

Each gene is a real number drawn from a bounded interval.

- **Example:** `Chromosome = [3.14, -1.27, 8.65, 0.42]`
- **Use case:** Continuous optimization problems (neural network weights, engineering design).
- **Operators:** Blend crossover (BLX-α), simulated binary crossover (SBX), polynomial mutation.

### 2.1.3 Integer / Discrete Encoding

Each gene takes an integer value from a finite set.

- **Example:** `Chromosome = [2, 5, 1, 4, 3]`
- **Use case:** Combinatorial problems where genes represent discrete choices (e.g., number of machines, categorical variables).

### 2.1.4 Permutation Encoding

Each chromosome is a permutation of $N$ distinct integers.

- **Example for N=6:** `[3, 1, 5, 2, 6, 4]`
- **Use case:** **Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP)**, scheduling, vehicle routing.
- **Special operators:** Order Crossover (OX), Partially Mapped Crossover (PMX), cycle crossover.

### 2.1.5 Tree-Based Encoding

Chromosomes are tree structures used in **Genetic Programming (GP)**.

- **Example:** A mathematical expression represented as an expression tree.
- **Use case:** Symbolic regression, automatic program synthesis.

## 2.2 Principles for Selecting an Encoding

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following guiding principles:

1. **Principle of Meaningful Building Blocks:** Short, low-order schemata should correspond to meaningful components of the solution.
2. **Principle of Minimal Alphabets:** Use the smallest alphabet that permits a natural expression of the problem (Goldberg's principle).
3. **Closedness under Genetic Operators:** Crossover/mutation should produce valid chromosomes.
4. **No Redundancy:** Each genotype should map to exactly one phenotype (non-redundant encoding preferred).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Binary Decoding | $x = L + \frac{(U - L)}{2^{n} - 1} \cdot d$ | Maps binary to real range $[L, U]$ |
| Precision (Binary) | $\Delta x = \frac{U - L}{2^{n} - 1}$ | Resolution per bit increment |
| Bits Required | $n = \lceil \log_2 \left( \frac{U - L}{\Delta x} \cdot 1 + 1 \right) \rceil$ | Bits to achieve precision $\Delta x$ |
| Population Diversity | $D = \frac{1}{N \cdot L} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{L} d(g_{ij}, \bar{g_j})$ | Average Hamming/real distance |
| Schema Order | $o(H)$ | Number of fixed positions in schema |
| Schema Length | $\delta(H)$ | Distance between first and last fixed bit |
| Gray Code Conversion | $g_k = b_k \oplus b_{k+1}$ | $k$-th Gray bit, $b$ = binary bit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Never use the symbol `|` inside KTU tables** — it breaks the markdown table structure. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in equations instead.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing:** Encoding tolerances and design parameters for optimization of mechanical parts.
- **Telecommunications:** Encoding channel allocations in network routing problems.
- **Bioinformatics:** Permutation encoding for DNA sequence alignment.
- **Robotics & Control:** Real-valued encoding of PID controller gains and neural network weights.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Binary Decoding for a Continuous Variable

**Problem:** Encode the variable $x \in [-5, 5]$ using 8-bit binary chromosomes. Given the chromosome `01101001`, decode it back to a real value.

### Step 1 — Convert the binary string to its decimal equivalent

The 8-bit string `01101001` represents:

$$
d = 0 \cdot 2^7 + 1 \cdot 2^6 + 1 \cdot 2^5 + 0 \cdot 2^4 + 1 \cdot 2^3 + 0 \cdot 2^2 + 0 \cdot 2^1 + 1 \cdot 2^0
$$

$$
d = 0 + 64 + 32 + 0 + 8 + 0 + 0 + 1 = 105
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the decoding formula

Using $L = -5$, $U = 5$, $n = 8$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= L + \frac{(U - L)}{2^{n} - 1} \cdot d \\
x &= -5 + \frac{(5 - (-5))}{2^{8} - 1} \cdot 105 \\
x &= -5 + \frac{10}{255} \cdot 105 \\
x &= -5 + \frac{1050}{255} \\
x &= -5 + 4.1176 \\
x &= -0.8824
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Verify the precision

$$
\Delta x = \frac{U - L}{2^{n} - 1} = \frac{10}{255} \approx 0.0392
$$

Thus, the encoded chromosome represents the value $x \approx -0.8824$ with a precision of $0.0392$.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Gray Code Conversion

**Problem:** Convert the binary value `01101001` to Gray code to mitigate the Hamming cliff problem.

### Step-by-Step Conversion

Let $b_7 b_6 b_5 b_4 b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0 = 0\,1\,1\,0\,1\,0\,0\,1$

Using $g_k = b_k \oplus b_{k+1}$ (MSB to LSB):

$$
\begin{aligned}
g_7 &= b_7 = 0 \\
g_6 &= b_7 \oplus b_6 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1 \\
g_5 &= b_6 \oplus b_5 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0 \\
g_4 &= b_5 \oplus b_4 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1 \\
g_3 &= b_4 \oplus b_3 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1 \\
g_2 &= b_3 \oplus b_2 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1 \\
g_1 &= b_2 \oplus b_1 = 0 \oplus 0 = 0 \\
g_0 &= b_1 \oplus b_0 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Gray Code Result:** `01011101`

> [!NOTE]
> Gray encoding ensures that **successive integers differ by only one bit**, eliminating the Hamming cliff and improving local search in binary GAs.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Encoding Selection Guide

| Problem Characteristic | Recommended Encoding | Rationale |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Continuous, smooth fitness landscape | **Real-Valued** | Avoids quantization error |
| Discrete, finite categorical choices | **Integer** | Natural domain mapping |
| Ordering / sequencing problems (TSP) | **Permutation** | Preserves uniqueness constraint |
| Symbolic expressions / formula discovery | **Tree-Based** | Variable-length structure required |
| Discrete yes/no combinatorial problems | **Binary** | Simple, well-studied operators |
| High-precision engineering optimization | **Real-Valued + Gray hybrid** | Continuous search + local optima escape |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Encoding Pipeline in a Genetic Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Problem Definition] --> B[Identify Decision Variables]
    B --> C[Choose Encoding Scheme]
    C --> D1[Binary Encoding]
    C --> D2[Real-Valued Encoding]
    C --> D3[Integer Encoding]
    C --> D4[Permutation Encoding]
    C --> D5[Tree-Based Encoding]
    D1 --> E[Initialize Population of Chromosomes]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> F[Evaluate Fitness Function]
    F --> G[Apply Genetic Operators]
    G --> H{Convergence Reached?}
    H -- No --> G
    H -- Yes --> I[Decode Best Chromosome]
    I --> J[Optimal Phenotype Solution]
```

## 4.2 Phenotype–Genotype Mapping Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Phenotype Space] -- encode E --> G[Genotype Space]
    G -- decode E inverse --> P
    G --> Op1[Selection]
    Op1 --> Op2[Crossover]
    Op2 --> Op3[Mutation]
    Op3 --> G
```

## 4.3 Binary Encoding Bit Structure

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Chromosome
        B7[Bit 7: Sign] --> B6[Bit 6: MSB]
        B6 --> B5[Bit 5]
        B5 --> B4[Bit 4]
        B4 --> B3[Bit 3]
        B3 --> B2[Bit 2]
        B2 --> B1[Bit 1]
        B1 --> B0[Bit 0: LSB]
    end
    Chromosome -- "Decimal Conversion: 105" --> Real["Real Value: -0.8824"]
```

## 4.4 Permutation Encoding for TSP

```mermaid
graph LR
    C["Chromosome: 3 1 5 2 6 4"] --> D["Decoded Tour"]
    D --> N1[City 3]
    N1 --> N2[City 1]
    N2 --> N3[City 5]
    N3 --> N4[City 2]
    N4 --> N5[City 6]
    N5 --> N6[City 4]
    N6 --> N7[Return to City 3]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define encoding in the context of Genetic Algorithms. List any two common encoding schemes.**

**Model Answer:**
Encoding is the process of representing candidate solutions of an optimization problem as chromosomes that can be manipulated by genetic operators. It maps the phenotype (actual solution) to a genotype (encoded form).
Two common schemes: (i) **Binary Encoding**, (ii) **Real-Valued Encoding**.
*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 per scheme)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the Hamming cliff problem in binary encoding? How is it resolved?**

**Model Answer:**
The Hamming cliff occurs when two numerically adjacent values (e.g., 7 = `0111` and 8 = `1000`) have binary representations that differ in *all* bits, making it difficult for single-bit mutation to traverse small numerical changes. It is resolved by using **Gray code encoding**, where successive integers differ by only one bit.
*(3 marks: 1 problem + 2 solution)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]**

**(a)** Explain different types of encoding schemes used in Genetic Algorithms with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A Genetic Algorithm processes chromosomes, so a solution must first be *encoded* into a chromosome structure. The major encoding schemes are:

1. **Binary Encoding** — Each chromosome is a bit string. Example: For two variables $x \in [0, 3]$ and $y \in [0, 7]$, a chromosome of length 5 like `10110` represents concatenated binary codes.

2. **Real-Valued Encoding** — Each gene is a real number. Example: `[2.34, -1.05, 7.89]`. Best for continuous optimization.

3. **Integer Encoding** — Each gene is an integer from a finite set. Example: `[2, 5, 1, 3]`. Used when variables are inherently discrete.

4. **Permutation Encoding** — Chromosome is a permutation of integers. Example: `[3, 1, 4, 2]` for TSP. Requires specialized operators (PMX, OX) to maintain validity.

5. **Tree-Based Encoding** — Used in Genetic Programming; chromosomes are expression trees. Example: Tree for $(x + 3) \cdot \sin(y)$.

> Selection depends on the **problem domain**, **operator compatibility**, and the **principle of minimal alphabets**.

**(b)** A variable $x \in [0, 10]$ is to be encoded using a 6-bit binary chromosome. Given the chromosome `101101`, decode it to a real value. Also compute the precision. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert binary `101101` to decimal.

$$
d = 1 \cdot 2^5 + 0 \cdot 2^4 + 1 \cdot 2^3 + 1 \cdot 2^2 + 0 \cdot 2^1 + 1 \cdot 2^0
$$

$$
d = 32 + 0 + 8 + 4 + 0 + 1 = 45
$$

**Step 2:** Apply decoding formula with $L = 0$, $U = 10$, $n = 6$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= L + \frac{(U - L)}{2^{n} - 1} \cdot d \\
x &= 0 + \frac{10 - 0}{2^{6} - 1} \cdot 45 \\
x &= \frac{10}{63} \cdot 45 \\
x &= \frac{450}{63} \\
x &= 7.1429
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute precision.

$$
\Delta x = \frac{U - L}{2^{n} - 1} = \frac{10}{63} \approx 0.1587
$$

**Final Answer:** $x \approx 7.1429$ with precision $0.1587$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Discuss the **principles for selecting an encoding** in a Genetic Algorithm. Why is encoding called the "language of the GA"? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Encoding is called the *language* of the GA because it defines how the algorithm *talks* about solutions. The principles are:

1. **Meaningful Building Blocks** — Schemata should correspond to functional solution components.
2. **Minimal Alphabet** (Goldberg) — Use the smallest alphabet (e.g., binary $\{0,1\}$) for maximum schema processing.
3. **Closedness** — Operators must always produce valid offspring.
4. **Non-redundancy** — One-to-one mapping between genotype and phenotype avoids wasted search effort.
5. **Scalability** — Encoding should scale gracefully with problem size.

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the role of encoding in the overall GA workflow. How does it differ across binary, real-valued, and permutation-based problems? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The block diagram flow is:

$$
\text{Problem} \rightarrow \text{Encoding} \rightarrow \text{Initialization} \rightarrow \text{Fitness Evaluation} \rightarrow \text{Selection} \rightarrow \text{Crossover} \rightarrow \text{Mutation} \rightarrow \text{Decoding} \rightarrow \text{Optimal Solution}
$$

**Differences:**
- **Binary:** Bit-flip mutation, single/multi-point crossover. Suits discrete problems.
- **Real-Valued:** BLX-α, SBX crossover; Gaussian/polynomial mutation. Suits continuous optimization.
- **Permutation:** PMX/OX crossover; swap/insert mutation. Suits ordering problems (TSP).

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Costing Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `−1` in the denominator** of the decoding formula — use $2^n - 1$, not $2^n$. This is a frequent 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Confusing genotype and phenotype** — the answer must clearly state *what* the chromosome represents and *how* it decodes back.
> 3. **Using standard crossover on permutation chromosomes** — this produces duplicates, making the tour invalid. Always mention PMX/OX.
> 4. **Not stating operator-encoding compatibility** — KTU examiners award marks for explaining *why* a particular encoding demands a particular operator.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Encoding = Genotype–Phenotype mapping.** It is the *first and most critical* design decision in a GA.
- **Five major types:** Binary, Real-Valued, Integer, Permutation, Tree-Based.
- **Decoding formula** for binary: $x = L + \frac{(U - L)}{2^{n} - 1} \cdot d$.
- **Gray code** solves the Hamming cliff issue.
- **Minimal alphabet principle** by Goldberg favors binary encoding when feasible.
- **Permutation encoding** is mandatory for TSP and scheduling problems.
- **Tree encoding** is the foundation of Genetic Programming.
- **Operator-encoding compatibility is non-negotiable** — pick the encoding that pairs cleanly with valid crossover/mutation.
- **No Free Lunch:** No encoding is universally superior; the choice is problem-driven.
- **KTU Hot Keywords:** *chromosome, schema, building block, gray code, PMX, precision, minimal alphabet, closedness.*
- Always end with a **decoding step** before reporting the final phenotypic solution.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reproduction: Roulette Wheel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT411 - SOFT COMPUTING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reproduction: Roulette Wheel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Roulette Wheel Selection (RWS)**, also termed *Fitness Proportionate Selection (FPS)*, is a **stochastic parent-selection operator** in **Genetic Algorithms (GAs)** that probabilistically chooses individuals from the current population to serve as parents for the next generation. The probability of an individual being selected is **directly proportional to its fitness value** relative to the total fitness of the population.

Mathematically, for a population of $N$ individuals with fitness values $f_1, f_2, \dots, f_N$, the selection probability of the $i$-th individual is given by:

$$P_i = \frac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** This operator sits under **Module 4 – Genetic Algorithms**, within the "Reproduction / Selection" sub-topic. It is the *canonical* GA selection scheme and a guaranteed topic in university examinations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **circular pie divided into wedges**, where each individual in the population is represented by a **slice whose arc-length is proportional to its fitness**. The pie is then spun like a *roulette wheel in a casino*, and a **fixed pointer** indicates the winning slice when the wheel stops.

- A **fitter individual** (larger fitness) = **larger wedge** = **higher chance** of being selected.
- A **weaker individual** (smaller fitness) = **smaller wedge** = **lower chance**, but **not zero** — it can still be chosen (this preserves genetic diversity).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Consider a **lottery drum** containing 1000 tickets, of which 700 belong to a topper student and 300 to a struggling student. If one ticket is drawn at random, the topper has a 70% chance of being "selected" — exactly mirroring the fitness-proportionate logic of RWS.

### 1.3 Why Selection is Necessary in GAs

Selection emulates Darwin's **"Survival of the Fittest"**. Without it, crossover and mutation would act on random individuals, and the algorithm would degrade into a **random search** with no convergence pressure. RWS provides *directional pressure* toward better solutions.

### 1.4 Key Terminology (Bolded for KTU Emphasis)

- **Population** ($N$): Total number of candidate solutions in a generation.
- **Fitness** ($f_i$): Quantitative measure of how "good" an individual is.
- **Selection Probability** ($P_i$): Probability that individual $i$ is chosen.
- **Expected Count** ($E_i$): Expected number of times individual $i$ is selected.
- **Cumulative Probability** ($C_i$): Running sum used to implement RWS efficiently.
- **Roulette Wheel**: Metaphorical name for the selection mechanism.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step

The Roulette Wheel Selection algorithm proceeds as follows:

1. **Evaluate Fitness:** Compute the raw fitness $f_i$ of every individual in the population of size $N$.
2. **Total Fitness:** Calculate the sum of all fitness values:
$$F_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i$$
3. **Selection Probability:** For each individual, compute:
$$P_i = \frac{f_i}{F_{total}}$$
4. **Cumulative Probability:** Build the cumulative distribution function (CDF):
$$C_i = \sum_{k=1}^{i} P_k$$
   such that $C_N = 1$.
5. **Spin the Wheel (Mating Pool):** Repeat the following $M$ times (where $M$ is the size of the mating pool, usually $M = N$):
   - Generate a uniform random number $r \sim U(0, 1)$.
   - Find the smallest index $i$ such that $C_i \geq r$.
   - Select individual $i$ for the mating pool.
6. **Return Mating Pool:** Output the selected parents for crossover and mutation.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why? | How? |
|------|------|------|
| Fitness evaluation | Selection needs a *ranking* criterion | Apply objective function $f(x)$ to each chromosome |
| Total fitness | Normalization denominator for probabilities | Simple summation |
| $P_i$ calculation | Translate fitness → probability | Divide each $f_i$ by $F_{total}$ |
| Cumulative $C_i$ | Convert probability lookup to a *single comparison* per spin | Running sum |
| Random spin | Inject *stochasticity* so fitter parents don't always dominate | Uniform random number generator |
| Return pool | Provide parents to crossover operator | Indexed array storage |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Meaning | Units |
|--------|--------------------|---------|-------|
| $P_i$ | $\dfrac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j}$ | Selection probability of individual $i$ | Dimensionless |
| $F_{total}$ | $\sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i$ | Total population fitness | Same as $f_i$ |
| $C_i$ | $\sum_{k=1}^{i} P_k$ | Cumulative probability up to $i$ | Dimensionless |
| $E_i$ | $N \cdot P_i = \dfrac{N \cdot f_i}{F_{total}}$ | Expected number of copies in mating pool | Integer (statistically) |
| Selection condition | $r \leq C_i$ where $r \sim U(0,1)$ | Spinning the wheel | — |
| Property | $\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i = 1$ | Probability axiom | — |
| Property | $C_N = 1$ | End of CDF | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall:** If fitness values can be **negative** (e.g., minimization problems), raw RWS fails. Use a *fitness scaling* or *windowing* transformation first, e.g., $f'_i = f_i - f_{min} + \epsilon$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Structural Design Optimization:** Selecting candidate beam cross-sections based on load-bearing fitness.
- **Neural Network Hyperparameter Tuning:** Evolving learning rates or architectures.
- **VLSI Circuit Partitioning:** Choosing partition layouts with minimal cut-cost.
- **Job-Shop Scheduling:** Picking high-throughput job orderings.
- **Antenna Design & Robotics Trajectory Planning:** Selecting morphologies or waypoints with superior performance metrics.

### 2.5 Advantages and Disadvantages (Exam Favorite)

**Advantages:**
- Simple to implement.
- Gives fitter individuals proportionally higher chance.
- Preserves stochasticity (allows weaker individuals occasionally).

**Disadvantages:**
- **Premature convergence** if one individual has dominant fitness (takes over the wheel).
- **Slow convergence** when fitness values are close to each other (low selection pressure).
- **Sensitive to fitness scaling** — scaling changes selection pressure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Numerical Worked Example (14-Mark Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement:** Consider a GA population of **5 individuals** with the following fitness values:
>
> | Individual | Chromosome | Fitness $f_i$ |
> |:----------:|:----------:|:-------------:|
> | A | 10110 | **20** |
> | B | 01001 | **35** |
> | C | 11010 | **15** |
> | D | 00111 | **50** |
> | E | 11100 | **30** |
>
> Perform **Roulette Wheel Selection** to produce a mating pool of size **4** using the random numbers: $r_1 = 0.45$, $r_2 = 0.78$, $r_3 = 0.12$, $r_4 = 0.91$.

### 3.2 Step 1 — Total Fitness

$$
F_{total} = f_A + f_B + f_C + f_D + f_E = 20 + 35 + 15 + 50 + 30 = 150
$$

### 3.3 Step 2 — Selection Probability of Each Individual

Using $P_i = \dfrac{f_i}{F_{total}} = \dfrac{f_i}{150}$:

$$
P_A = \frac{20}{150} = 0.1333
$$

$$
P_B = \frac{35}{150} = 0.2333
$$

$$
P_C = \frac{15}{150} = 0.1000
$$

$$
P_D = \frac{50}{150} = 0.3333
$$

$$
P_E = \frac{30}{150} = 0.2000
$$

**Sanity check:**
$$P_A + P_B + P_C + P_D + P_E = 0.1333 + 0.2333 + 0.1000 + 0.3333 + 0.2000 = 1.0000 \checkmark$$

### 3.4 Step 3 — Cumulative Probability

$$
C_A = P_A = 0.1333
$$

$$
C_B = P_A + P_B = 0.1333 + 0.2333 = 0.3667
$$

$$
C_C = C_B + P_C = 0.3667 + 0.1000 = 0.4667
$$

$$
C_D = C_C + P_D = 0.4667 + 0.3333 = 0.8000
$$

$$
C_E = C_D + P_E = 0.8000 + 0.2000 = 1.0000
$$

### 3.5 Step 4 — Spin the Wheel (Mating Pool Construction)

For each random number $r$, find the smallest $i$ such that $C_i \geq r$.

**Spin 1: $r_1 = 0.45$**
- $C_A = 0.1333 < 0.45$ → skip
- $C_B = 0.3667 < 0.45$ → skip
- $C_C = 0.4667 \geq 0.45$ → **SELECT C**

**Spin 2: $r_2 = 0.78$**
- $C_A = 0.1333 < 0.78$ → skip
- $C_B = 0.3667 < 0.78$ → skip
- $C_C = 0.4667 < 0.78$ → skip
- $C_D = 0.8000 \geq 0.78$ → **SELECT D**

**Spin 3: $r_3 = 0.12$**
- $C_A = 0.1333 \geq 0.12$ → **SELECT A**

**Spin 4: $r_4 = 0.91$**
- $C_A = 0.1333 < 0.91$ → skip
- $C_B = 0.3667 < 0.91$ → skip
- $C_C = 0.4667 < 0.91$ → skip
- $C_D = 0.8000 < 0.91$ → skip
- $C_E = 1.0000 \geq 0.91$ → **SELECT E**

### 3.6 Step 5 — Final Mating Pool and Verification

**Selected Mating Pool:** $\{C, \ D, \ A, \ E\}$

**Verification via Expected Count:**
$$
E_i = N \cdot P_i = 5 \cdot P_i
$$

$$
E_A = 0.6667, \quad E_B = 1.1667, \quad E_C = 0.5000, \quad E_D = 1.6667, \quad E_E = 1.0000
$$

| Individual | Expected $E_i$ | Actual count | Match? |
|:----------:|:--------------:|:------------:|:------:|
| A | 0.6667 | 1 | ✓ (probabilistic) |
| B | 1.1667 | 0 | ✗ (bad luck) |
| C | 0.5000 | 1 | ✓ (probabilistic) |
| D | 1.6667 | 1 | ✓ (probabilistic) |
| E | 1.0000 | 1 | ✓ (exact) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even though B has higher expected count (1.17), it was not selected — this is the **stochastic nature** of RWS. Over many generations, the expected counts converge to the actual frequencies. D is the most-fit individual and **dominates** the wheel with a **33.33% chance per spin**.

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Study (Exam Bonus)

| Selection Method | Selection Pressure | Best For | Disadvantage |
|------------------|-------------------|----------|--------------|
| **Roulette Wheel** | Medium | Balanced search | Premature convergence possible |
| **Rank Selection** | Adjustable | When fitness spread is large | Loses absolute fitness info |
| **Tournament Selection** | Adjustable (via size $k$) | Parallel/noisy fitness | Needs more comparisons |
| **Stochastic Universal Sampling (SUS)** | Low variance version of RWS | More uniform sampling | Slightly more complex |
| **Boltzmann Selection** | Adaptive | Early/late GA phases | Temperature schedule tuning |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — RWS Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Population of N individuals"] --> B["Evaluate fitness f_i for each individual"]
    B --> C["Compute total fitness F_total = sum of f_i"]
    C --> D["Compute selection probability P_i = f_i / F_total"]
    D --> E["Compute cumulative probability C_i = running sum of P_i"]
    E --> F["Set mating pool counter m = 0"]
    F --> G["Generate random number r in range 0 to 1"]
    G --> H{"Find smallest index i where C_i is greater than or equal to r"}
    H --> I["Add individual i to mating pool"]
    I --> J{"Is mating pool size equal to N?"}
    J -->|"No"| G
    J -->|"Yes"| K["Return mating pool of N parents"]
    K --> L["Pass to Crossover Operator"]
    L --> M["End of Selection Phase"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of RWS within GA

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P1["Population Input"]
        I1["Individual 1: f_1"]
        I2["Individual 2: f_2"]
        I3["Individual N: f_N"]
    end

    subgraph P2["Probability Engine"]
        S1["Total Fitness: F_total"]
        S2["P_i Calculator"]
        S3["Cumulative CDF Builder"]
    end

    subgraph P3["Selection Engine"]
        R1["Random Number Generator"]
        R2["Comparator: r less than or equal to C_i"]
        R3["Index Resolver"]
    end

    subgraph P4["Mating Pool Output"]
        O1["Parent Pool for Crossover"]
        O2["Parent Pool for Mutation"]
    end

    I1 --> S1
    I2 --> S1
    I3 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> R2
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
    R3 --> O1
    R3 --> O2
```

### 4.3 Visual Pie-Wedge Interpretation of the Worked Example

The wheel is divided into wedges whose **arc angles** total $360°$, proportional to each $P_i$:

| Individual | $P_i$ | Arc Angle (degrees) |
|:----------:|:-----:|:-------------------:|
| A | 0.1333 | $48.0°$ |
| B | 0.2333 | $84.0°$ |
| C | 0.1000 | $36.0°$ |
| D | 0.3333 | $120.0°$ |
| E | 0.2000 | $72.0°$ |
| **Total** | **1.0000** | **360.0°** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Individual **D** alone occupies **1/3 of the wheel** (120°), making it the most likely parent — a fact confirmed in the worked example where D was selected on the second spin.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Roulette Wheel Selection in Genetic Algorithms. Write its selection probability formula.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Roulette Wheel Selection is a fitness-proportionate parent selection operator in GAs where each individual is assigned a probability of selection directly proportional to its fitness. The wheel is conceptually divided into wedges whose sizes correspond to these probabilities, and a spin selects a parent.

**Formula (1 Mark):**
$$P_i = \frac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j}$$

where $f_i$ is the fitness of the $i$-th individual and $N$ is the population size. The cumulative probability $C_i = \sum_{k=1}^{i} P_k$ is used during the spin, with the condition $C_N = 1$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]**
**List any three limitations of Roulette Wheel Selection.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Premature convergence** — A single highly-fit individual can dominate the wheel, causing loss of diversity. **(1 Mark)**
2. **Negative fitness problem** — Raw RWS fails for minimization problems with negative $f_i$. **(1 Mark)**
3. **Low selection pressure** when fitness values are nearly equal, slowing convergence. **(1 Mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### ❖ Question A — [KTU University Exam Model – Module 4]

**(a)** Explain the **Roulette Wheel Selection** mechanism in Genetic Algorithms with a neat diagram. Derive the expressions for **selection probability** and **expected count**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a GA population of **6 individuals** with fitness values $f = \{12, 25, 8, 40, 18, 22\}$. Apply the Roulette Wheel Selection procedure to construct a mating pool of size **4** using the random numbers $r = \{0.55, 0.18, 0.82, 0.40\}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✦ Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Theory (7 Marks):**

1. **Definition (2 Marks):** RWS is a stochastic selection method in GAs where the probability of selecting an individual as a parent is proportional to its fitness. The population is visualized as a roulette wheel divided into $N$ wedges, each of arc-length $360° \cdot P_i$.

2. **Selection Probability Formula (2 Marks):**
$$P_i = \frac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j} = \frac{f_i}{F_{total}}$$

3. **Expected Count Derivation (2 Marks):** If the mating pool has size $M$ (usually $M = N$), the expected number of times individual $i$ is selected is:
$$
E_i = M \cdot P_i = \frac{M \cdot f_i}{F_{total}}
$$
This is the *expected* value; actual selection is governed by the random number comparison with the cumulative distribution $C_i$.

4. **Algorithm Outline (1 Mark):** Build $C_i$, generate $r \sim U(0,1)$, find smallest $i$ with $C_i \geq r$, repeat $M$ times. (Diagram from SECTION 4.1 can be referenced.)

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Total Fitness:**
$$
F_{total} = 12 + 25 + 8 + 40 + 18 + 22 = 125
$$

**Step 2 — Selection Probabilities:**

$$
P_1 = \frac{12}{125} = 0.0960, \quad P_2 = \frac{25}{125} = 0.2000, \quad P_3 = \frac{8}{125} = 0.0640
$$

$$
P_4 = \frac{40}{125} = 0.3200, \quad P_5 = \frac{18}{125} = 0.1440, \quad P_6 = \frac{22}{125} = 0.1760
$$

**Sanity check:** $0.0960 + 0.2000 + 0.0640 + 0.3200 + 0.1440 + 0.1760 = 1.0000$ ✓

**Step 3 — Cumulative Probabilities:**

$$
C_1 = 0.0960, \quad C_2 = 0.2960, \quad C_3 = 0.3600, \quad C_4 = 0.6800, \quad C_5 = 0.8240, \quad C_6 = 1.0000
$$

**Step 4 — Wheel Spins:**

| Spin | $r$ | Comparison | Selected Individual |
|:----:|:---:|:----------:|:-------------------:|
| 1 | 0.55 | $C_3 = 0.36 < 0.55 \leq C_4 = 0.68$ | **4** |
| 2 | 0.18 | $C_1 = 0.096 < 0.18 \leq C_2 = 0.296$ | **2** |
| 3 | 0.82 | $C_5 = 0.824 \geq 0.82$ | **5** |
| 4 | 0.40 | $C_3 = 0.36 < 0.40 \leq C_4 = 0.68$ | **4** |

**Final Mating Pool:** $\{4, \ 2, \ 5, \ 4\}$

**Note:** Individual 4 (highest fitness = 40) appears twice — consistent with its $E_4 = 4 \times 0.32 = 1.28$ expected count. **(1 Mark for verification/commentary.)**

---

#### ❖ Question B — [KTU University Exam Model – Module 4]

**(a)** Discuss the **advantages and disadvantages** of Roulette Wheel Selection. Why is it preferred as a baseline method? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A population has 4 individuals with fitnesses $f = \{5, 15, 25, 55\}$. Calculate $P_i$, $C_i$, and the expected count for each. If the random numbers are $r = \{0.20, 0.60, 0.05, 0.95\}$, determine the selected mating pool. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✦ Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Discussion (7 Marks):**

**Advantages (3 Marks):**
- **Simplicity:** Easy to understand and implement in any language.
- **Proportional fairness:** Fitter individuals get more reproductive chances.
- **Stochasticity:** Maintains population diversity by allowing weaker individuals a non-zero chance.

**Disadvantages (3 Marks):**
- **Premature convergence:** A "super-individual" with $f_i \gg$ others hogs the wheel, causing diversity collapse.
- **Fitness scaling sensitivity:** Selection pressure depends heavily on the spread of $f_i$.
- **Negative/zero fitness:** Direct RWS is undefined for $f_i \leq 0$.

**Why Baseline? (1 Mark):** It is the *first* selection scheme taught in GAs, intuitively mirrors Darwinian selection, and serves as a benchmark against which **Rank**, **Tournament**, and **SUS** selection are compared.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Total Fitness:**
$$
F_{total} = 5 + 15 + 25 + 55 = 100
$$

**Step 2 — Selection Probabilities and Cumulative Values:**

| Individual | $f_i$ | $P_i = f_i / 100$ | $C_i$ (cumulative) | $E_i = 4 \cdot P_i$ |
|:----------:|:-----:|:-----------------:|:-----------------:|:-------------------:|
| 1 | 5 | 0.05 | 0.05 | 0.20 |
| 2 | 15 | 0.15 | 0.20 | 0.60 |
| 3 | 25 | 0.25 | 0.45 | 1.00 |
| 4 | 55 | 0.55 | 1.00 | 2.20 |

**Step 3 — Verify:** $\sum P_i = 0.05 + 0.15 + 0.25 + 0.55 = 1.00$ ✓

**Step 4 — Wheel Spins (4 spins, $M=4$):**

| Spin | $r$ | Decision Rule | Selected |
|:----:|:---:|:-------------|:--------:|
| 1 | 0.20 | $C_2 = 0.20 \geq 0.20$ | **2** |
| 2 | 0.60 | $C_3 = 0.45 < 0.60 \leq C_4 = 1.00$ | **4** |
| 3 | 0.05 | $C_1 = 0.05 \geq 0.05$ | **1** |
| 4 | 0.95 | $C_4 = 1.00 \geq 0.95$ | **4** |

**Final Mating Pool:** $\{2, \ 4, \ 1, \ 4\}$

**Observation:** Individual 4 (fittest with $f_4 = 55$) dominates with **55% wheel share** and is selected **twice** out of 4 spins — matching its expected count of 2.2. Individual 1 (weakest) is selected once by chance, demonstrating that RWS *preserves diversity*.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in RWS Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the sum-to-one check** — Examiners allot 1 mark for verifying $\sum P_i = 1$. Students who skip this *invariably lose a mark*.
> 2. **Off-by-one indexing in cumulative probability** — $C_i$ must include $P_i$ (i.e., $C_i = C_{i-1} + P_i$, with $C_0 = 0$). Some students mistakenly use $C_i = \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} P_k$, which shifts all selections by one index.
> 3. **Random number comparison direction** — The standard rule is $C_{i-1} < r \leq C_i$. Using strict $r < C_i$ leads to incorrect selection of the first individual.
> 4. **Confusing expected count with actual count** — $E_i$ is *expected*, not guaranteed. Examiners expect students to acknowledge this in their explanation.
> 5. **Not handling negative fitness** — If the question gives a minimization objective, raw RWS must be adapted (fitness scaling); failing to do so costs full methodology marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Roulette Wheel Selection = Fitness Proportionate Selection (FPS).**
- Selection probability: $P_i = f_i / \sum f_j$.
- **Cumulative probability** $C_i$ is the working tool for the spin.
- One spin = one random number $r \sim U(0,1)$, then find smallest $i$ with $C_i \geq r$.
- **Mating pool size** typically equals population size $N$.
- **Expected count** $E_i = N \cdot P_i$ — useful for validation.
- **Total fitness must be positive**; otherwise, apply fitness scaling.
- **Limitations:** Premature convergence, scaling sensitivity, low pressure on near-equal fitness.
- **Alternatives:** Rank Selection, Tournament Selection, SUS, Boltzmann Selection.
- Always **verify** $\sum P_i = 1$ and $C_N = 1$ before spinning.
- Digram construction steps: Fitness → Probability → Cumulative → Random Spin → Parent Pool.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tournament Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT411 - SOFT COMPUTING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tournament Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Tournament Selection** is a probabilistic, fitness-proportional selection operator used in **Evolutionary Algorithms (EAs)** and **Genetic Algorithms (GAs)** to choose parent chromosomes for the mating pool. The mechanism operates by repeatedly drawing a small subset (a *tournament*) of **k** individuals from the current population at random, allowing them to "compete," and copying the fittest member of the subset into the mating pool. The parameter **k** is called the **Tournament Size** (commonly **k = 2, 3, 4, or 5**) and directly controls the **selection pressure** of the algorithm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tournament Selection** is the most widely used selection scheme in modern GAs because it is **O(N)** in time complexity, requires no global fitness scaling, and is trivially parallelizable.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cricket Knockout" Model

Imagine a national-level **T-20 cricket knockout tournament** with hundreds of teams. Instead of ranking every team by past performance (which is biased and requires comparing everyone with everyone), the organizers run a series of mini-tournaments:

1. Pick **k = 4** teams at random from the registered pool.
2. Make them play a quick match.
3. The **winner** advances to the national-level league; the losers return to the pool.
4. Repeat this pick-2/4/play/promote cycle until the league is full.

The clever insight: a team that *just lost* a mini-tournament is **not eliminated globally** — it simply has *lower probability* of being picked in the next random draw, but it can still come back. This preserves **diversity** while steadily favouring the **fitter** individuals — exactly the philosophy of tournament selection in GAs.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold Highlights for KTU Answer Scripts)

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Tournament Size (k)** | Number of individuals competing in one selection event |
| **Mating Pool** | Intermediate population of selected parents |
| **Selection Pressure** | Tendency to favour high-fitness chromosomes (↑ as k ↑) |
| **Deterministic Tournament** | The strict best of the k always wins (probability = 1) |
| **Probabilistic (Soft) Tournament** | The best wins only with probability *p* (0.5 ≤ p ≤ 1) |
| **Loss of Diversity (LOD)** | Fraction of population not represented in mating pool |
| **Takeover Time** | Generations needed for the best individual to dominate the population |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Tournament Selection was formally proposed by **Goldberg and Deb (1991)** as an efficient alternative to **Roulette Wheel** and **Rank-Based** selection. It is **invariant to positive affine transformations of the fitness function** — meaning multiplying or adding a constant to all fitness values does **not change** the selection behaviour. This is a major advantage over fitness-proportional methods.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic

The **Deterministic Binary Tournament Selection (k = 2)** algorithm executes the following logic for every slot in the mating pool:

1. **Initialize** an empty mating pool `M` of size `N` (same as the parent population).
2. **Repeat** `N` times (to fill the mating pool):
    * **Step 2a** → Draw **two** individuals `x_i` and `x_j` uniformly at random from the current population `P` (sampling with replacement is common in practice).
    * **Step 2b** → **Compare** their fitness values `f(x_i)` and `f(x_j)`.
    * **Step 2c** → **Copy** the individual with the higher fitness into the next slot of `M`.
    * **Step 2d** *(Tie-breaker)* → If `f(x_i) = f(x_j)`, select either one with equal probability (typically 50–50).
3. **Return** the filled mating pool `M` to the crossover/mutation operators.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why" and "How"** — The reason we sample *with replacement* is that it makes the algorithm **statistically independent** of population size, eliminating the need for shuffling the population at every generation.

### 2.2 Probability of Selecting the Best Individual

For a population of **N** individuals where exactly one individual has the highest fitness, the probability that this **best chromosome** survives a single k-tournament is:

$$
P_{\text{best, single trial}} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \left(1 \;-\; \frac{1}{N}\right)^{k}
$$

> This is essentially the classical **"birthday-paradox-style"** inclusion probability: at least one of the `k` drawn slots must contain the elite.

The expected number of times the best individual appears in the **mating pool of size N** is:

$$
E_{\text{best in pool}} \;=\; N \;\cdot\; \left[1 \;-\; \left(1 \;-\; \frac{1}{N}\right)^{k}\right]
$$

### 2.3 General Selection Probability for Individual *i*

For an arbitrary individual *i* occupying a fraction `p_i = f_i / \Sigma f` of the total population fitness (for fitness-proportional interpretation), the probability of winning a single k-tournament is:

$$
P_{i}^{(k)} \;=\; k \;\cdot\; p_i \;\cdot\; \left(1 \;-\; (1 - p_i)^{k-1}\right) \;\approx\; k \;\cdot\; p_i
$$

The approximation `P_i ≈ k · p_i` holds for **small k** and is the reason why tournament selection behaves like a **linear rank-based** scheme for `k = 2`.

### 2.4 Selection Pressure and Loss of Diversity

The **Selection Intensity** *I* (a normalized measure of how aggressively the selection pushes the mean fitness upward) is approximated as:

$$
I \;\approx\; \sqrt{2\,(\ln k \;-\; \ln \sqrt{4.14 \,\cdot\, \ln k})}
$$

The **Loss of Diversity (LOD)** — the fraction of the population that fails to be represented in the mating pool — is:

$$
\text{LOD} \;=\; k^{-\,(k-1)/k} \;\approx\; k^{-1} \quad \text{for large } N
$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Engineering Utility |
| :-- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Best individual wins single trial | $P_{\text{best}} = 1 - \left(1 - \tfrac{1}{N}\right)^{k}$ | Predicts elite preservation across generations |
| 2 | Best individual in mating pool (size N) | $E_{\text{best}} = N \cdot \left[1 - \left(1 - \tfrac{1}{N}\right)^{k}\right]$ | Used to size population for required elitism |
| 3 | Selection probability of individual *i* | $P_{i}^{(k)} \approx k \cdot p_i$ | Linear-in-fitness weighting for small k |
| 4 | Selection intensity | $I \approx \sqrt{2(\ln k - \ln\sqrt{4.14 \ln k})}$ | Predicts convergence rate of the GA |
| 5 | Loss of diversity | $\text{LOD} \approx k^{-1}$ | Maintains exploration of the search space |
| 6 | Takeover time (generations) | $t^{*} \approx \frac{\ln N}{\ln k}$ | Generations for best chromosome to dominate |
| 7 | Time complexity per generation | $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot k)$ | Lower than rank-based $\mathcal{O}(N \log N)$ |
| 8 | Space complexity | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ extra | Fits embedded / FPGA implementations |
| 9 | Sampling assumption | With replacement (default) | Statistical independence per trial |
| 10 | Fitness-transformation invariance | Affine-positive (i.e. $a f + b$ for $a>0$) | No rescaling needed vs. fitness-proportional |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Note** — Tournament selection is the de-facto standard in **neuroevolution** (e.g., NEAT, CMA-ES hybrids), **structural optimization** in civil engineering, **hyper-parameter tuning** of deep networks, and **real-time control** loops — wherever the fitness function is noisy, expensive, or partially defined.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Probability of Selecting the Best Individual

**Given:** Population size `N = 8`, tournament size `k = 3`. The best chromosome occupies exactly one slot in the population (so its fraction `p_{\text{best}} = 1/8`).

**Find:** Probability that the best individual wins a single 3-tournament.

> **Step 1 — Define the complement event.**
> The best individual *fails* to win only if **none** of the `k = 3` sampled slots contain the best chromosome. Since sampling is with replacement, each draw is independent with probability `1 - 1/N = 7/8` of *not* picking the best.

$$
P(\text{best NOT selected}) \;=\; \left(1 \;-\; \frac{1}{N}\right)^{k} \;=\; \left(\frac{7}{8}\right)^{3}
$$

> **Step 2 — Evaluate the numerical value.**

$$
\left(\frac{7}{8}\right)^{3} \;=\; \frac{7 \cdot 7 \cdot 7}{8 \cdot 8 \cdot 8} \;=\; \frac{343}{512} \;\approx\; 0.670
$$

> **Step 3 — Apply the complement rule.**

$$
P(\text{best selected}) \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{343}{512} \;=\; \frac{512 - 343}{512} \;=\; \frac{169}{512} \;\approx\; 0.330
$$

> **Step 4 — Cross-check with the expected value formula in a mating pool of size `N = 8`.**

$$
E_{\text{best in pool}} \;=\; 8 \cdot 0.330 \;\approx\; 2.64 \;\text{copies}
$$

This means **on average ~2.6 copies** of the best individual enter the mating pool per generation, providing strong but not overwhelming selection pressure.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Selection Probabilities for a Small Worked Population (k = 2)

**Population (N = 5):**

| Individual | A | B | C | D | E |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Fitness | 50 | 30 | 20 | 15 | 10 |

**Total fitness:** `Σ f = 50 + 30 + 20 + 15 + 10 = 125`

**Tournament size:** `k = 2` (binary tournament, sampling with replacement)

> **Step 1 — Compute the fitness fraction of each individual.**

$$
p_A = \tfrac{50}{125} = 0.40, \quad p_B = \tfrac{30}{125} = 0.24, \quad p_C = \tfrac{20}{125} = 0.16, \quad p_D = \tfrac{15}{125} = 0.12, \quad p_E = \tfrac{10}{125} = 0.08
$$

> **Step 2 — Derive the binary-tournament selection probability.**

In a binary tournament, individual *i* wins if *i* is drawn in **either** of the two slots AND has the higher fitness than the other slot. With replacement, the probability of *i* being selected is:

$$
P_i \;=\; 2\,p_i \;-\; p_i^{2}
$$

Derivation logic: $P_i = P(\text{i in slot 1}) \cdot P(\text{j not fitter than i}) + P(\text{i in slot 2}) \cdot P(\text{j not fitter than i}) - \text{overlap}$. Under the fitness-fraction proxy $p_i$, this reduces to $2p_i - p_i^2$.

> **Step 3 — Compute $P_i$ for each individual.**

$$
P_A = 2(0.40) - (0.40)^{2} = 0.80 - 0.16 = 0.64
$$

$$
P_B = 2(0.24) - (0.24)^{2} = 0.48 - 0.0576 = 0.4224
$$

$$
P_C = 2(0.16) - (0.16)^{2} = 0.32 - 0.0256 = 0.2944
$$

$$
P_D = 2(0.12) - (0.12)^{2} = 0.24 - 0.0144 = 0.2256
$$

$$
P_E = 2(0.08) - (0.08)^{2} = 0.16 - 0.0064 = 0.1536
$$

> **Step 4 — Verify total probability ≈ 1.0.**

$$
\Sigma P_i = 0.64 + 0.4224 + 0.2944 + 0.2256 + 0.1536 = 1.7360
$$

The sum is **not exactly 1** because the formula $2p_i - p_i^2$ is an *approximation* that assumes $p_i \ll 1$ and independence of the two slots. For a *rigorous* analysis, the joint-event formula is required. To obtain a **proper probability distribution**, we normalize by the total:

$$
P_i^{\text{norm}} = \frac{P_i}{\Sigma P_j} = \frac{2p_i - p_i^{2}}{1.7360}
$$

| Individual | $p_i$ | $P_i = 2p_i - p_i^2$ | $P_i^{\text{norm}}$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| A (f=50) | 0.40 | 0.6400 | 0.3687 |
| B (f=30) | 0.24 | 0.4224 | 0.2433 |
| C (f=20) | 0.16 | 0.2944 | 0.1696 |
| D (f=15) | 0.12 | 0.2256 | 0.1300 |
| E (f=10) | 0.08 | 0.1536 | 0.0885 |
| **Total** | 1.00 | 1.7360 | 1.0000 |

> **Step 5 — Expected number of copies in a mating pool of size 5.**

$$
E_A = 5 \times 0.3687 \approx 1.84, \quad E_B \approx 1.22, \quad E_C \approx 0.85, \quad E_D \approx 0.65, \quad E_E \approx 0.44
$$

The cumulative sum is `≈ 5.00`, confirming conservation of chromosomes. Note that the best individual (A) gets **almost 2 copies**, while the worst (E) is at risk of being lost — yet **not eliminated**, preserving diversity.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Tournament Size *k*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is **frequently asked** in KTU ESE Part-A and is a high-yield revision table.

| Tournament Size (k) | Selection Pressure | Diversity Preserved | Typical Use-Case |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| k = 2 | Low | High | Slow, exploratory search |
| k = 3 | Moderate | Moderate | **Default** in most GAs |
| k = 4–5 | High | Low | Fast convergence, near-optimum refinement |
| k = N | Maximal (deterministic best of all) | Zero | Degenerates to pure elitism |

**Takeover time** (generations for the best individual to take over the entire population):

$$
t^{*} \;\approx\; \frac{\ln N}{\ln k}
$$

> For `N = 100` and `k = 2`: $t^* \approx \ln 100 / \ln 2 \approx 6.64$ generations.
> For `N = 100` and `k = 5`: $t^* \approx \ln 100 / \ln 5 \approx 2.86$ generations.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flowchart — Tournament Selection Algorithm (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Generation gen] --> B[Initialize mating pool M of size N]
    B --> C{i >= N?}
    C -- No --> D[Randomly draw k individuals from population P with replacement]
    D --> E[Form tournament group T = x1, x2, ..., xk]
    E --> F[Evaluate fitness of each x in T]
    F --> G{Soft tournament mode?}
    G -- No --> H[Deterministic: select x with max fitness]
    G -- Yes --> I[Generate uniform random r in 0,1]
    I --> J{r <= p_win?}
    J -- Yes --> H
    J -- No --> K[Select random x from T]
    H --> L[Copy winner to M at position i]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Increment i to i + 1]
    M --> C
    C -- Yes --> N[Return filled mating pool M]
    N --> O[Crossover and Mutation operators act on M]
    O --> P[End: Form new generation gen + 1]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style N fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style P fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style H fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style K fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Data-Flow Architecture of Tournament Selection in a GA

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Current Population P of size N] -->|Random k-subset draw| T1[Tournament Slot 1]
    P -->|Random k-subset draw| T2[Tournament Slot 2]
    P -->|Random k-subset draw| T3[Tournament Slot k]
    T1 --> Cmp[Fitness Comparator Module]
    T2 --> Cmp
    T3 --> Cmp
    Cmp --> Win[Winner x with max fitness]
    Win -->|Append| M[Mating Pool M of size N]
    M --> XO[Crossover Operator]
    XO --> Mut[Mutation Operator]
    Mut --> Child[Offspring Population Q of size N]
    Child --> Rep[Replacement Strategy - elitism or generational]
    Rep --> P

    style P fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style Win fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style M fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style Child fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Schematic — k-Tournament Tournament Tree (k = 4)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[R1: Candidate x1 - fitness 12] --> M1[Match 1]
    R2[R2: Candidate x2 - fitness 25] --> M1
    M1 --> W1[Winner W1 - fitness 25]
    R3[R3: Candidate x3 - fitness 8] --> M2[Match 2]
    R4[R4: Candidate x4 - fitness 30] --> M2
    M2 --> W2[Winner W2 - fitness 30]
    W1 --> F[Final Tournament]
    W2 --> F
    F --> Champ[Champion x4 - fitness 30 selected for mating pool]

    style Champ fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style W1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style W2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style R1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style R2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style R3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style R4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Tournament Tree** representation above makes it clear why this selection method scales **logarithmically** with population size for the elite: only $\lceil \log_2 k \rceil$ "matches" are needed per selection event, regardless of how large `N` is.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model-Q]**
> **Q1.** Define **Tournament Selection** in Genetic Algorithms. Mention the role of the **tournament size `k`**.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Tournament Selection is a probabilistic selection mechanism in Genetic Algorithms in which **k** individuals are randomly chosen (with replacement) from the current population, made to "compete" in a tournament, and the fittest member is copied into the mating pool. This is repeated `N` times to fill the mating pool of size `N`. The parameter **k** is called the *tournament size*; it directly controls the **selection pressure** — as `k` increases, fitter individuals are more strongly favoured, but population diversity decreases. The most common choice is **k = 2** (binary tournament) because it offers a balanced trade-off between exploitation and exploration.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model-Q]**
> **Q2.** List **any three advantages** of Tournament Selection over **Roulette Wheel Selection**.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Time efficiency** — Tournament selection runs in $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot k)$ time and requires no global summation of fitness, whereas roulette wheel needs $\mathcal{O}(N)$ to compute the cumulative fitness and $\mathcal{O}(N)$ for the binary search per individual.
> 2. **Invariant to positive affine transformations** — Multiplying or adding a positive constant to all fitness values does not change the selection behaviour, eliminating the need for fitness rescaling.
> 3. **Handles negative fitness values** — Tournament selection only requires *ordinal comparison* of fitness (greater-than, less-than), not absolute values, so it works seamlessly with negative or zero fitness — a major weakness of roulette wheel.
> 4. *(Bonus)* — Easy to implement on **parallel hardware** because each tournament is an independent mini-experiment.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, ESE Module 4, Q-Set A]**
>
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Tournament Selection** algorithm in detail. Clearly state the role of the tournament size `k` and the difference between **deterministic** and **probabilistic (soft) tournament** selection.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] For a population of `N = 10` individuals, compute the probability that the best individual wins a single tournament of size `k = 3`. Also estimate the **expected number of copies** of the best individual in a mating pool of size 10.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

Tournament Selection is performed in `N` independent rounds. In each round:

* **Step 1:** Randomly sample `k` individuals from the population (sampling with or without replacement).
* **Step 2:** Evaluate the fitness of these `k` candidates.
* **Step 3:** In the **deterministic** variant, the individual with the **strictly maximum** fitness is deterministically copied to the mating pool. In the **probabilistic (soft) variant**, the best is chosen with probability *p* (typically 0.75 ≤ *p* ≤ 1.0); otherwise, a random non-best candidate is chosen.
* **Step 4:** Repeat `N` times to fill the mating pool.

**Role of `k`:** The parameter `k` governs **selection pressure**. For `k = 2` the pressure is low (genuine exploration); for `k = 5` the pressure is high (rapid convergence at the cost of diversity); for `k = N` it degenerates into pure elitism (best of all always wins). A widely used default is `k = 2` or `k = 3`.

**Deterministic vs Probabilistic (Soft) Tournament:**

| Aspect | Deterministic | Probabilistic (Soft) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Best wins with probability | 1.0 | *p* (0.5 ≤ *p* ≤ 1.0) |
| Diversity preserved | Low | Higher |
| Premature convergence risk | Higher | Lower |
| Implementation complexity | Simple | Needs uniform RNG |

*(Distribution: 2 marks definition, 2 marks role of k, 3 marks comparison table.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:** `N = 10`, `k = 3`, single elite individual.

> **Step 1 — Probability that the best wins a single trial.**

$$
P_{\text{best}} = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^{k} = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{10}\right)^{3} = 1 - (0.9)^{3}
$$

$$
(0.9)^{3} = 0.729
$$

$$
P_{\text{best}} = 1 - 0.729 = 0.271
$$

> **Step 2 — Expected number of copies in mating pool of size 10.**

$$
E_{\text{best in pool}} = N \cdot P_{\text{best}} = 10 \times 0.271 = 2.71
$$

**Result:** The best individual wins **27.1 %** of the time per trial and contributes **~2.71 copies** to the mating pool on average — strong but not overwhelming selection pressure, ideal for balanced exploration–exploitation.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, ESE Module 4, Q-Set B]**
>
> **Question B (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Compare **Tournament Selection** with **Roulette Wheel Selection** and **Rank-Based Selection**. State **two** situations where Tournament Selection is the **only** viable option.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Derive the **selection probability** of the best individual in a k-tournament when sampling is done *with replacement*. Hence, compute the **selection intensity** for `k = 3`, `N = 50`.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

| Criterion | Roulette Wheel | Rank-Based | Tournament |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Selection pressure | Low (fixed) | Tunable (via selective pressure *SP*) | Tunable (via *k*) |
| Time complexity | $\mathcal{O}(N)$ per gen | $\mathcal{O}(N \log N)$ | $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot k)$ |
| Handles negative fitness | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Global fitness stats needed | Yes (sum, cumulative) | Yes (sort) | **No** |
| Parallelism | Hard | Moderate | **Trivial** |
| Fitness-transformation invariance | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |

**Two situations where Tournament Selection is the *only* viable option:**
1. **Noisy or non-deterministic fitness functions** (e.g., robotics simulation, financial markets) — tournament selection averages out noise locally and is robust to fitness variance.
2. **Distributed / parallel / hardware implementations** (e.g., FPGA, GPU-based GAs, edge-computing embedded systems) — because each tournament is independent, the algorithm parallelizes without communication overhead.

*(Distribution: 4 marks comparison table, 3 marks for the two situations.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Derivation:**

> **Step 1.** Let $A$ = event "the best individual is selected in one trial". In a `k`-tournament with replacement, the best is selected if it appears in **at least one** of the `k` drawn slots.

> **Step 2.** Compute the probability of the complement event $\bar{A}$ = "the best is **not** drawn in any of the k slots". Each draw is independent; the chance of *not* drawing the best in one draw is $1 - 1/N$. Across `k` draws:

$$
P(\bar{A}) = \left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^{k}
$$

> **Step 3.** Apply the complement rule:

$$
P(A) = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^{k}
$$

> **Step 4.** Compute the selection intensity for `k = 3`, `N = 50` using $I \approx \sqrt{2(\ln k - \ln\sqrt{4.14 \ln k})}$:

$$
\ln 3 \approx 1.0986, \quad \ln k = 1.0986
$$

$$
\ln(4.14 \cdot \ln 3) = \ln(4.14 \cdot 1.0986) = \ln(4.548) \approx 1.5149
$$

$$
\ln\sqrt{4.14 \cdot \ln 3} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1.5149 \approx 0.7575
$$

$$
I \approx \sqrt{2 (1.0986 - 0.7575)} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 0.3411} = \sqrt{0.6822} \approx 0.826
$$

**Result:** The selection intensity is `I ≈ 0.826`, indicating moderately strong selection pressure appropriate for steady convergence in mid-sized GAs.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks (Examiner Insights):**
> 1. **Confusing sampling with vs. without replacement** — The formula $P_{\text{best}} = 1 - (1 - 1/N)^k$ is valid **only** for sampling *with replacement*. For *without replacement*, the formula becomes $P_{\text{best}} = k/N$ (linear in `k`). Writing the wrong version costs **2 marks** in ESE.
> 2. **Forgetting the complement** — Many students compute $P(\text{best NOT selected})$ and submit it as the answer. The complement step `1 - (...)` is **mandatory**.
> 3. **Mixing up Selection Pressure and Selection Intensity** — *Pressure* is a behavioural hyper-parameter (controlled by `k`); *Intensity* is the resulting statistical moment `I` of the selected distribution. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 4. **Ignoring the role of replacement** — In the binary tournament derivation, students often write $P_i = 2 p_i$ without subtracting $p_i^2$ and lose the **affine-invariance** property.
> 5. **Not stating the assumption of randomness** — Always explicitly mention "sampling with replacement" or "uniformly at random" in your answer scripts. Missing this costs **1 mark** in KTU evaluation.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Tournament Selection** = random draw of **k** individuals → best wins → copied to mating pool. *(Core definition — memorize verbatim.)*
- Default tournament size in textbook GAs: **k = 2** (binary tournament).
- Selection pressure **increases monotonically** with `k`; diversity **decreases** with `k`.
- **Best individual selection probability (with replacement):** $P_{\text{best}} = 1 - (1 - 1/N)^k$.
- **Best individual selection probability (without replacement):** $P_{\text{best}} = k/N$.
- **Selection intensity approximation:** $I \approx \sqrt{2(\ln k - \ln\sqrt{4.14 \ln k})}$.
- **Loss of Diversity (LOD):** $\text{LOD} \approx k^{-1}$.
- **Takeover time:** $t^* \approx \ln N / \ln k$.
- **Time complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot k)$ — **fastest** among classical selection schemes.
- **Invariance:** Positive affine fitness transformations leave selection behaviour unchanged.
- **Negative fitness? No problem.** Only ordinal comparisons are needed.
- **Deterministic** tournament = best always wins (prob = 1); **Soft/Probabilistic** tournament = best wins with prob *p* (0.5 ≤ p ≤ 1).
- **Parallelism:** Trivially parallelizable — each tournament is an independent sub-task.
- **Compared to Roulette Wheel:** Faster, no global fitness summation, handles negative fitness, fitness-transformation invariant.
- **Compared to Rank-Based:** No sorting needed ($\mathcal{O}(N \cdot k)$ vs. $\mathcal{O}(N \log N)$).
- **Common pitfall:** Forgetting the complement `1 - (...)` in the $P_{\text{best}}$ derivation.
- **KTU Board Favourite Topics:** (i) Numerical evaluation of $P_{\text{best}}$, (ii) selection-probability tables for small populations, (iii) comparison with roulette wheel, (iv) takeover-time calculation, (v) effect of `k` on exploration vs. exploitation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rank Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT411 - SOFT COMPUTING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rank Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Rank Selection** is a parent selection operator in **Genetic Algorithms (GAs)** in which individuals are chosen for reproduction based on their *rank* within the sorted population rather than on their raw fitness values. The population is first sorted according to fitness, and a probability of selection is then assigned to each individual as a monotonically decreasing (or increasing) function of its rank position. This decoupling of selection pressure from raw fitness magnitude ensures a **bounded and controllable selection pressure**, which avoids the two classical pathologies of fitness-proportionate (Roulette-Wheel) selection — namely **premature convergence** caused by a few super-fit individuals and **stagnation/slow finishing** caused by nearly identical fitness values across the population.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Genetic Algorithms):** Rank selection is mandatory study material as a *parent selection mechanism*. You must know (a) why it is needed, (b) the linear ranking formula, (c) the role of the bias factor $SP$ (or $\eta^+$, $\eta^-$), and (d) its comparison with Roulette-Wheel and Tournament selection.

## 1.2 The Two Variants of Rank Selection

| Variant | Function Used | Common Setting |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear Ranking** | Linear function of rank | $\eta^+ \in [1.0,\,2.0]$ |
| **Exponential (Non-Linear) Ranking** | Exponential function of rank | $c \in (0,1)$ base parameter |

In KTU exams, **Linear Ranking** is the canonical variant and is asked most frequently.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Job Interview" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a company shortlists **100 candidates** for an interview. Instead of hiring the "highest-Marks" candidate 90% of the time (Roulette-Wheel behaviour), the HR department simply **ranks** them — best CV gets rank 1, next best rank 2, and so on — and then picks people for interview slots based on this rank. Even if one candidate has Marks *dramatically* higher than the rest, the rank-based system guarantees the worst candidate still has a *minimum* non-zero chance and the best candidate is only mildly favoured. This *bounded favouritism* is exactly what Rank Selection enforces inside a Genetic Algorithm.

## 1.4 Why Rank Selection Exists — The Two Failures of Pure Fitness-Proportionate Selection

1. **Premature Convergence (Domination Problem):** If one individual has fitness $10\times$ larger than the rest, in Roulette-Wheel selection it will occupy $>90\%$ of the wheel and crowd out diversity in the gene pool, causing the GA to converge to a local optimum.
2. **Stagnation (Indistinguishability Problem):** Late in evolution, when most individuals have very similar fitness values (e.g., 0.991, 0.992, 0.993), the Roulette-Wheel cannot distinguish them and selection becomes effectively random, slowing or stalling the search.

Rank Selection solves **both** by replacing raw fitness $f_i$ with a *rank-derived probability* that is always well-conditioned.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline of Rank Selection

The execution of Rank Selection proceeds in the following ordered stages:

1. **Stage 1 — Fitness Evaluation:** Compute raw fitness $f_i$ for every individual $i = 1, 2, \dots, N$ in the current population.
2. **Stage 2 — Sort by Fitness:** Sort the population in *ascending* order of fitness (worst first, best last). Assign rank $i$ to the $i^{th}$ individual.
3. **Stage 3 — Assign Expected Count / Probability:** Using the linear ranking formula, assign each rank a selection probability $P(i)$.
4. **Stage 4 — Stochastic Sampling:** Perform sampling (e.g., Stochastic Universal Sampling, SUS) using the rank-based probability distribution to choose mating parents.
5. **Stage 5 — Reproduce:** Selected parents undergo crossover and mutation to produce offspring for the next generation.

## 2.2 Linear Ranking — Mathematical Formulation

Let $N$ be the population size, $i$ the rank (with $i = 1$ for the **worst** and $i = N$ for the **best** individual). Define two parameters:

- $\eta^+$ = expected number of copies allocated to the **best** individual (rank $N$).
- $\eta^-$ = expected number of copies allocated to the **worst** individual (rank $1$).

The expected count for the individual at rank $i$ is:

$$
E(i) \;=\; \eta^{+} \;-\; (\eta^{+} - \eta^{-})\,\dfrac{i - 1}{N - 1}
$$

The corresponding **selection probability** is obtained by normalising by $N$:

$$
P(i) \;=\; \dfrac{1}{N}\left[\eta^{+} - (\eta^{+} - \eta^{-})\,\dfrac{i - 1}{N - 1}\right]
$$

### Constraint Equations (must be satisfied)

$$
\eta^{+} + \eta^{-} = 2, \qquad 1 \le \eta^{+} \le 2, \qquad 0 \le \eta^{-} \le 1
$$

The most commonly recommended setting in literature and in KTU numericals is:

$$
\eta^{+} = 1.1 \;\;\text{to}\;\; 1.2, \quad \eta^{-} = 2 - \eta^{+}
$$

This gives a **selection pressure $SP \in [1.0,\,2.0]$** (low-to-moderate), which is well-behaved across problem types.

> [!TIP]
> **Physical / Engineering Utility:** Rank Selection is the workhorse selection scheme in production-grade GAs used for **VLSI circuit layout, antenna array design, job-shop scheduling, neural-architecture search, and PID controller tuning** — anywhere you need a *stable* and *predictable* evolutionary search that does not collapse prematurely.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> **Master Reference Table — Memorise This!**

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| $N$ | Population size | $20 - 200$ | Individuals |
| $i$ | Rank of an individual (1 = worst, $N$ = best) | $1 \le i \le N$ | Dimensionless integer |
| $f_i$ | Raw fitness of individual at rank $i$ | Problem-specific | Domain-dependent |
| $\eta^+$ | Expected copies of the best individual | $1.1 - 2.0$ | Dimensionless |
| $\eta^-$ | Expected copies of the worst individual | $0.0 - 0.9$ | Dimensionless |
| $E(i)$ | Expected number of offspring for rank $i$ | $\eta^- \le E(i) \le \eta^+$ | Dimensionless |
| $P(i)$ | Selection probability for rank $i$ | $\sum P(i) = 1$ | Probability |
| $SP$ | Selection pressure (Baker's definition) | $1.0 - 2.0$ | Dimensionless |
| $c$ | Base of exponential ranking | $0 < c < 1$ | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall Avoidance — Markdown & LaTeX:** In the table above, vertical bars are NOT used to enclose $\eta^+$, $\eta^-$, etc. We write $\eta^+$, $\eta^-$ as standalone symbols. When you write your own answer scripts, **never write** $\vert x \vert$ inside a markdown table; use $\lvert x \rvert$ or the verbal form "absolute value of x".

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example (Linear Rank Selection)

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A GA population has **$N = 6$** individuals with the following raw fitness values (unsorted):

| Individual | A | B | C | D | E | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fitness | 50 | 90 | 10 | 80 | 30 | 60 |

Use **Linear Rank Selection** with $\eta^+ = 1.6$ and $\eta^- = 0.4$ to compute:
1. The expected count $E(i)$ and probability $P(i)$ for each rank.
2. The expected number of times each individual is selected if SUS (Stochastic Universal Sampling) is used with **$N_{samp} = 6$** sample points.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Sort the population in ascending order of fitness (worst first).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sorted (worst to best):} \quad & C(10),\; E(30),\; A(50),\; F(60),\; D(80),\; B(90) \\
\text{Assigned ranks:} \quad & i = 1,\;2,\;3,\;4,\;5,\;6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify the constraint on $\eta^+, \eta^-$.**

$$
\eta^+ + \eta^- = 1.6 + 0.4 = 2.0 \quad \checkmark
$$

The parameters are within permissible bounds: $1 \le 1.6 \le 2$ and $0 \le 0.4 \le 1$.

**Step 3 — Apply the Linear Ranking formula** $E(i) = \eta^{+} - (\eta^{+} - \eta^{-})\dfrac{i - 1}{N - 1}$, with $N - 1 = 5$.

For rank $i = 1$ (worst — individual **C**):
$$
E(1) = 1.6 - (1.6 - 0.4)\cdot\dfrac{1 - 1}{5} = 1.6 - (1.2)(0) = 1.6
$$

For rank $i = 2$ (individual **E**):
$$
E(2) = 1.6 - (1.2)\cdot\dfrac{2 - 1}{5} = 1.6 - \dfrac{1.2}{5} = 1.6 - 0.24 = 1.36
$$

For rank $i = 3$ (individual **A**):
$$
E(3) = 1.6 - (1.2)\cdot\dfrac{3 - 1}{5} = 1.6 - \dfrac{2.4}{5} = 1.6 - 0.48 = 1.12
$$

For rank $i = 4$ (individual **F**):
$$
E(4) = 1.6 - (1.2)\cdot\dfrac{4 - 1}{5} = 1.6 - \dfrac{3.6}{5} = 1.6 - 0.72 = 0.88
$$

For rank $i = 5$ (individual **D**):
$$
E(5) = 1.6 - (1.2)\cdot\dfrac{5 - 1}{5} = 1.6 - \dfrac{4.8}{5} = 1.6 - 0.96 = 0.64
$$

For rank $i = 6$ (best — individual **B**):
$$
E(6) = 1.6 - (1.2)\cdot\dfrac{6 - 1}{5} = 1.6 - \dfrac{6.0}{5} = 1.6 - 1.20 = 0.40
$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check on the sum of expected counts** (must equal $N = 6$ for a stationary population):
$$
\sum_{i=1}^{6} E(i) = 1.60 + 1.36 + 1.12 + 0.88 + 0.64 + 0.40 = 6.00 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 5 — Convert expected counts to selection probabilities** $P(i) = E(i)/N$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
