P(1) &= 1.60 / 6 \approx 0.2667 \\
P(2) &= 1.36 / 6 \approx 0.2267 \\
P(3) &= 1.12 / 6 \approx 0.1867 \\
P(4) &= 0.88 / 6 \approx 0.1467 \\
P(5) &= 0.64 / 6 \approx 0.1067 \\
P(6) &= 0.40 / 6 \approx 0.0667
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum check: $0.2667 + 0.2267 + 0.1867 + 0.1467 + 0.1067 + 0.0667 = 1.0002 \approx 1.000$ (the $0.0002$ excess is rounding). $\checkmark$

### 3.1.3 Final Results Table

| Rank $i$ | Individual | Fitness | $E(i)$ | $P(i)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (worst) | C | 10 | 1.60 | 0.2667 |
| 2 | E | 30 | 1.36 | 0.2267 |
| 3 | A | 50 | 1.12 | 0.1867 |
| 4 | F | 60 | 0.88 | 0.1467 |
| 5 | D | 80 | 0.64 | 0.1067 |
| 6 (best) | B | 90 | 0.40 | 0.0667 |
| **Total** | — | — | **6.00** | **1.0000** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Counter-Intuitive Observation — Common KTU Trap:** Notice that the **worst** individual C (fitness 10) actually has a *higher* expected count (1.60) than the **best** individual B (fitness 90, expected count 0.40). This is **not an error** — it is the *defining feature* of a linear ranking where the **best** is at rank $N$ and the **worst** at rank 1, and where the slope of the linear function happens to be **negative** in this orientation. Many students invert the ranking direction in the exam. Always double-check which end is rank 1.

## 3.2 Comparison with Other Selection Schemes (Analytical Matrix)

> **Why Rank Selection is Preferred over Roulette Wheel — A KTU Favourite Question**

| Property | Roulette Wheel | Rank Selection | Tournament Selection |
|---|---|---|---|
| Selection based on | Raw fitness | Rank (sorted position) | Random subset of $k$ |
| Premature convergence risk | **High** | **Low** | Low–Medium (depends on $k$) |
| Stagnation handling | Poor | **Good** | Good |
| Selection pressure control | Implicit (fitness ratio) | **Explicit** via $\eta^+$ | Tunable via $k$ |
| Computational cost | $O(N)$ | $O(N \log N)$ (sorting) | $O(k)$ per pick |
| Robust to negative fitness | No (needs scaling) | **Yes** | Yes |
| KTUs' favourite mark-yield | 2–3 marks | **7–14 marks** | 5–7 marks |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Rank Selection Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Pop Init: N Individuals"] --> B["Eval Raw Fitness f_i"]
    B --> C["Sort Ascending by Fitness"]
    C --> D["Assign Rank i = 1 to N"]
    D --> E["Apply E i = eta_plus minus eta_plus minus eta_minus times i-1 over N-1"]
    E --> F["Compute P i = E i divided by N"]
    F --> G["Build Cumulative Prob Wheel"]
    G --> H["Stochastic Universal Sampling SUS"]
    H --> I["Select Mating Pool of Parents"]
    I --> J["Crossover and Mutation"]
    J --> K["Next Generation Offspring"]
    K --> A

    style A fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    style B fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000

    style J fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#6600cc,color:#000
    style K fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Position of Rank Selection inside a GA

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PRE["Pre-Selection Stage"]
        P1["Population Pool P_t"]
        P2["Fitness Function phi"]
        P3["Fitness Vector F = phi x for x in P_t"]
    end

    subgraph CORE["Rank Selection Core"]
        C1["Sorting Module"]
        C2["Rank Assignment Module"]
        C3["Probability Calculator using eta_plus and eta_minus"]
        C4["Sampling Module SUS or Roulette"]
    end

    subgraph POST["Post-Selection Stage"]
        Q1["Mating Pool M_t"]
        Q2["Crossover Operator"]
        Q3["Mutation Operator"]
        Q4["Offspring Pool P_t_plus_1"]
    end

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 --> Q1 --> Q2 --> Q3 --> Q4

    style P1 fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    style P2 fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000

    style C4 fill:#ffe6f0,stroke:#cc0066,color:#000
    style Q1 fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#6600cc,color:#000
    style Q2 fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    style Q3 fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    style Q4 fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Selection-Probability vs Rank Plot (Schematic Bar Chart)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1["Rank 1 worst P 0.267"] --> R2["Rank 2 P 0.227"]
    R2 --> R3["Rank 3 P 0.187"]
    R3 --> R4["Rank 4 P 0.147"]
    R4 --> R5["Rank 5 P 0.107"]
    R5 --> R6["Rank 6 best P 0.067"]

    style R1 fill:#0066cc,stroke:#003366,color:#fff
    style R2 fill:#1a75d2,stroke:#003366,color:#fff
    style R3 fill:#4d94ff,stroke:#003366,color:#fff
    style R4 fill:#80b3ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style R5 fill:#b3d1ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style R6 fill:#e6f0ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
```

The colour gradient (dark blue → light blue) visually conveys the *decreasing* selection probability from the worst to the best rank under the chosen orientation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between fitness-proportionate (Roulette-Wheel) selection and Rank Selection. Why is Rank Selection often preferred in practice?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Roulette-Wheel Selection | Rank Selection |
|---|---|---|
| Basis of probability | Raw fitness magnitude $f_i$ | Rank position after sorting |
| Handling of super-fit individuals | They dominate the wheel | Selection pressure is bounded by $\eta^+$ |
| Handling of near-equal fitness | Cannot distinguish (stagnation) | Always distinguishes by rank |
| Negative fitness values | Requires fitness scaling | **Not required** |
| Computational cost | $O(N)$ | $O(N \log N)$ due to sorting |

Rank Selection is preferred because it gives the GA designer **explicit control** over selection pressure via the parameters $\eta^+$ and $\eta^-$, decoupling selection probability from raw fitness magnitude, which in turn **prevents premature convergence** and **avoids stagnation** — the two classic failure modes of pure fitness-proportionate selection. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State the linear ranking formula and explain the role of the parameter $\eta^+$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The expected number of copies of the individual with rank $i$ (where $i = 1$ is the worst and $i = N$ is the best) is:

$$
E(i) = \eta^{+} - (\eta^{+} - \eta^{-})\,\dfrac{i - 1}{N - 1}
$$

subject to the constraint $\eta^{+} + \eta^{-} = 2$ with $1 \le \eta^{+} \le 2$ and $0 \le \eta^{-} \le 1$.

**Role of $\eta^+$:** It is the expected number of copies allocated to the **best** individual in the population. It directly controls the **selection pressure** of the GA:
- A **small** value of $\eta^+$ (e.g., 1.1) yields a *low* selection pressure — the GA behaves almost like random search, preserving diversity.
- A **larger** value of $\eta^+$ (e.g., 1.8) yields a *high* selection pressure — the best individuals dominate reproduction, accelerating convergence but risking premature convergence.

Thus, $\eta^+$ is the **GA designer's tuning knob** for the exploration–exploitation trade-off. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Consider a GA population of $N = 8$ individuals with fitness values: $\{12,\,5,\,28,\,40,\,3,\,36,\,20,\,45\}$.**

**(a)** Apply **Linear Rank Selection** with $\eta^+ = 1.8$ and $\eta^- = 0.2$ to compute the expected count $E(i)$ and selection probability $P(i)$ for each rank. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **advantages** of rank selection over roulette-wheel selection and discuss how the value of $\eta^+$ affects the exploration–exploitation trade-off. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Sort the population in ascending order and assign ranks.**

Sorted fitness values: $3,\;5,\;12,\;20,\;28,\;36,\;40,\;45$

| Rank $i$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fitness | 3 | 5 | 12 | 20 | 28 | 36 | 40 | 45 |

**Step 2 — Apply the linear ranking formula** with $N = 8$, $N - 1 = 7$, $\eta^+ = 1.8$, $\eta^- = 0.2$, so $\eta^+ - \eta^- = 1.6$.

$$
E(i) = 1.8 - (1.6)\cdot\dfrac{i - 1}{7}
$$

Evaluating for each rank:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(1) &= 1.8 - 1.6 \cdot \dfrac{0}{7} = 1.800 \\
E(2) &= 1.8 - 1.6 \cdot \dfrac{1}{7} = 1.8 - 0.2286 = 1.5714 \\
E(3) &= 1.8 - 1.6 \cdot \dfrac{2}{7} = 1.8 - 0.4571 = 1.3429 \\
E(4) &= 1.8 - 1.6 \cdot \dfrac{3}{7} = 1.8 - 0.6857 = 1.1143 \\
E(5) &= 1.8 - 1.6 \cdot \dfrac{4}{7} = 1.8 - 0.9143 = 0.8857 \\
E(6) &= 1.8 - 1.6 \cdot \dfrac{5}{7} = 1.8 - 1.1429 = 0.6571 \\
E(7) &= 1.8 - 1.6 \cdot \dfrac{6}{7} = 1.8 - 1.3714 = 0.4286 \\
E(8) &= 1.8 - 1.6 \cdot \dfrac{7}{7} = 1.8 - 1.6000 = 0.2000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute selection probabilities** $P(i) = E(i)/8$:

| Rank $i$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $E(i)$ | 1.8000 | 1.5714 | 1.3429 | 1.1143 | 0.8857 | 0.6571 | 0.4286 | 0.2000 |
| $P(i)$ | 0.2250 | 0.1964 | 0.1679 | 0.1393 | 0.1107 | 0.0821 | 0.0536 | 0.0250 |

**Sum check:** $\sum E(i) = 1.8000 + 1.5714 + 1.3429 + 1.1143 + 0.8857 + 0.6571 + 0.4286 + 0.2000 = 8.0000$ $\checkmark$

**Sum check on probabilities:** $\sum P(i) \approx 1.0000$ $\checkmark$ **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Advantages of Rank Selection over Roulette-Wheel Selection:**

1. **Bounded Selection Pressure:** Because the best individual receives only $\eta^+$ copies (≤ 2) regardless of how much larger its fitness is than the rest, super-fit individuals cannot dominate the gene pool. This **avoids premature convergence**. (1 mark)
2. **Resistance to Stagnation:** When all individuals have nearly equal fitness (a common late-stage scenario), raw-fitness methods cannot distinguish them. Rank Selection always produces a strictly monotonic distribution by rank, so selection remains effective. (1 mark)
3. **No Need for Fitness Scaling:** Roulette-Wheel requires non-negative fitness and often benefits from linear/sigma scaling to remain effective. Rank Selection works directly on any fitness values, including negative ones. (1 mark)
4. **Tunable Pressure:** The single parameter $\eta^+$ gives the designer an explicit, mathematically clean way to control the convergence speed. (1 mark)

**Effect of $\eta^+$ on Exploration vs Exploitation:**

- **Small $\eta^+$ (close to 1.0):** $E(i) \approx 1$ for all ranks — the GA behaves like *uniform random selection*. The algorithm performs extensive **exploration** of the search space, preserves high genetic diversity, and is unlikely to converge prematurely, but convergence will be slow. (1.5 marks)
- **Large $\eta^+$ (close to 2.0):** The best individual receives almost 2 copies on average per generation while the worst receives ≈ 0. The GA performs strong **exploitation** of the current best solutions, leading to fast convergence but increasing the risk of getting trapped in local optima (premature convergence). (1.5 marks)
- **Recommended range:** $\eta^+ \in [1.1,\,1.2]$ is widely used in practice as a balanced setting. **(Total: 7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Describe the **stagnation problem** in fitness-proportionate selection. Show with a small numerical example (a population of 4 individuals) how this problem is overcome by rank selection. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the expected count and selection probability for a population of 5 individuals with fitness values $\{2,\,8,\,14,\,5,\,11\}$ using Linear Rank Selection with $\eta^+ = 1.4$ and $\eta^- = 0.6$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Stagnation Problem Description (3 marks):**

In fitness-proportionate (Roulette-Wheel) selection, the selection probability of individual $i$ is:

$$
P_i = \dfrac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j}
$$

When the population has converged and most individuals have *very similar* fitness values, the *differences* $f_i - f_j$ become very small relative to $\sum f_j$. Consequently, every $P_i$ becomes nearly equal to $1/N$, and the wheel becomes nearly uniform. Selection degenerates into **almost random sampling**, and the GA can no longer apply any selective pressure. This is called **stagnation** (or *slow finishing*): the algorithm has converged in fitness, but the genetic operators are no longer making meaningful progress.

**Counter-example showing how rank selection overcomes it (4 marks):**

Consider a population of 4 individuals late in evolution with fitness values:

$$
f_1 = 0.991,\quad f_2 = 0.992,\quad f_3 = 0.993,\quad f_4 = 0.994
$$

**Roulette-Wheel probabilities:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= 0.991 / 3.970 = 0.2496 \\
P_2 &= 0.992 / 3.970 = 0.2499 \\
P_3 &= 0.993 / 3.970 = 0.2501 \\
P_4 &= 0.994 / 3.970 = 0.2504
\end{aligned}
$$

All probabilities are essentially **$1/4 = 0.25$** — the wheel is nearly uniform; selection is effectively random. **Stagnation has occurred.** (2 marks)

**Now apply Rank Selection** with $\eta^+ = 1.6$, $\eta^- = 0.4$, $N = 4$, $N-1 = 3$:

$$
E(i) = 1.6 - (1.2)\,\dfrac{i - 1}{3}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(1) &= 1.6 - 0 = 1.600 \;\Rightarrow\; P(1) = 0.400 \\
E(2) &= 1.6 - 0.4 = 1.200 \;\Rightarrow\; P(2) = 0.300 \\
E(3) &= 1.6 - 0.8 = 0.800 \;\Rightarrow\; P(3) = 0.200 \\
E(4) &= 1.6 - 1.2 = 0.400 \;\Rightarrow\; P(4) = 0.100
\end{aligned}
$$

Notice that even though the raw fitness values are nearly equal, the rank-based probabilities remain **well-separated** (0.40, 0.30, 0.20, 0.10), so the GA can still apply strong selective pressure. **Stagnation is prevented.** (2 marks) **(Total: 7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Sort the population in ascending order:**

Unsorted: $\{2,\;8,\;14,\;5,\;11\}$ → Sorted: $\{2,\;5,\;8,\;11,\;14\}$

| Rank $i$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fitness | 2 | 5 | 8 | 11 | 14 |

**Step 2 — Apply the formula** with $N = 5$, $N - 1 = 4$, $\eta^+ = 1.4$, $\eta^- = 0.6$, so $\eta^+ - \eta^- = 0.8$.

$$
E(i) = 1.4 - 0.8 \cdot \dfrac{i - 1}{4}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(1) &= 1.4 - 0.8 \cdot \dfrac{0}{4} = 1.4 - 0.00 = 1.4000 \\
E(2) &= 1.4 - 0.8 \cdot \dfrac{1}{4} = 1.4 - 0.20 = 1.2000 \\
E(3) &= 1.4 - 0.8 \cdot \dfrac{2}{4} = 1.4 - 0.40 = 1.0000 \\
E(4) &= 1.4 - 0.8 \cdot \dfrac{3}{4} = 1.4 - 0.60 = 0.8000 \\
E(5) &= 1.4 - 0.8 \cdot \dfrac{4}{4} = 1.4 - 0.80 = 0.6000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute selection probabilities** $P(i) = E(i)/5$:

| Rank $i$ | Fitness | $E(i)$ | $P(i)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | 1.4000 | 0.2800 |
| 2 | 5 | 1.2000 | 0.2400 |
| 3 | 8 | 1.0000 | 0.2000 |
| 4 | 11 | 0.8000 | 0.1600 |
| 5 | 14 | 0.6000 | 0.1200 |
| **Total** | — | **5.0000** | **1.0000** |

**Verification:** $\sum E(i) = 1.4 + 1.2 + 1.0 + 0.8 + 0.6 = 5.0 = N$ $\checkmark$ **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on Rank Selection Questions (Examiner's Pitfall Guide):**
>
> 1. **Inverted Ranking Direction (–2 marks typical):** Many students assign rank 1 to the BEST individual. The convention used in the linear ranking formula $\bigl(E(i) = \eta^+ - (\eta^+ - \eta^-)(i-1)/(N-1)\bigr)$ is **rank 1 = worst, rank $N$ = best**. Reversing this gives a wrong monotonic direction.
> 2. **Forgetting the constraint $\eta^+ + \eta^- = 2$ (–1 mark):** Always verify this constraint first. If the problem states only one parameter (e.g., "$\eta^+ = 1.6$"), infer the other from the constraint.
> 3. **Not summing the expected counts back to $N$ (–1 mark):** A self-check that costs one line of arithmetic and instantly validates your answer. Always do it.
> 4. **Confusing expected count $E(i)$ with selection probability $P(i)$ (–1 to –2 marks):** $E(i)$ is the *number of copies per generation* (dimensionless integer-ish, but often fractional); $P(i) = E(i)/N$ is a probability. State both clearly.
> 5. **Forgetting to sort the population first (–1 mark):** Rank Selection *requires* the population to be sorted. Applying $E(i)$ to the unsorted fitness vector is a fundamental conceptual error.
> 6. **In KTU 14-mark questions, failure to interpret the $\eta^+$ parameter semantically (–1 to –2 marks):** Just plugging in numbers gets you ≤ 5 of 7 marks on part (b). You MUST also discuss its effect on exploration/exploitation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary — Read This the Night Before the Exam!**

- **Definition:** Rank Selection assigns selection probability based on **rank order after sorting**, not on raw fitness.
- **Two Variants:** **Linear Ranking** (most common) and **Exponential Ranking** (less common in KTU).
- **Linear Ranking Formula:** $E(i) = \eta^{+} - (\eta^{+} - \eta^{-})\,\dfrac{i - 1}{N - 1}$.
- **Probability Form:** $P(i) = E(i)/N$.
- **Constraint:** $\eta^+ + \eta^- = 2$, with $1 \le \eta^+ \le 2$ and $0 \le \eta^- \le 1$.
- **Rank Convention:** $i = 1$ → **worst**; $i = N$ → **best**.
- **Sums:** $\sum E(i) = N$ and $\sum P(i) = 1$ (always verify).
- **Recommended Setting:** $\eta^+ = 1.1$ to $1.2$ (moderate, robust).
- **Why It Exists:** Solves **premature convergence** (super-fit domination) and **stagnation** (near-equal fitness).
- **Cost:** $O(N \log N)$ due to sorting — slightly more expensive than Roulette-Wheel's $O(N)$.
- **Bonus Property:** Works for **negative fitness values** without scaling — unlike Roulette-Wheel.
- **Sampling:** Usually combined with **Stochastic Universal Sampling (SUS)** to minimise sampling noise.
- **Comparison Tagline:** "Rank Selection = Roulette-Wheel with bounded, tunable pressure."
- **Common Numerical Trap:** Always check whether rank 1 is the *worst* or the *best* in the formula you are using.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steady State Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT411 - SOFT COMPUTING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Steady State Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Steady-State Selection** is a replacement strategy in Genetic Algorithms (GAs) — and more broadly in Evolutionary Algorithms (EAs) — in which only a small subset of the population (typically **1 to 2 individuals**, or a small fraction $\le 20\%$) is replaced by newly generated offspring in each iteration. The majority of the existing, high-fitness chromosomes are preserved, and the population is said to be in a "steady-state" because the chromosome pool is updated incrementally rather than wholesale.

Formally, at generation $t$ with population $P(t)$ of size $N$:

$$P(t+1) = \bigl[P(t) \setminus R(t)\bigr] \cup O(t)$$

where $R(t)$ is the set of removed individuals (replacers) and $O(t)$ is the newly produced offspring. The **Generation Gap ($G$)** is the fraction of population replaced:

$$G = \frac{\vert R(t) \vert}{N} = \frac{\vert O(t) \vert}{N}, \qquad 0 < G \ll 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Steady-State GA (SSGA) is explicitly contrasted with the **Generational GA (GGA)** in Module 4. In GGA, $G = 1.0$ (entire population replaced), while in SSGA, $G \in (0, 0.2]$. This distinction is a frequently tested concept in KTU Part A and Part B questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a classroom of 100 students where a teacher replaces only the **2 weakest performers** each week with 2 newly enrolled students who have just been trained. The top 98 students are never disturbed — they keep their seats, their friendships, and their accumulated knowledge.

- The **classroom** = the population
- The **weakest 2 students removed** = the replacement mechanism (`Replace-Worst` policy)
- The **2 newcomers** = offspring produced by crossover + mutation
- The **teacher** = the GA's replacement operator

> **Key Insight:** Because the elite majority is preserved, useful genetic material discovered earlier is **never lost** between generations. This is why SSGA exhibits faster convergence on stable fitness landscapes but risks premature convergence on deceptive multi-modal landscapes.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Nomenclature

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $N$ | Population size | $50$ to $200$ |
| $G$ | Generation Gap | $0.01$ to $0.20$ |
| $n_{off}$ | Offspring per generation | $1$ to $10$ |
| $p_{c}$ | Crossover probability | $0.6$ to $0.9$ |
| $p_{m}$ | Mutation probability | $0.001$ to $0.05$ |
| $\tau$ | Selection pressure | $1.0$ to $2.0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Generation Gap $G$** is the single most important tunable parameter in SSGA. A small $G$ yields elitist, slow exploration; a larger $G$ behaves more like a generational model.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Steps of Steady-State Selection (Step-by-Step Logic)

The SSGA loop executes the following structured sequence at **every iteration**:

1. **Selection of Parents:** Choose 2 (or more) parents from $P(t)$ using a selection scheme such as Fitness Proportional, Rank-Based, or Tournament Selection.
2. **Crossover Application:** Apply crossover operator with probability $p_c$ on the selected parents to produce offspring candidates.
3. **Mutation Application:** Apply mutation operator with probability $p_m$ on each offspring gene to maintain diversity.
4. **Replacement Decision:** Identify the individuals to be evicted from $P(t)$ using a **replacement policy** (Replace-Worst, Replace-Random, Replace-Oldest, or Tournament Replacement).
5. **Insertion:** Insert the new offspring into the slots vacated by the removed individuals, forming $P(t+1)$.
6. **Termination Check:** Repeat steps 1–5 until a stopping criterion is satisfied (max generations, convergence threshold, or fitness target).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **fundamental difference** from Generational GA is that in SSGA, the newly inserted offspring **immediately participate** in the selection of the *next* iteration's parents. There is no "intermediate population" buffer.

## 2.2 Replacement Policies — The Core Variants

### 2.2.1 Replace-Worst (RWS)
The $n_{off}$ individuals with the **lowest fitness** in $P(t)$ are removed and replaced by the offspring. This is the **most elitist** policy and is the canonical definition of "Steady-State Selection" in KTU textbooks.

- **Pros:** Guarantees monotonic non-decrease of best-so-far fitness.
- **Cons:** High risk of premature convergence in multi-modal problems.

### 2.2.2 Replace-Random (RRS)
$n_{off}$ individuals are chosen uniformly at random from $P(t)$ and replaced. No fitness information is used for the *eviction* decision.

- **Pros:** Maintains diversity; explores search space more broadly.
- **Cons:** May discard high-quality genetic material, slowing convergence.

### 2.2.3 Replace-Oldest (ROS)
The $n_{off}$ individuals that have lived the **longest** in the population (i.e., have the highest age counter) are replaced.

- **Pros:** Prevents any single chromosome from dominating forever; balanced exploration.
- **Cons:** Loses the strong elitist guarantees of RWS.

### 2.2.4 Tournament Replacement
A subset of $k$ individuals is randomly chosen, the worst among them is evicted, and the offspring takes its place.

- **Pros:** Stochastic, tunable pressure via $k$.
- **Cons:** Slightly higher per-iteration computational cost.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $G = \dfrac{n_{off}}{N}$ | Generation Gap | Dimensionless, $0 < G \le 1$ |
| 2 | $\bar{f}(t+1) = \dfrac{(N-n_{off})\bar{f}(t) + n_{off}\,\bar{f}_{off}}{N}$ | Mean fitness update rule | $n_{off}$ small |
| 3 | $f_{best}(t+1) \ge f_{best}(t)$ | Monotonicity (RWS only) | Strict under elitism |
| 4 | $p_{i,sel} = \dfrac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j}$ | Roulette-Wheel parent selection prob. | Requires $f_i > 0$ |
| 5 | $P(\text{replacement of } i) = \dfrac{N - \text{rank}(i) + 1}{\sum_{j=1}^{N}(N-j+1)}$ | Rank-based selection | Linear ranking |
| 6 | $P_{loss} = (1 - G)^t$ | Probability elite remains after $t$ iters | Geometric decay |
| 7 | $N_{eff} = \dfrac{N \cdot \bar{f}^2}{\overline{f^2}}$ | Effective population size | Diversity metric |
| 8 | $\tau = \dfrac{\bar{f}_{sel} - \bar{f}}{\sigma_f}$ | Selection intensity | Standardized fitness |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notation Rule:** In all exam answers, use the standard KTU convention — denote the *current* population as $P(t)$ and the *next* as $P(t+1)$. The Generation Gap $G$ is **always** italicized and treated as a real number in $(0, 1]$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Steady-State GAs are widely deployed in:

- **Real-time control systems** where the fitness function evaluates continuously and the system must adapt online (e.g., adaptive cruise control, robotic gait tuning).
- **Embedded optimization** with limited memory footprints, because only $n_{off} \ll N$ new solutions need to be stored per iteration.
- **Antenna design** and **VLSI floorplanning**, where evaluating each candidate is computationally expensive and incremental improvement is preferred.
- **Neuro-evolution** (evolving neural network weights), where the elite network weights must be preserved to avoid catastrophic forgetting.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mean-Fitness Update Equation

**Goal:** Derive how the mean population fitness evolves from $P(t)$ to $P(t+1)$ under Steady-State Selection with Replace-Worst policy.

**Given:**
- Population size: $N$ (fixed)
- Number of offspring inserted: $n_{off}$ (with $n_{off} \ll N$)
- Mean fitness of $P(t)$: $\bar{f}(t)$
- Mean fitness of offspring batch: $\bar{f}_{off}$

**Step 1 — Compute total fitness of $P(t)$:**

$$\text{Total}_t = N \cdot \bar{f}(t)$$

**Step 2 — Subtract the fitness of removed (worst) individuals:**

Under Replace-Worst, the $n_{off}$ weakest individuals are removed. Let their mean fitness be $\bar{f}_{worst}$. By definition of "worst":

$$\bar{f}_{worst} \le \bar{f}(t)$$

Total fitness after removal:

$$\text{Total}_{after\_removal} = N \cdot \bar{f}(t) - n_{off} \cdot \bar{f}_{worst}$$

**Step 3 — Add the fitness of newly inserted offspring:**

$$\text{Total}_{t+1} = N \cdot \bar{f}(t) - n_{off} \cdot \bar{f}_{worst} + n_{off} \cdot \bar{f}_{off}$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $N$ to obtain the new mean fitness:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{f}(t+1) &= \frac{N \cdot \bar{f}(t) - n_{off} \cdot \bar{f}_{worst} + n_{off} \cdot \bar{f}_{off}}{N} \\[6pt]
&= \bar{f}(t) + \frac{n_{off}}{N}\bigl(\bar{f}_{off} - \bar{f}_{worst}\bigr) \\[6pt]
&= \bar{f}(t) + G \cdot \bigl(\bar{f}_{off} - \bar{f}_{worst}\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Each iteration, the mean fitness improves by a quantity proportional to $G$ times the gap between offspring quality and worst-individual quality. This is the **defining dynamic equation** of SSGA convergence.

> [!NOTE]
> In the limiting case $G = 1$ (full replacement), this reduces to $\bar{f}(t+1) = \bar{f}_{off}$, which is precisely the Generational GA update rule.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Probability that an Elite Individual Survives $t$ Iterations

**Goal:** Compute the probability that a chromosome currently in $P(t)$ still exists in $P(t+k)$ after $k$ generations.

**Assumption:** Each iteration, the $n_{off}$ individuals selected for removal are chosen uniformly at random from the population (Replace-Random policy).

**Step 1 — Probability an individual survives ONE iteration:**

$$P(\text{survive 1 iter}) = 1 - \frac{n_{off}}{N} = 1 - G$$

**Step 2 — Probability the individual is NOT selected in any of $k$ iterations:**

Since removals across iterations are approximately independent (valid for $n_{off} \ll N$):

$$P(\text{survive } k \text{ iters}) = (1 - G)^k$$

**Step 3 — Special case: For RWS policy, the probability is 1 (the worst is always targeted, so an elite is never the eviction target if it is not in the bottom $n_{off}$).**

Thus, the survival curve is a **geometric decay** for RRS and a **step function** (1 then drops) for RWS.

**Numerical Example:** For $G = 0.1$ and $k = 10$ iterations:

$$P = (1 - 0.1)^{10} = 0.9^{10} \approx 0.3487$$

So only ~35% of the original elite survives 10 iterations under RRS.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Replacement Policies

| Property | Generational (GGA) | Steady-State RWS | Steady-State RRS | Steady-State ROS |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Generation Gap $G$ | $1.0$ | $\le 0.2$ | $\le 0.2$ | $\le 0.2$ |
| Best-fitness monotonicity | Not guaranteed | **Guaranteed** | Not guaranteed | Not guaranteed |
| Diversity preservation | High | Low | Medium-High | Medium |
| Risk of premature convergence | Low | **High** | Medium | Medium |
| Per-iteration cost | High ($N$ crossovers) | Low ($1$–$2$ crossovers) | Low | Low |
| Online adaptability | Poor | **Excellent** | Good | Good |
| Selection pressure | Tunable | Very high (elitist) | Tunable | Tunable |
| Use case | Off-line batch optimization | Real-time control | Multi-modal search | Dynamic environments |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Takeaway:** The choice of replacement policy directly modulates the **exploration–exploitation trade-off**. RWS maximizes exploitation; RRS maximizes exploration; ROS provides a balanced middle-ground.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A SSGA has $N = 50$, $n_{off} = 2$, current $\bar{f}(t) = 80$, $\bar{f}_{off} = 95$, $\bar{f}_{worst} = 40$. Compute the new mean fitness.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G &= \frac{n_{off}}{N} = \frac{2}{50} = 0.04 \\[4pt]
\bar{f}(t+1) &= \bar{f}(t) + G \cdot (\bar{f}_{off} - \bar{f}_{worst}) \\[4pt]
&= 80 + 0.04 \times (95 - 40) \\[4pt]
&= 80 + 0.04 \times 55 \\[4pt]
&= 80 + 2.2 \\[4pt]
&= 82.2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $\bar{f}(t+1) = 82.2$. The mean fitness increased by $2.2$ units, which is consistent with the small generation gap of $0.04$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SSGA Operational Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize Population P of size N] --> B[Evaluate Fitness of all individuals in P]
    B --> C{Termination criterion met?}
    C -- Yes --> Z[Output best individual and STOP]
    C -- No --> D[Select 2 parents from P using selection scheme]
    D --> E[Apply Crossover with probability pc to produce 2 offspring]
    E --> F[Apply Mutation with probability pm to each offspring gene]
    F --> G[Evaluate fitness of new offspring]
    G --> H[Identify n_off individuals to be removed using replacement policy]
    H --> I[Insert offspring into vacated slots to form P_new]
    I --> J[Set P = P_new and go to step C]
```

## 4.2 Comparison Block Diagram: Generational vs. Steady-State

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GGA["GENERATIONAL GA - Gap equals 1.0"]
        G1[P of N parents] --> G2[Select N parents]
        G2 --> G3[Crossover and Mutation on all N]
        G3 --> G4[Form offspring pool Q of size N]
        G4 --> G5[P becomes Q completely]
    end

    subgraph SSGA["STEADY STATE GA - Gap less than 0.2"]
        S1[P of N parents] --> S2[Select only 2 parents]
        S2 --> S3[Produce only 2 offspring]
        S3 --> S4[Remove n_off worst from P]
        S4 --> S5[Insert 2 offspring into P]
        S5 --> S6[P updated incrementally]
    end
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree for Choosing a Replacement Policy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the fitness landscape single-peaked and unimodal?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2[Use REPLACE WORST for fast elitist convergence]
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Are multiple optima of equal value expected?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4[Use REPLACE RANDOM for diversity]
    Q3 -- No --> Q5{Is the environment dynamic or changing over time?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6[Use REPLACE OLDEST to track moving optimum]
    Q5 -- No --> Q7[Use TOURNAMENT REPLACEMENT for tunable balance]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagrammatic Convention:** All Mermaid node IDs above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `G1`, `S4`, `Q1`) to comply with Mermaid v10+ parsing rules. No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as node identifiers.

## 4.4 Convergence Behavior Schematic (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph PHASE1["PHASE 1: EXPLORATION - Early generations"]
        P1A[High diversity in P] --> P1B[Many distinct fitness values]
        P1B --> P1C[Replace Random policy preferred]
    end
    subgraph PHASE2["PHASE 2: EXPLOITATION - Mid generations"]
        P2A[Convergence begins] --> P2B[Elite emerges clearly]
        P2B --> P2C[Replace Worst policy activated]
    end
    subgraph PHASE3["PHASE 3: REFINEMENT - Late generations"]
        P3A[Population clustered near optimum] --> P3B[Fitness improvements marginal]
        P3B --> P3C[Replace Oldest for final fine tuning]
    end
    PHASE1 --> PHASE2 --> PHASE3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Steady-State Selection in Genetic Algorithms. How does it differ from Generational Replacement?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Steady-State Selection is a replacement strategy in Genetic Algorithms where only a small number of offspring (typically 1 to 2, or at most 20% of the population) are produced and inserted into the population per iteration, replacing the least fit members. The defining feature is the **Generation Gap $G$** being very small ($0 < G \le 0.2$).

**Key difference from Generational Replacement:**
- In **Generational GA**, the **entire population** of size $N$ is replaced in each generation ($G = 1.0$). An intermediate offspring pool of size $N$ is created and replaces the parents wholesale.
- In **Steady-State GA**, only a **few individuals** are replaced ($G \le 0.2$), and the elite majority is preserved across generations. This gives SSGA **better elitism** and **lower per-iteration cost**, at the risk of **premature convergence**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing "Steady-State GA produces all offspring in one generation" — this is wrong. SSGA produces only 1–2 offspring per iteration, which is the *very definition* of steady-state.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three replacement policies used in Steady-State Genetic Algorithms and state one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Policy | Advantage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Replace-Worst (RWS)** | Guarantees monotonic improvement of best fitness (elitist). |
| 2 | **Replace-Random (RRS)** | Preserves population diversity; better for multi-modal landscapes. |
| 3 | **Replace-Oldest (ROS)** | Prevents any chromosome from dominating indefinitely; suitable for dynamic environments. |
| 4 | **Tournament Replacement** | Tunable selection pressure via tournament size $k$. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the Steady-State Genetic Algorithm (SSGA) in detail with its complete algorithmic steps. How is the Generation Gap $G$ computed, and what role does it play in controlling selection pressure?**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** SSGA is a variant of the standard GA in which only a small fraction of the population is replaced per iteration, while the elite majority is retained. This contrasts with the Generational GA where the entire population is replaced.

**Algorithmic Steps (4 marks):**
1. **Initialize** population $P(0)$ of $N$ random individuals.
2. **Evaluate** fitness $f(x)$ for every $x \in P(t)$.
3. **Check termination:** If max generations reached or fitness target achieved, stop.
4. **Select** 2 parents from $P(t)$ using fitness-proportional or tournament selection.
5. **Crossover** the parents with probability $p_c$ to produce 2 offspring.
6. **Mutate** each offspring gene with probability $p_m$.
7. **Evaluate** offspring fitness.
8. **Replace:** Remove the $n_{off}$ worst individuals from $P(t)$ and insert the offspring to form $P(t+1)$.
9. **Set** $t = t+1$ and return to step 3.

**Generation Gap formula (1 mark):**

$$G = \frac{n_{off}}{N}$$

**Role in controlling selection pressure (1 mark):** A smaller $G$ means stronger elitism (less turnover, higher pressure on the best), while a larger $G$ (approaching 1) approximates a Generational GA with weaker elitism but broader exploration.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare Replace-Worst, Replace-Random, and Replace-Oldest policies on the basis of: (i) elitism, (ii) diversity preservation, (iii) suitability for multi-modal problems. Use the mean-fitness update equation to justify your answer.**

**Model Solution:**

The mean-fitness update equation (derived in Section 3.1) is:

$$\bar{f}(t+1) = \bar{f}(t) + G \cdot \bigl(\bar{f}_{off} - \bar{f}_{worst}\bigr)$$

**(i) Elitism (2 marks):**
- **RWS:** Maximally elitist. $\bar{f}_{worst}$ is always the absolute minimum in the population, so $\bar{f}_{off} - \bar{f}_{worst}$ is maximal → largest fitness gain per iteration. Best-fitness is monotonically non-decreasing.
- **RRS:** No elitism. $\bar{f}_{worst}$ here is the mean of randomly chosen individuals, which on average equals $\bar{f}(t)$ → expected fitness gain is **zero** (no systematic improvement in mean).
- **ROS:** Moderate elitism. $\bar{f}_{worst}$ depends on age, not fitness, so eviction is partially decoupled from quality.

**(ii) Diversity Preservation (2 marks):**
- **RWS:** Lowest diversity. High-fitness individuals proliferate; low-fitness alleles are aggressively culled. Risk of premature convergence is **highest**.
- **RRS:** Highest diversity. Removal is uniform and fitness-independent; all fitness levels are equally vulnerable to eviction.
- **ROS:** Medium diversity. Decouples eviction from fitness, but old individuals (which may still be fit) get removed.

**(iii) Suitability for Multi-Modal Problems (2 marks):**
- **RWS:** **Poor** — converges to the first peak discovered and gets stuck.
- **RRS:** **Best** — preserves exploration, allowing the algorithm to sample multiple peaks.
- **ROS:** **Good** — provides a balance; good for problems where peaks shift over time.

**(1 mark) Verdict:** For unimodal problems use RWS; for multi-modal or unknown landscapes, use RRS; for dynamic problems use ROS.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse **selection** (picking parents) with **replacement** (evicting old individuals). These are two different stages of the GA loop. Marks are specifically awarded for clearly distinguishing the two. **Do not write "Replace-Worst removes the worst parents"** — parents have already been used; what is removed are members of the *current population* to make room for offspring.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Alternative]` (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the mean-fitness update equation for a Steady-State GA. Show that in the limit $G = 1$ it reduces to the Generational GA update.**

**Model Solution:**

**Setup (1 mark):** Let $N$ = population size, $n_{off}$ = number of offspring, $G = n_{off}/N$, $\bar{f}(t)$ = mean fitness of $P(t)$, $\bar{f}_{off}$ = mean fitness of offspring, $\bar{f}_{worst}$ = mean fitness of the $n_{off}$ worst individuals in $P(t)$.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Total fitness of $P(t)$ is $N \cdot \bar{f}(t)$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** After removing the $n_{off}$ worst, total fitness becomes $N \cdot \bar{f}(t) - n_{off} \cdot \bar{f}_{worst}$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** After inserting the $n_{off}$ offspring, total fitness is $N \cdot \bar{f}(t) - n_{off} \cdot \bar{f}_{worst} + n_{off} \cdot \bar{f}_{off}$.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Divide by $N$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{f}(t+1) &= \bar{f}(t) - \frac{n_{off}}{N} \bar{f}_{worst} + \frac{n_{off}}{N} \bar{f}_{off} \\[4pt]
&= \bar{f}(t) + G \cdot (\bar{f}_{off} - \bar{f}_{worst})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Limit case (1 mark):** Setting $G = 1$ (full replacement) and noting that under full replacement, $\bar{f}_{worst} = \bar{f}(t)$ (since the *entire* population is "the worst" relative to the new offspring pool):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{f}(t+1) &= \bar{f}(t) + 1 \cdot (\bar{f}_{off} - \bar{f}(t)) = \bar{f}_{off}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is exactly the Generational GA rule: the new population's mean fitness equals the offspring pool's mean fitness.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the role of Steady-State Selection in real-time and embedded optimization applications. Why is SSGA preferred over Generational GA in such scenarios?**

**Model Solution:**

**Real-time characteristics (2 marks):** In real-time systems (e.g., adaptive controllers, online tuning of robotic parameters), the system cannot pause execution to evaluate an entirely new generation. Decisions must be made continuously. SSGA's incremental nature aligns with this constraint.

**Why SSGA is preferred (5 marks):**

1. **Low per-iteration cost:** Only 1–2 crossovers and 2 fitness evaluations are needed per iteration, vs. $N$ evaluations in GGA. Critical when fitness evaluation is expensive (e.g., CFD simulations, hardware-in-the-loop tests).

2. **Immediate feedback:** New offspring are inserted into the active population immediately, so the system adapts within a single cycle — no "waiting for next generation."

3. **Memory efficiency:** No need to store an intermediate offspring pool of size $N$. Only $n_{off}$ new solutions need to be kept in memory at any time. This is vital for embedded systems with kilobytes of RAM.

4. **Monotonic improvement (RWS):** For safety-critical control, RWS guarantees the best solution never degrades — a hard requirement for stability proofs.

5. **Real-time deadline compliance:** Since $n_{off}$ is small and bounded, the wall-clock time per iteration is predictable, enabling worst-case execution time (WCET) analysis required in hard real-time systems.

**Examples (for context):** Adaptive PID tuning, neuro-evolution of robot locomotion policies, online path planning for autonomous vehicles, evolutionary antenna design with hardware-in-loop testing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A frequent error is stating that SSGA "does not use crossover or mutation." This is **incorrect**. SSGA uses the *same* variation operators as GGA — only the *replacement* mechanism differs. Marks will be deducted if the answer implies that variation operators are absent.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Definition:** Steady-State Selection replaces only a *small fraction* ($G \le 0.2$) of the population per iteration, preserving the elite majority.
- ⭐ **Generation Gap $G$:** Defined as $G = n_{off}/N$; controls the speed vs. stability trade-off.
- ⭐ **Four canonical policies:** Replace-Worst (RWS), Replace-Random (RRS), Replace-Oldest (ROS), Tournament Replacement.
- ⭐ **RWS = Elitist; RRS = Exploratory; ROS = Balanced; Tournament = Tunable.**
- ⭐ **Mean-fitness update:** $\bar{f}(t+1) = \bar{f}(t) + G \cdot (\bar{f}_{off} - \bar{f}_{worst})$ — memorize this for derivations.
- ⭐ **Limit case $G = 1$** → reduces to Generational GA: $\bar{f}(t+1) = \bar{f}_{off}$.
- ⭐ **Elite survival probability under RRS** = $(1 - G)^k$ after $k$ iterations (geometric decay).
- ⭐ **Best-fitness monotonicity** is *only* guaranteed under RWS (and other explicit elitist variants).
- ⭐ **Real-time / embedded systems** strongly favor SSGA due to bounded per-iteration cost and memory efficiency.
- ⭐ **Confusion traps to avoid:**
  - *Selection* ≠ *Replacement*. Selection picks parents; replacement evicts old individuals.
  - SSGA still uses crossover and mutation; only the replacement strategy differs from GGA.
  - Small $G$ ≠ no replacement. Even with $G = 0.02$, the algorithm still evolves; it just evolves slowly and conservatively.
  - "Steady-state" refers to the *population turnover rate*, not to a steady-state in the *dynamical systems* sense.
- ⭐ **Diagram to redraw in exams:** The SSGA operational flowchart (selection → crossover → mutation → replacement → loop) — a 6–7 box flowchart earns full marks in 7-mark questions.
- ⭐ **Numerical sanity check:** For typical $G = 0.04$ and a fitness gap of $50$, mean-fitness improvement per iteration is $\approx 2$ units — small but cumulatively significant over hundreds of iterations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crossover
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT411 - SOFT COMPUTING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Crossover

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
**Crossover (Recombination)** is a stochastic genetic operator used in **Genetic Algorithms (GAs)** that combines the genetic material of two parent chromosomes to produce one or more offspring. It is the primary **exploration mechanism** in GA, responsible for propagating high-quality "building blocks" (schemas) from the parent population into the next generation.

Mathematically, given two parent chromosomes $P_1$ and $P_2$ of length $L$, the crossover operator is a mapping:

$$\Gamma: \mathcal{C} \times \mathcal{C} \times \Omega \rightarrow \mathcal{C}$$

where $\mathcal{C}$ is the chromosome space and $\Omega$ is the randomness space (crossover points, masks, blending weights).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Crossover is **NOT** applied to every selected pair. A parameter called the **Crossover Probability ($P_c$)** decides whether two parents undergo recombination. Typical $P_c \in [0.6, 0.9]$.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of crossover like **breeding two champion racehorses**:
- Each parent horse has a "DNA blueprint" (the chromosome).
- The breeder picks a few "traits" (genes) from the father and the rest from the mother.
- The goal is to produce a foal (offspring) that inherits the **best of both parents** — the father's speed and the mother's stamina.
- However, breeding is **probabilistic** — sometimes the breeder decides *not* to mix them, simply cloning the parents.

This is exactly what crossover does in GA: it shuffles gene combinations to discover better solutions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is Crossover Critical?**
> 1. **Exploration of search space** — creates new gene combinations never seen before.
> 2. **Schema propagation** — preserves short, high-fitness patterns (the *Building Block Hypothesis* of Goldberg).
> 3. **Population diversity** — prevents premature convergence to local optima.
> 4. **Speed of convergence** — empirically converges 10x–100x faster than random search.

### Real-World Significance
Crossover is used in:
- **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)** route optimization
- **Job-Shop Scheduling** in manufacturing
- **Neural Network weight optimization** (neuro-evolution)
- **Antenna design, VLSI placement, robotic gait tuning**
- **Stock market portfolio optimization**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Crossover
The crossover operation follows a strict 5-step logic:

1. **Parent Selection** — Two parents $P_1$ and $P_2$ are selected from the mating pool (typically via Roulette Wheel or Tournament selection).
2. **Probability Check** — Generate a random number $r \in [0,1]$. If $r \leq P_c$, proceed; else, copy parents unchanged to the offspring pool.
3. **Cut-Point / Mask Generation** — Randomly determine where to "cut" or "mix" the chromosomes (one point, two points, or a bit-mask).
4. **Gene Exchange** — Swap, blend, or permute gene segments according to the chosen operator.
5. **Offspring Validation** — Ensure the offspring lies within the feasible region (e.g., valid permutation for TSP).

### 2.2 Types of Crossover Operators

#### A. Binary / Discrete Crossover (for binary-encoded chromosomes)

**(i) Single-Point Crossover**

$$O_1[i] = \begin{cases} P_1[i] & \text{if } i \leq k \\ P_2[i] & \text{if } i > k \end{cases} \quad ; \quad O_2[i] = \begin{cases} P_2[i] & \text{if } i \leq k \\ P_1[i] & \text{if } i > k \end{cases}$$

where $k \in \{1, 2, \ldots, L-1\}$ is the random cut-point.

**(ii) Two-Point / Multi-Point Crossover**

$$O_1[i] = \begin{cases} P_1[i] & \text{if } i \leq k_1 \text{ or } i > k_2 \\ P_2[i] & \text{if } k_1 < i \leq k_2 \end{cases}$$

**(iii) Uniform Crossover** (Bitwise independent mixing)

$$O_1[i] = \begin{cases} P_1[i] & \text{if } M_i = 1 \\ P_2[i] & \text{if } M_i = 0 \end{cases}$$

where the mask $M$ is a binary string with $M_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(0.5)$.

#### B. Real-Valued Crossover (for real-coded GAs)

**(i) Simple / Whole Arithmetic Crossover**

$$O_1 = \alpha \cdot P_1 + (1-\alpha) \cdot P_2$$
$$O_2 = (1-\alpha) \cdot P_1 + \alpha \cdot P_2$$

with $\alpha \in [0,1]$ (often drawn from $U(0,1)$).

**(ii) Single Arithmetic Crossover** — Apply blending to one randomly chosen gene only.

**(iii) BLX-$\alpha$ Crossover**

$$\text{Offspring} \in [\min(P_1,P_2) - \alpha \cdot d, \; \max(P_1,P_2) + \alpha \cdot d]$$

where $d = \vert P_1 - P_2 \vert$.

**(iv) Simulated Binary Crossover (SBX)** — Simulates single-point binary behavior on real numbers using polynomial distribution.

#### C. Permutation Crossover (for TSP / Sequencing problems)

**(i) Order Crossover (OX)** — Preserves relative order.
**(ii) Partially Mapped Crossover (PMX)** — Preserves absolute positions via mapping.
**(iii) Cycle Crossover (CX)** — Preserves cycles from parents.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Typical Value | Application |
|---|---------|----------------------|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | Crossover Probability | $P_c$ | $\mathbf{0.6 \text{ to } 0.9}$ | Decides if crossover occurs |
| 2 | Expected Crossovers | $E[N_{cross}] = N_{pop} \cdot P_c$ | $0.6 N_{pop}$ | Population-level metric |
| 3 | Single-Point Cut | $k \sim U(1, L-1)$ | Integer | Binary GA |
| 4 | Two-Point Cut | $k_1, k_2 \sim U(1,L-1)$ with $k_1 \leq k_2$ | Integer | Binary GA |
| 5 | Uniform Mask | $M_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(0.5)$ | $\Pr(M_i=1) = 0.5$ | Bitwise mixing |
| 6 | Arithmetic Blend | $O_1 = \alpha P_1 + (1-\alpha) P_2$ | $\alpha \in U(0,1)$ | Real-coded GA |
| 7 | BLX-$\alpha$ Range | $[\min - \alpha d, \; \max + \alpha d]$ | $\alpha \in [0,0.5]$ | Real-coded GA |
| 8 | Schemata Survival | $P(\text{schema survives}) \geq 1 - P_c \cdot \delta(H)$ | — | Schema theorem |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In VLSI design, arithmetic crossover tunes transistor widths continuously. In logistics, PMX/Order Crossover solves vehicle routing. The choice of operator directly determines solution feasibility and search efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Three Crossover Types on Binary Chromosomes

**Given Parents (Length $L = 8$):**

$$P_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
$$P_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

#### Example 1: Single-Point Crossover

**Step 1:** Generate random cut-point $k = 4$.

**Step 2:** Partition both parents at position 4:
- $P_1 = [1, 1, 0, 0 \mid 1, 0, 1, 1]$
- $P_2 = [1, 0, 1, 0 \mid 1, 1, 0, 0]$

**Step 3:** Exchange tail segments.

**Step 4:** Form offspring:

$$O_1 = [1, 1, 0, 0 \mid 1, 1, 0, 0] = 11001100$$
$$O_2 = [1, 0, 1, 0 \mid 1, 0, 1, 1] = 10101011$$

**Step 5:** Verify — Both $O_1$ and $O_2$ are valid 8-bit chromosomes. ✓

---

#### Example 2: Two-Point Crossover

**Step 1:** Generate cut-points $k_1 = 2$, $k_2 = 6$.

**Step 2:** Partition chromosomes:
- $P_1 = [1 \mid 1, 0, 0, 1, 0 \mid 1, 1]$
- $P_2 = [1 \mid 0, 1, 0, 1, 1 \mid 0, 0]$

**Step 3:** Exchange the middle segment.

**Step 4:** Form offspring:

$$O_1 = [1 \mid 0, 1, 0, 1, 1 \mid 1, 1] = 10101111$$
$$O_2 = [1 \mid 1, 0, 0, 1, 0 \mid 0, 0] = 11001000$$

**Step 5:** Verify fitness & feasibility. ✓

---

#### Example 3: Uniform Crossover

**Step 1:** Generate random mask $M = 0, 1, 1, 0, 1, 0, 0, 1$ (1 = take from $P_1$, 0 = take from $P_2$).

**Step 2:** Apply mask position-by-position using:

$$O_1[i] = M_i \cdot P_1[i] + (1 - M_i) \cdot P_2[i]$$

| Position $i$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| $P_1$ | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| $P_2$ | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| $M$ | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| $O_1$ | $P_2$=1 | $P_1$=1 | $P_1$=0 | $P_2$=0 | $P_1$=1 | $P_2$=1 | $P_2$=0 | $P_1$=1 |
| $O_2$ (complement mask) | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |

**Step 3:** Read off offspring:

$$O_1 = 1, 1, 0, 0, 1, 1, 0, 1 = 11001101$$
$$O_2 = 1, 0, 1, 0, 0, 0, 1, 0 = 10100010$$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Operator | Information Transmitted | Search Bias | Best Suited For | Disadvantage |
|----------|------------------------|-------------|------------------|--------------|
| Single-Point | Adjacent genes (positional bias) | Low disruption | Small-length binary strings | Creates tight linkage |
| Two-Point | Linked segments | Moderate | Long chromosomes | Position bias at edges |
| Uniform | Independent per-gene | High disruption | Discrete, independent features | May destroy building blocks |
| Arithmetic | Continuous blend | Smooth | Real-coded optimization | Limited to convex combinations |
| BLX-$\alpha$ | Extended range | Exploratory | Real-coded, multi-modal | May overshoot feasible region |
| Order (OX) | Relative order | Positional | TSP, scheduling | Complex to implement |
| PMX | Absolute position | Positional | Permutation problems | Mapping conflicts |

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Expected Number of Crossovers in a Population

Let $N_{pop}$ be the population size and $P_c$ the crossover probability. Each parent pair undergoes crossover with probability $P_c$.

**Step 1:** Number of parent pairs selected per generation:

$$N_{pairs} = \frac{N_{pop}}{2}$$

**Step 2:** Expected number of pairs undergoing crossover:

$$E[N_{cross}] = N_{pairs} \cdot P_c = \frac{N_{pop} \cdot P_c}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Number of offspring produced from crossovers (each crossover yields 2 offspring):

$$E[N_{cross\_off}] = 2 \cdot E[N_{cross}] = N_{pop} \cdot P_c$$

**Step 4:** Number of offspring that bypass crossover (cloned parents):

$$E[N_{clone}] = N_{pop} \cdot (1 - P_c)$$

**Step 5:** Total offspring (conservation check):

$$E[N_{cross\_off}] + E[N_{clone}] = N_{pop} \cdot P_c + N_{pop} \cdot (1 - P_c) = N_{pop} \quad \checkmark$$

This proves that the population size is conserved through the crossover-selection-mutation cycle.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Crossover's Position in the GA Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize Population of N chromosomes] --> B[Evaluate Fitness of every individual]
    B --> C[Selection Operator Roulette or Tournament]
    C --> D[Generate random number r in 0 to 1]
    D --> E{Is r less than or equal to Pc?}
    E -->|No| F[Clone parents directly to offspring pool]
    E -->|Yes| G[Apply Crossover Operator Single Two or Uniform]
    G --> H[Generate 2 offspring chromosomes]
    F --> I[Apply Mutation with probability Pm]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Form New Generation]
    J --> K{Convergence criterion satisfied?}
    K -->|No| B
    K -->|Yes| L[Return Best Solution Found]
```

### 4.2 Taxonomy of Crossover Operators

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Crossover Operator] --> B[Binary Discrete Crossover]
    A --> C[Real Valued Crossover]
    A --> D[Permutation Crossover]
    B --> B1[Single Point]
    B --> B2[Two Point]
    B --> B3[Multi Point]
    B --> B4[Uniform]
    C --> C1[Simple Arithmetic]
    C --> C2[Single Arithmetic]
    C --> C3[Whole Arithmetic]
    C --> C4[BLX Alpha]
    C --> C5[SBX Simulated Binary]
    D --> D1[Order Crossover OX]
    D --> D2[Partially Mapped PMX]
    D --> D3[Cycle Crossover CX]
    D --> D4[Edge Recombination]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Crossover Module

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Module]
        P1[Parent Chromosome P1]
        P2[Parent Chromosome P2]
        R[Random Source RNG]
    end
    subgraph CTRL[Control Module]
        PC[Probability Gate Pc]
        K[Cut Point or Mask Generator]
    end
    subgraph PROC[Processing Module]
        EX[Gene Exchange Engine]
        BL[Blending Engine for real coded]
        PERM[Permutation Resolver]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Module]
        O1[Offspring O1]
        O2[Offspring O2]
    end
    P1 --> EX
    P2 --> EX
    R --> PC
    PC --> K
    K --> EX
    K --> BL
    K --> PERM
    EX --> O1
    EX --> O2
    BL --> O1
    BL --> O2
    PERM --> O1
    PERM --> O2
```

### 4.4 Effect of Crossover Probability on Convergence (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Pc very low near 0] --> A1[Behavior: Almost pure cloning, slow exploration, may stagnate]
    B[Pc moderate 0.6 to 0.8] --> B1[Behavior: Balanced exploration, ideal GA behavior]
    C[Pc very high near 1] --> C1[Behavior: High disruption, may destroy good schemas, slower convergence]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
Define **Crossover** in Genetic Algorithms. List **any four** types of crossover operators used for binary-coded chromosomes.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Crossover is a stochastic recombination operator in GA that combines the genetic material of two parent chromosomes to produce offspring, enabling exploration of new regions in the search space. The four types of crossover operators for binary-coded chromosomes are: (i) Single-point crossover, (ii) Two-point (multi-point) crossover, (iii) Uniform crossover, and (iv) Shuffle crossover. *(1 mark for definition + 0.5 each × 4 = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
With a suitable example, explain **single-point crossover** and state the role of **crossover probability $P_c$**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In single-point crossover, a single cut-position $k$ is randomly chosen, and the gene segments beyond $k$ are exchanged between the two parents. **Example:** If $P_1 = 1010\textbar 1100$ and $P_2 = 0110\textbar 0010$, then offspring are $O_1 = 10100010$ and $O_2 = 01101100$. Crossover probability $P_c$ (typically $0.6$–$0.9$) determines whether two selected parents undergo crossover or are copied unchanged — controlling the exploration vs. exploitation balance. *(1.5 marks example + 1.5 marks $P_c$ role)*

---

### 📗 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — Internal Choice Provided)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **role of crossover** in a Genetic Algorithm. Discuss **single-point, two-point, and uniform crossover** with neat diagrams. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**
*Role of crossover (2 marks):* Crossover is the primary exploration operator in GA. It recombines high-fitness parents to propagate good "building blocks" (Schemata) into the next generation, ensuring the population explores new combinations of genes that may yield higher fitness. It is governed by crossover probability $P_c$.

*Single-point crossover (1.5 marks):* A cut-point $k \in \{1, ..., L-1\}$ is randomly chosen; genes after $k$ are swapped. This produces tightly linked gene segments. **Disadvantage:** Positional bias — terminal genes rarely get separated from their neighbours.

*Two-point crossover (1.5 marks):* Two cut-points $k_1$ and $k_2$ are chosen; the segment between them is swapped. This reduces positional bias and allows more flexible recombination of mid-chromosome genes.

*Uniform crossover (2 marks):* Each gene is independently chosen from either parent based on a randomly generated mask $M$ (50% probability per position). This provides the most disruptive crossover — every position has equal exchange probability, eliminating positional bias entirely. **Example:** $P_1 = 1100$, $P_2 = 1010$, $M = 0101 \Rightarrow O_1 = 1110$, $O_2 = 1000$.

---

**(b)** Two parent chromosomes of length 8 are given as $P_1 = 11001011$ and $P_2 = 10101100$. Perform: **(i)** Single-point crossover at $k = 3$, **(ii)** Two-point crossover at $k_1 = 2, k_2 = 6$, and **(iii)** Uniform crossover with mask $M = 01101001$. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Single-point crossover at $k = 3$ (2 marks):**

Partition: $P_1 = 110 \mid 01011$, $P_2 = 101 \mid 01100$.

After exchange: $O_1 = 11001100$ and $O_2 = 10101011$.

**(ii) Two-point crossover at $k_1 = 2, k_2 = 6$ (2.5 marks):**

Partition: $P_1 = 1 \mid 10010 \mid 11$, $P_2 = 1 \mid 01011 \mid 00$.

After exchange: $O_1 = 101011 00 = 10101100$, $O_2 = 110010 11 = 11001011$.

**(iii) Uniform crossover with mask $M = 01101001$ (2.5 marks):**

| Position | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| $P_1$ | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| $P_2$ | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| $M$ | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| $O_1$ | $P_2$=1 | $P_1$=1 | $P_1$=0 | $P_2$=0 | $P_1$=1 | $P_2$=1 | $P_2$=0 | $P_1$=1 |
| $O_2$ | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |

Result: $O_1 = 11001101$, $O_2 = 10100010$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss the **importance of crossover probability $P_c$**. How does it influence the convergence behaviour of a GA? Explain **arithmetic crossover** for real-coded GAs with a suitable example. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**
*Importance of $P_c$ (2.5 marks):* $P_c$ controls the **rate of genetic material exchange**. A high $P_c$ (close to 1) promotes rapid exploration but may destroy high-fitness chromosomes (excessive disruption). A low $P_c$ (close to 0) preserves elites but causes stagnation. Empirically, $P_c \in [0.6, 0.9]$ gives the best balance. Sensitivity studies by De Jong and Goldberg show convergence degrades sharply outside this range.

*Arithmetic crossover (3 marks):* Used in real-coded GAs. With blending weight $\alpha \in U(0,1)$:

$$O_1 = \alpha P_1 + (1-\alpha) P_2, \quad O_2 = (1-\alpha) P_1 + \alpha P_2$$

**Example:** $P_1 = (1.2, 3.4)$, $P_2 = (0.8, 2.9)$, $\alpha = 0.4$:

$$O_1 = (0.4 \cdot 1.2 + 0.6 \cdot 0.8, \; 0.4 \cdot 3.4 + 0.6 \cdot 2.9) = (0.96, \; 3.10)$$
$$O_2 = (0.6 \cdot 1.2 + 0.4 \cdot 0.8, \; 0.6 \cdot 3.4 + 0.4 \cdot 2.9) = (1.04, \; 3.20)$$

*Convergence influence (1.5 marks):* High $P_c$ → faster initial progress but higher variance; Low $P_c$ → slower but more stable convergence. Adaptive schemes (e.g., $P_c$ decreasing over generations) often outperform fixed values.

---

**(b)** Two parent tours for the Travelling Salesman Problem (7 cities) are:

$$P_1 = [3, 5, 2, 7, 1, 4, 6], \quad P_2 = [4, 1, 6, 2, 5, 3, 7]$$

Apply **Order Crossover (OX)** with cut-points at positions **2 and 5** to generate one offspring. Show all steps. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify cut-points $k_1 = 2$, $k_2 = 5$. Mark the segment to copy from $P_1$:

$$P_1 = [3, \underline{5, 2, 7, 1}, 4, 6]$$
$$P_2 = [4, \underline{1, 6, 2, 5}, 3, 7]$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Copy the segment from $P_1$ into the offspring at the same positions:

$$O = [\_, 5, 2, 7, 1, \_ , \_]$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Take the second parent $P_2$ and list its genes **starting from position $k_2+1 = 6$**, wrapping around to position 1:

Starting list: $3, 7, 4, 1, 6, 2, 5$ (starting from position 6 of $P_2$, wrapping to start).

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Eliminate genes already present in the offspring segment $\{5, 2, 7, 1\}$:

Filtered list: $3, 4, 6$ (removed 5, 2, 7, 1 from the cycle).

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Place the filtered genes into the empty positions, starting from position $k_2+1 = 6$ and wrapping:

- $O[6] = 3$
- $O[7] = 4$
- $O[1] = 6$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Final offspring:

$$O = [6, 5, 2, 7, 1, 3, 4]$$

**Verification:** Contains each city $\{1,2,3,4,5,6,7\}$ exactly once ✓ — valid tour.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Wrong cut-point convention (–1 mark):** Some students use 0-indexed while others 1-indexed. Always **state your convention explicitly**.
> 2. **Forgetting wrap-around in OX (–1 to –2 marks):** When filling from $P_2$, you MUST wrap around from position $L$ back to position 1.
> 3. **Confusing $P_c$ and $P_m$ (–0.5 mark):** $P_c$ is crossover, $P_m$ is mutation — never interchange.
> 4. **Not verifying offspring feasibility (–0.5 mark):** For permutation OX/PMX, the offspring MUST be a valid permutation — duplicates are NOT allowed.
> 5. **Skipping probability check:** Crossover is conditional on $P_c$ — failing to mention this loses a key conceptual mark.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Crossover = Exploration Operator** of GA (mutation = exploitation).
- ✅ Governed by **crossover probability $P_c \in [0.6, 0.9]$**; if $r > P_c$, parents are cloned.
- ✅ **Single-point** has positional bias; **Two-point** reduces it; **Uniform** removes it entirely.
- ✅ **Real-coded GAs** use **Arithmetic / BLX-$\alpha$ / SBX** crossover (no discrete cuts).
- ✅ **Permutation problems (TSP)** use **OX, PMX, CX** to maintain feasibility.
- ✅ **Schemata Theorem** (Goldberg): Short, low-order, high-fitness schemata grow exponentially under crossover + selection.
- ✅ **Expected offspring per generation:** $E[N_{cross\_off}] = N_{pop} \cdot P_c$.
- ✅ **Uniform mask bits** are independent Bernoulli(0.5) trials.
- ✅ **Arithmetic blend:** $O_1 = \alpha P_1 + (1-\alpha) P_2$, with $\alpha \sim U(0,1)$.
- ✅ **Permutation crossover MUST** preserve uniqueness of genes (no duplicates allowed).
- ✅ **High $P_c$** → exploration; **Low $P_c$** → exploitation. Adaptive $P_c$ schedules often outperform fixed values.
- ✅ **Crossover ≠ Mutation:** Crossover combines existing good genes; mutation introduces new genes.
- ✅ **Validity check is mandatory** for every offspring in TSP-style permutation problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mutation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT411 - SOFT COMPUTING TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Mutation # 🔬 MUTATION IN GENETIC ALGORITHMS — FOUNDATIONS ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) In the context of **Soft Computing Techniques** and **Evolutionary Algorithms**, **Mutation** is defined as a **stochastic unary genetic operator** that introduces **random alterations** to one or more gene positions (loci) in a chromosome, with the primary purpose of maintaining and restoring **genetic diversity** within a candidate population, thereby preventing premature convergence to local optima and enabling broader exploration of the solution search space. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Board Standard):** > Mutation is a background operator applied with a small probability $p_m$ (typically $0.001 \le p_m \le 0.1$) to each gene of a selected chromosome, producing a new offspring that carries a slight perturbation of the parent's genetic material — analogous to the biological phenomenon of genetic mutation by base-pair substitution, inversion, translocation, or frameshift. **Mathematical Notation:** $$\text{Mutation}: \mathcal{C} \to \mathcal{C}', \quad c = (g_1, g_2, \ldots, g_L) \mapsto c' = (g'_1, g'_2, \ldots, g'_L)$$ where $\mathcal{C}$ is the chromosome space, $L$ is the chromosome length, and at least one $g'_i \neq g_i$ with probability determined by the mutation policy. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **🎯 Real-World Analogy — "The Spice Shop Discovery"** Imagine you are a chef following a traditional family recipe passed down through generations. Every time you cook, you **cross over** (mix) ingredients from two successful dishes to create a new one. Now suppose you accidentally drop a pinch of **cinnamon** into a savory pasta sauce — that was *not* in the original recipe. The dish might taste terrible, **OR** it might become a revolutionary new cuisine. That accidental, small, random tweak is **mutation**. In a Genetic Algorithm: - **Crossover** = mixing two parent's ingredients (recombination of known good traits) - **Mutation** = the accidental new ingredient (random discovery of new traits) Without mutation, the recipe pool would eventually become "inbred" — all dishes would taste the same (premature convergence). Mutation keeps the menu diverse and allows the discovery of brand-new flavors (global optimum). --- ## 1.3 Role in the Genetic Algorithm Pipeline The mutation operator occupies a critical position between the **Selection** and **Replacement** stages: | Stage | Operator | Role | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Selection** | Picks parents based on fitness | | 2 | **Crossover** | Combines parent genes (exploitation) | | 3 | **Mutation** | Perturbs offspring genes (exploration) | | 4 | **Replacement** | Forms next generation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exploration vs Exploitation Balance:** Crossover performs *exploitation* of known high-fitness building blocks, while mutation performs *exploration* of new, unseen regions of the search space. Mutation is the **primary engine of exploration** in a GA. --- ## 1.4 Physical & Engineering Constants Used - **Mutation Rate ($p_m$)**: small probability per gene, $p_m \in [0.001, 0.1]$ - **Chromosome Length ($L$)**: number of genes per individual - **Gene Domain Bounds**: $x_{\min} \le g_i \le x_{\max}$ (for real-coded GAs) - **Population Size ($N$)**: number of individuals in each generation - **Standard Bäck Recommendation**: $p_m = \frac{1}{L}$ (inversely proportional to chromosome length) # 🧠 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — TYPES, MATHEMATICS & MECHANICS ## 2.1 Classification of Mutation Operators Mutation operators are broadly classified according to the **encoding scheme** of the chromosome (binary, integer, real-valued, permutation). ### A. Binary-Encoded Mutation Operators #### 2.1.1 Bit-Flip Mutation (Standard Binary Mutation) For each gene $g_i \in \{0, 1\}$, a uniform random number $r \sim U(0,1)$ is drawn: $$g'_i = \begin{cases} 1 - g_i & \text{if } r < p_m \\ g_i & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ This is the **canonical mutation** for binary GAs (Holland, 1975). #### 2.1.2 Random Resetting (Bit-Flip with Re-roll) Each gene is independently re-sampled from $\{0,1\}$ with probability $p_m$. Mathematically equivalent to bit-flip for binary alphabets but generalizes naturally to non-binary discrete alphabets. #### 2.1.3 Inversion Mutation A random substring $[a, b]$ of the chromosome is selected and reversed: $$c = (g_1, \ldots, g_{a-1}, \boxed{g_a, g_{a+1}, \ldots, g_b}, g_{b+1}, \ldots, g_L)$$ $$c' = (g_1, \ldots, g_{a-1}, \boxed{g_b, g_{b-1}, \ldots, g_a}, g_{b+1}, \ldots, g_L)$$ ### B. Permutation-Encoded Mutation (Combinatorial Problems) #### 2.1.4 Swap (Transposition) Mutation Two random positions $i, j$ are selected and their genes exchanged: $$c' = (g_1, \ldots, g_i, \ldots, g_j, \ldots, g_L) \to (g_1, \ldots, g_j, \ldots, g_i, \ldots, g_L)$$ #### 2.1.5 Scramble Mutation A random substring is selected and its genes are randomly permuted: $$c' = (g_1, \ldots, \text{permuted}(g_a, \ldots, g_b), \ldots, g_L)$$ ### C. Real-Encoded (Continuous) Mutation Operators #### 2.1.6 Gaussian Mutation $$g'_i = g_i + \sigma \cdot \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$$ where $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$ is the standard normal distribution and $\sigma$ is the **mutation step size** (standard deviation). The gene is clamped to $[x_{\min}, x_{\max}]$ if it exceeds bounds. #### 2.1.7 Uniform Mutation $$g'_i = x_{\min} + r \cdot (x_{\max} - x_{\min}), \quad r \sim U(0,1)$$ applied with probability $p_m$ per gene. #### 2.1.8 Polynomial Bounded Mutation (Deb & Agrawal, 1999) A perturbation $\delta$ is sampled from the polynomial distribution: $$\delta = \begin{cases} (2u)^{1/(\eta+1)} - 1, & u < 0.5 \\ 1 - [2(1-u)]^{1/(\eta+1)}, & u \ge 0.5 \end{cases}$$ where $u \sim U(0,1)$ and $\eta$ is the **distribution index** (typically 20–100). The new gene is: $$g'_i = g_i + \delta \cdot (x_{\max} - x_{\min})$$ #### 2.1.9 Non-Uniform Mutation (Michalewicz, 1992) $$g'_i = \begin{cases} g_i + \Delta(t, x_{\max} - g_i), & \text{if random bit } = 0 \\ g_i - \Delta(t, g_i - x_{\min}), & \text{if random bit } = 1 \end{cases}$$ where the perturbation function $\Delta(t, y)$ returns a value in $[0, y]$ that tends to $0$ as generations $t$ increase: $$\Delta(t, y) = y \cdot \left(1 - r^{(1 - t/T)^b}\right)$$ Here $r \sim U(0,1)$, $T$ is the maximum generation count, and $b$ is a shape parameter (typically 2–5). --- ## 2.2 The Mutation Rate — Critical Hyperparameter ### 2.2.1 Theoretical Analysis of $p_m$ The **expected number of mutated genes** per chromosome is: $$E[\text{mutations per chromosome}] = L \cdot p_m$$ The **probability of no mutation** occurring on a single chromosome is: $$P(\text{no mutation}) = (1 - p_m)^L$$ The **probability of exactly $k$ mutations** on a chromosome follows the **binomial distribution**: $$P(K = k) = \binom{L}{k} p_m^k (1 - p_m)^{L-k}$$ ### 2.2.2 Choice of $p_m$ — Empirical & Theoretical Guidance | Mutation Rate $p_m$ | Behaviour | Risk | |---|---|---| | $p_m \to 0$ | Pure exploitation; no diversity | Premature convergence | | $p_m = 1/L$ (Bäck's rule) | Balanced | Optimal for most problems | | $p_m \to 1$ | Pure random search | Loss of learned information | | $0.01 \le p_m \le 0.1$ | High exploration | Useful for deceptive landscapes | --- ## 2.3 Schema Theorem — The Role of Mutation > [!NOTE] > **Holland's Schema Theorem with Mutation** Let $H$ be a schema with order $o(H)$ (number of fixed positions) and defining length $\delta(H)$. The expected number of instances of $H$ in the next generation is: $$E[m(H, t+1)] \ge m(H,t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left[ 1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{L-1} - o(H) \cdot p_m \right]$$ where $p_c$ is the crossover probability and $f(H)$ is the mean fitness of schema instances. **Mutation** disrupts schema instances with probability $\approx o(H) \cdot p_m$. This shows that: - **Low-order schemata** (small $o(H)$) are more robust to mutation. - **High $p_m$** destroys short, high-fitness schemata → harms convergence. - **Excessively low $p_m$** → no exploration of new schema combinations. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Mutation Formulas — KTU 2024 Module 4** | **#** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Application** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $g'_i = 1 - g_i$ (if $r < p_m$) | Bit-flip mutation | Binary GA | | 2 | $g'_i = g_i + \sigma \mathcal{N}(0,1)$ | Gaussian mutation | Real-coded GA | | 3 | $g'_i = x_{\min} + r(x_{\max} - x_{\min})$ | Uniform mutation | Real-coded GA | | 4 | $\Delta(t,y) = y(1 - r^{(1-t/T)^b})$ | Non-uniform mutation | Time-varying mutation | | 5 | $E[\text{mutations}] = L \cdot p_m$ | Expected mutations per chromosome | Statistical analysis | | 6 | $P(K=k) = \binom{L}{k}p_m^k(1-p_m)^{L-k}$ | Binomial mutation count | Probabilistic analysis | | 7 | $P_{\text{disrupt}}(H) \approx 1 - (1-p_m)^{o(H)}$ | Schema disruption prob. | Schema theorem | | 8 | $p_m = 1/L$ | Bäck's recommended rate | Rule of thumb | | 9 | $P(\text{no mutation}) = (1-p_m)^L$ | Chromosome unchanged | Convergence test | > **Engineering Utility:** Mutation operators are central to real-world **neural architecture search (NAS)**, **feature selection in ML**, **PID controller tuning**, **job-shop scheduling**, **VLSI circuit layout**, **antenna design optimization**, and **structural engineering optimization** — wherever the search space is non-convex and gradient information is unavailable. --- ## 2.5 Adaptive Mutation Strategies Static $p_m$ may be sub-optimal. **Adaptive mutation** dynamically adjusts $p_m$ based on population convergence: $$p_m(t+1) = p_m(t) + \alpha \cdot \left( \frac{1}{L} \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma_P(t) + \epsilon} - p_m(t) \right)$$ where $\sigma_P(t)$ is the population's standard deviation of fitness and $\alpha$ is a learning rate. As the population converges ($\sigma_P \to 0$), $p_m$ is increased to maintain diversity. # 📐 STEP-BY-STEP ANALYTICAL DERIVATIONS ## 3.1 Derivation: Expected Number of Mutations per Generation > [!NOTE] > **Problem Setup:** A GA has a population of $N$ chromosomes, each of length $L$ bits, with per-gene mutation rate $p_m$. Find the expected number of mutated genes per generation. ### Step 1 — Define the Random Variable Let $X_{ij}$ be the indicator random variable for mutation at gene $j$ of chromosome $i$: $$X_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if gene } j \text{ of chromosome } i \text{ is mutated} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ with $P(X_{ij} = 1) = p_m$. ### Step 2 — Expected Value of a Single Indicator By definition of expectation of a Bernoulli random variable: $$E[X_{ij}] = 1 \cdot p_m + 0 \cdot (1 - p_m) = p_m$$ ### Step 3 — Total Mutations per Generation The total number of mutations in the entire population is: $$X_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{L} X_{ij}$$ ### Step 4 — Apply Linearity of Expectation $$E[X_{\text{total}}] = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{L} E[X_{ij}] = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{L} p_m = N \cdot L \cdot p_m$$ ### Step 5 — Final Closed-Form Result $$\boxed{E[X_{\text{total}}] = N \cdot L \cdot p_m}$$ > **Interpretation:** For a population of 100 chromosomes of length 20 bits with $p_m = 0.01$, the expected number of mutations per generation is $100 \times 20 \times 0.01 = 20$ bit-flips per generation. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Probability of Schema Disruption by Mutation > [!NOTE] > **Problem Setup:** A schema $H$ has order $o(H)$ — i.e., $o(H)$ fixed positions. Each of these $o(H)$ positions can be disrupted by mutation. Compute the probability that mutation destroys the schema. ### Step 1 — Independence of Gene-Level Mutations Each gene mutates independently with probability $p_m$. The probability that a *fixed* position remains *unmutated* is $(1 - p_m)$. ### Step 2 — All Fixed Positions Must Survive For the schema $H$ to survive (not be disrupted), **all** $o(H)$ fixed positions must remain unchanged: $$P(\text{schema survives}) = (1 - p_m)^{o(H)}$$ ### Step 3 — Disruption Probability $$P(\text{schema disrupted}) = 1 - P(\text{schema survives}) = 1 - (1 - p_m)^{o(H)}$$ ### Step 4 — Binomial Expansion (for small $p_m$) Using the binomial expansion $(1-p_m)^{o(H)} = 1 - o(H) p_m + \binom{o(H)}{2} p_m^2 - \ldots$ and truncating at first order (since $p_m$ is small): $$P(\text{schema disrupted}) \approx o(H) \cdot p_m \quad \text{(for small } p_m\text{)}$$ ### Step 5 — Numerical Example Let $L = 50$, $p_m = 0.01$, $o(H) = 5$: $$P(\text{disruption}) = 1 - (1 - 0.01)^5 = 1 - (0.99)^5$$ $$= 1 - 0.95099 = 0.04901 \approx 4.9\%$$ $$\text{vs. approximation: } 5 \times 0.01 = 5\%$$ The first-order approximation is accurate to within 0.1% for $p_m \le 0.05$. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Convergence of Bit-Flip Mutation Operator > [!NOTE] > **Problem Setup:** Show that the bit-flip mutation operator is **ergodic** — i.e., given any chromosome $c_1$ and any target chromosome $c_2$, there exists a non-zero probability of reaching $c_2$ from $c_1$ via mutation in a single step. ### Step 1 — Hamming Distance Let $d(c_1, c_2)$ be the Hamming distance — the number of positions at which $c_1$ and $c_2$ differ. To transform $c_1$ into $c_2$, we must flip exactly those $d$ differing positions. ### Step 2 — Single-Step Transformation Probability Each of the $L$ genes independently mutates with probability $p_m$.
