$$v_1 + v_3 = 30 + 16 = 46, \quad w_1 + w_3 = 6 + 4 = 10 \le W \;\checkmark$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: GA for TSP (3-City Subset)

**Problem:** 4 cities with coordinates:

| City | $x$ | $y$ |
|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | 0 |
| B | 3 | 0 |
| C | 3 | 4 |
| D | 0 | 4 |

**Step 1: Compute Distances**

$$d(A,B) = 3, \quad d(B,C) = 4, \quad d(C,D) = 3, \quad d(D,A) = 4$$
$$d(A,C) = 5, \quad d(B,D) = \sqrt{9+16} = 5$$

**Step 2: Evaluate a Few Tours**

| Tour | Distance $D$ | Fitness $1/D$ |
|---|---|---|
| A → B → C → D → A | $3+4+3+4 = 14$ | $0.0714$ |
| A → B → D → C → A | $3+5+3+5 = 16$ | $0.0625$ |
| A → C → B → D → A | $5+4+5+4 = 18$ | $0.0556$ |
| A → D → C → B → A | $4+3+4+3 = 14$ | $0.0714$ |

**Step 3: Identify Optimum**

The optimal tour has length **14** (there are two equivalent optima: A-B-C-D and A-D-C-B). GA will converge to one of these.

**Step 4: GA Operators for TSP**

If ordinary one-point crossover is applied to two parent tours:

- Parent 1: $[A, B, C, D]$
- Parent 2: $[A, C, B, D]$
- Offspring (cut after position 1, fill from parent 2): $[A, B, B, D]$ — **INVALID** (B repeated, C missing).

This is why TSP requires **Order Crossover (OX)** or **Partially Mapped Crossover (PMX)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **OX Procedure:** Select a substring from one parent, then fill remaining positions with the order they appear in the other parent.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generalized GA Application Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Problem Definition] --> P1[Encoding Strategy Selection]
    P1 --> P2[Initial Population Generation]
    P2 --> P3[Evaluate Fitness Function]
    P3 --> P4{Convergence Met?}
    P4 -->|No| P5[Selection Operator]
    P5 --> P6[Crossover Operator]
    P6 --> P7[Mutation Operator]
    P7 --> P8[New Generation]
    P8 --> P3
    P4 -->|Yes| P9[Decode Best Chromosome]
    P9 --> P10[Report Solution]
```

**Explanation of Pipeline:**

- **Encoding Strategy Selection** chooses binary, real-valued, permutation, or tree-based representation.
- **Convergence Check** uses a fitness threshold or maximum generation limit.
- **The loop** from Selection through New Generation represents one complete *generation*.

---

### 4.2 GA Operator Interaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Parent Pool] --> B[Selection Module]
    B --> C[Mating Pool]
    C --> D[Crossover Module]
    D --> E[Offspring Pool]
    E --> F[Mutation Module]
    F --> G[Mutated Offspring]
    G --> H[Fitness Evaluation]
    H --> I[Replacement Strategy]
    A -.-> I
    I --> J[Next Generation]
```

**Explanation:**

- The **Selection Module** uses roulette wheel, tournament, or rank-based methods.
- **Crossover** combines genetic material; for TSP, PMX/OX is used; for continuous problems, SBX.
- **Mutation** introduces diversity — bit-flip for binary, Gaussian perturbation for real-coded.
- **Replacement** decides whether to use generational, steady-state, or elitist replacement.

---

### 4.3 Application Domain Mapping

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Optimization
        A1[Function Optimization]
        A2[TSP]
        A3[Knapsack]
    end
    subgraph Scheduling
        B1[Job Shop]
        B2[Timetabling]
        B3[Resource Allocation]
    end
    subgraph MachineLearning
        C1[Neural Net Weights]
        C2[Feature Selection]
        C3[Rule Mining]
    end
    subgraph ControlSystems
        D1[Fuzzy Controller Tuning]
        D2[PID Gain Scheduling]
        D3[Robot Path Planning]
    end
    E[Genetic Algorithm Core] --> Optimization
    E --> Scheduling
    E --> MachineLearning
    E --> ControlSystems
```

**Explanation:** This block diagram illustrates the versatility of a single GA core. The same algorithmic framework (selection, crossover, mutation) serves all four domains — only the **encoding**, **fitness**, and **custom operators** change.

---

### 4.4 Convergence Behavior Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Generation 0: High Diversity, Low Fitness] --> B[Early Generations: Rapid Fitness Gain]
    B --> C[Mid Generations: Exploration vs Exploitation Balance]
    C --> D[Late Generations: Convergence, Low Diversity]
    D --> E[Final: Near-Optimal Solution]
```

**Explanation:**

- **Early generations** see rapid fitness improvement as poor solutions are eliminated.
- **Mid generations** balance exploration (mutation) and exploitation (crossover).
- **Late generations** see population convergence — diversity drops, and the algorithm may suffer from **premature convergence** if mutation is not sufficient.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Explain why permutation encoding is mandatory for the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) when using a Genetic Algorithm. Why does binary one-point crossover fail here?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Permutation encoding represents each chromosome as an ordered sequence of unique city IDs (e.g., [3, 1, 4, 2, 5]). This is mandatory because the TSP solution is a **Hamiltonian cycle** — every city must appear exactly once. Binary one-point crossover fails because it can produce **invalid offspring** with repeated or missing cities. For example, crossing [1,2,3,4] and [3,1,4,2] at position 2 gives [1,2,4,2] — city 2 is duplicated and city 3 is missing. Hence, TSP requires specialized operators like **Order Crossover (OX)** or **Partially Mapped Crossover (PMX)** that preserve permutation validity. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*Define the fitness function for the 0/1 Knapsack Problem using the penalty method. State the role of the penalty coefficient $\lambda$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The fitness function for the 0/1 Knapsack Problem using the penalty method is:

$$f(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i x_i - \lambda \cdot \max\left(0, \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i - W\right)$$

where $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$ indicates whether item $i$ is included, $v_i$ and $w_i$ are the value and weight of item $i$, $W$ is the knapsack capacity, and $\lambda$ is the **penalty coefficient**. The role of $\lambda$ is to heavily penalize chromosomes that violate the capacity constraint, thereby driving the GA toward feasible solutions. A small $\lambda$ allows infeasible solutions to dominate, while a large $\lambda$ makes the search overly restrictive. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Case Study: GA for Travelling Salesman Problem**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Describe the complete GA framework for solving the TSP for 5 cities. Specify the encoding scheme, fitness function, and explain the Order Crossover (OX) operator with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the distance matrix below for 4 cities, demonstrate one generation of GA with population size 2. Show the selection, crossover, and mutation steps, and compute the fitness of parent and offspring chromosomes. **(7 marks)**

| | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | 10 | 15 | 20 |
| B | 10 | 0 | 35 | 25 |
| C | 15 | 35 | 0 | 30 |
| D | 20 | 25 | 30 | 0 |

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — GA Framework for TSP (7 marks):**

1. **Encoding (2 marks):** Use *permutation encoding*. Each chromosome is a sequence of the $n$ city IDs in the order they are visited. Example: For $n = 5$, chromosome $[3, 1, 5, 2, 4]$ means: start at city 3, then 1, 5, 2, 4, and return to 3.

2. **Fitness Function (2 marks):** Since TSP is a minimization problem, the fitness is defined as the inverse of the total tour distance:
$$f(x) = \frac{1}{D(x)} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} d(c_i, c_{i+1}) + d(c_n, c_1)}$$
Higher fitness = shorter tour = better solution.

3. **Order Crossover (OX) (3 marks):**
   - **Step 1:** Select a random substring from Parent 1.
   - **Step 2:** Mark these positions as filled.
   - **Step 3:** From Parent 2, copy the remaining cities in their original order, skipping those already in the substring.
   - **Example:**
     - Parent 1: $[1, 2, 3, 4, 5]$, Parent 2: $[3, 4, 5, 1, 2]$
     - Select substring positions 2–3 from Parent 1: $[2, 3]$
     - From Parent 2, sequence is $[3, 4, 5, 1, 2]$; remove 2 and 3, leaving $[4, 5, 1]$
     - Offspring: $[4, 5, 1, 2, 3]$ — valid permutation.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Demonstration (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Initial Population (1 mark)**
- Parent 1 (P1): $[A, B, C, D]$
- Parent 2 (P2): $[A, C, B, D]$

**Step 2: Compute Parent Fitness (2 marks)**
- $D(P1) = d(A,B) + d(B,C) + d(C,D) + d(D,A) = 10 + 35 + 30 + 20 = 95$
- $D(P2) = d(A,C) + d(C,B) + d(B,D) + d(D,A) = 15 + 35 + 25 + 20 = 95$
- Fitness: $f(P1) = f(P2) = 1/95 \approx 0.0105$

**Step 3: Selection (1 mark)**
Tournament selection with $k=2$: both have equal fitness, so randomly select P1 and P2.

**Step 4: Order Crossover (2 marks)**
- Substring (positions 1–2) from P1: $[A, B]$
- From P2 sequence $[A, C, B, D]$, remove $A, B$, leaving $[C, D]$
- Offspring 1: $[C, D, A, B]$

**Step 5: Mutation (1 mark)**
Swap mutation on Offspring 1: swap positions 1 and 3: $[A, D, C, B]$

**Step 6: Compute Offspring Fitness (1 mark)**
- $D(\text{Offspring}) = d(A,D) + d(D,C) + d(C,B) + d(B,A) = 20 + 30 + 35 + 10 = 95$
- $f(\text{Offspring}) = 1/95 \approx 0.0105$

**Conclusion:** The fitness is the same in this small example; the GA will require multiple generations to find a better tour.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Case Study: GA for Continuous Function Optimization**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the limitations of binary encoding for continuous optimization problems. Describe real-coded GA and its advantages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the real-coded GA to find the maximum of $f(x) = x \sin(10\pi x) + 1$ in the interval $x \in [0, 1]$ for 2 generations. Use population size = 4, tournament selection ($k=2$), simulated binary crossover (SBX), and polynomial mutation. Show all steps. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Binary vs Real-Coded GA (7 marks):**

1. **Limitations of Binary Encoding (3 marks):**
   - **Hamming Cliff:** Small changes in the variable may require flipping many bits. For example, 0111 (7) → 1000 (8) requires changing 4 bits.
   - **Precision Loss:** The number of bits $L$ determines resolution $\Delta = (b-a)/2^L$. Achieving high precision requires long chromosomes.
   - **Scale Mismatch:** For variables with different ranges, fixed bit allocation is inefficient.
   - **Computational Overhead:** Encoding/decoding at every step adds cost.

2. **Real-Coded GA (2 marks):** Each chromosome is a vector of real numbers, $x = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ where $x_i \in [a_i, b_i]$. The search operates directly in the continuous domain.

3. **Advantages (2 marks):**
   - No encoding/decoding overhead.
   - High precision (limited only by floating-point representation).
   - Specialized operators like **SBX** and **polynomial mutation** respect the real-valued structure.
   - Faster convergence for smooth landscapes.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Optimization (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Random Initial Population (1 mark)**
Let $P_0 = \{0.20, 0.45, 0.70, 0.85\}$.

**Step 2: Fitness Evaluation (1 mark)**
$$f(x) = x \sin(10\pi x) + 1$$

| Individual | $x$ | $\sin(10\pi x)$ | $f(x)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $x_1$ | 0.20 | $\sin(2\pi) = 0$ | 1.00 |
| $x_2$ | 0.45 | $\sin(4.5\pi) = -1$ | 0.55 |
| $x_3$ | 0.70 | $\sin(7\pi) = 0$ | 1.00 |
| $x_4$ | 0.85 | $\sin(8.5\pi) = 1$ | 1.85 |

**Step 3: Tournament Selection (1 mark)**
- Tournament 1: $x_1$ (1.00) vs $x_2$ (0.55) → winner $x_1$
- Tournament 2: $x_3$ (1.00) vs $x_4$ (1.85) → winner $x_4$
- Mating pool: $\{0.20, 0.85\}$

**Step 4: SBX Crossover (2 marks)**
For parents $p_1 = 0.20$, $p_2 = 0.85$, with $\eta_c = 2$:
Using a simple averaging for illustration:
$$c_1 = 0.5(p_1 + p_2) = 0.525, \quad c_2 = 0.5(p_1 + p_2) - 0.1 = 0.425$$

**Step 5: Polynomial Mutation (1 mark)**
Mutate $c_2 = 0.425$ by adding $\delta = 0.05$: $c_2' = 0.475$.

**Step 6: New Generation Evaluation (1 mark)**
- $f(0.525) = 0.525 \sin(5.25\pi) + 1 = 0.525 \cdot 1 + 1 = 1.525$
- $f(0.475) = 0.475 \sin(4.75\pi) + 1 = 0.475 \cdot (-1) + 1 = 0.525$

**New Population:** $\{0.20, 0.85, 0.525, 0.475\}$

**Best fitness after 2 generations:** $f(0.85) = 1.85$.

> [!NOTE]
> The true maximum of $f(x) = x \sin(10\pi x) + 1$ in $[0,1]$ is approximately $1.85$, attained at $x \approx 0.85$. The GA converges toward this value.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Using standard binary crossover for TSP** — the examiner will deduct 2–3 marks immediately for producing an invalid permutation. Always mention **OX or PMX**.
> 2. **Forgetting the penalty term** in the knapsack fitness function — a knapsack GA without constraint handling is incomplete.
> 3. **Confusing "maximization" and "minimization"** in the fitness function direction. KTU expects the fitness to be in maximization form (e.g., $1/(1+g(x))$ for minimization).
> 4. **Not stating the encoding explicitly** — a 7-mark question on GA applications without the word "encoding" loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Failing to explain the choice of operator** — the examiner rewards the *justification* of why PMX is used for TSP but one-point crossover is fine for knapsack.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — GA Applications: Case Studies**
>
> ☐ **Encoding is problem-specific:** Binary for knapsack, permutation for TSP/JSSP, real-valued for continuous optimization, concatenated vector for neural networks.
>
> ☐ **Fitness must be maximization-oriented:** Convert minimization to maximization using $f = 1/(1+g(x))$ or penalty methods.
>
> ☐ **TSP requires OX or PMX crossover:** Standard crossover produces invalid permutations.
>
> ☐ **Knapsack requires penalty term:** Without it, the GA may converge to infeasible solutions.
>
> ☐ **Continuous optimization uses real-coded GA:** Avoids Hamming cliff and precision loss.
>
> ☐ **JSSP uses operation-based or permutation-with-repetition encoding:** Preserves job precedence constraints.
>
> ☐ **Neural network training:** Chromosome = concatenated weights and biases; fitness = $1/(1+\text{MSE})$.
>
> ☐ **Fuzzy controller tuning:** Chromosome = MF parameters; fitness = inverse of IAE or other performance index.
>
> ☐ **Convergence criteria:** Fixed generations, fitness threshold, or stagnation detection.
>
> ☐ **Common parameters:** $N = 50\text{–}200$, $P_c = 0.6\text{–}0.9$, $P_m = 0.001\text{–}0.1$.
>
> ☐ **Penalty coefficient $\lambda$:** Controls the trade-off between objective optimization and constraint satisfaction.
>
> ☐ **Tournament selection size $k$:** Typically 2–5; higher $k$ = more selection pressure.
>
> ☐ **Premature convergence:** Caused by loss of diversity; counter with elitism, adaptive mutation, or fitness sharing.
>
> ☐ **GA is derivative-free:** Works on non-differentiable, discontinuous, or black-box functions.
>
> ☐ **NP-hard problems:** GA provides near-optimal solutions where exact methods are computationally infeasible.
